31.31: A direct revelation: 'Behold the days are coming when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' The new covenant is the Christological center of the OT's prophetic program: Jesus at the Last Supper explicitly claims to enact this covenant (Luke 22:20: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood'), and Hebrews quotes all of Jer 31:31-34 (8:8-12) as the scriptural proof that the old covenant's priesthood and sacrificial system were provisional and superseded. The three elements of the new covenant are fulfilled in Christ: (1) law on hearts → the Spirit writes Christ's character in the believer; (2) universal knowledge of YHWH → all who come to Christ know the Father (John 17:3); (3) permanent forgiveness → the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ (Heb 9:26-28; 10:14).
31.1: The opening oracle — "at that time" I will be the God of all the clans of Israel and they will be my people — uses the eschatological time-marker baʿet hahiʿ to signal that what follows transcends the immediate historical moment. The covenant formula "I will be their God and they will be my people" runs from the Sinai narrative (Exod 6:7) through Jeremiah to its NT fulfillment: Revelation 21:3 declares, "Behold, the dwelling of God is with man. He will dwell with them and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God." Christ's incarnation is the hinge on which the covenant formula passes from promise to fulfillment.
31.2: The recollection of finding grace in the wilderness — Israel going to its resting place, YHWH appearing from afar — grounds the new-covenant hope in the original Exodus pattern. The wilderness-grace motif recurs in the NT: John the Baptist (Matt 3:1-3) appears in the wilderness as the voice preparing the way; Jesus himself is led into the wilderness (Matt 4:1) and emerges to begin his redemptive mission. Every new act of divine grace follows the Exodus pattern: wilderness first, then the rest of the promised land.
31.3: I have loved you with an everlasting love; therefore I have drawn you with lovingkindness — the declaration of ʾahavat ʿolam (everlasting love) and ḥesed (covenant loyalty). Paul's argument in Romans 8:38-39 is the NT counterpart: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus. The "everlasting love" that Jeremiah announces as the foundation of the new-covenant hope is precisely the love that Christ embodies and enacts in his incarnation and death (John 13:1: "having loved his own... he loved them to the end").
31.4: The promise to build you again, O virgin Israel, so that she takes up her tambourines and dances — the image of the restored community as rebuilt and rejoicing. Revelation 19:7-9 presents the eschatological counterpart: the wedding of the Lamb where the bride has made herself ready. The virgin Israel who dances with tambourines is a type of the church presented as a pure bride to Christ (2 Cor 11:2; Eph 5:27). The rebuilding of the community anticipates the building of the church Christ promises (Matt 16:18).
31.5: The planting of vineyards on the mountains of Samaria and eating their fruit — the restoration of the northern kingdom — points to the eschatological gathering that includes both Israel and Judah. Jesus's parable of the vineyard (Matt 20:1-16; 21:28-41) uses vine-planting as the image of God's kingdom project. The vine imagery culminates in John 15:1: "I am the true vine" — Christ himself is the vineyard that Israel's planting anticipated, and through him all nations share in the fruit.
31.6: The promise that watchmen in Ephraim will call out: Rise and let us go up to Zion to YHWH our God — the northern tribes returning to worship in Jerusalem — anticipates the gathering of all Israel under one shepherd. Jesus presents himself as the good shepherd who gathers the scattered sheep (John 10:16: "I have other sheep not of this fold; I must bring them also"). The call from Ephraim to go up to Zion is fulfilled when the Gentiles and dispersed Israel are gathered into the one flock under Christ, the one shepherd.
31.7: The command to shout for Jacob with joy, rejoice over the chief of the nations — the nations called to celebrate Israel's restoration — is an eschatological jubilee. The NT presents the ultimate jubilee as Christ's redemption: Luke 4:18-19, where Jesus reads from Isaiah 61 and announces the year of the Lord's favor, declares the jubilee Christ inaugurates. The nations shouting for joy over Jacob finds its fulfillment in the nations rejoicing in the gospel (Rom 15:10-12, citing Deut 32:43 and Ps 117:1).
31.8: YHWH gathering his people from the north country and from the ends of the earth — including the blind, the lame, the pregnant, and the nursing mother — is the great eschatological ingathering. Jesus's healing of the blind (Matt 9:27-31; Mark 10:46-52; John 9) and the lame (Mark 2:1-12; John 5:1-15) are the inaugurated signs of this gathering. Isaiah 35:5-6 (the blind see, the lame leap) and Luke 7:22 (Jesus's answer to John the Baptist) establish that the messianic era is marked by precisely these reversals. Christ's healing ministry is the advance sign of this worldwide gathering.
31.9: The promise that they shall come with weeping and with pleas for mercy; I will lead them by a straight way, for I am a father to Israel, and Ephraim is my firstborn — the divine fatherhood and the return of the lost son. Jesus's parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:11-32) draws directly on this imagery: the returning son comes in weeping penitence; the father runs to him and restores him. The explicit affirmation "I am a father to Israel" is the theological ground for Jesus's teaching about the Father's love for returning sinners. The "straight way" (derek yashar) is the path of restoration that Jesus himself claims to be (John 14:6).
31.10: The nations called to hear — He who scattered Israel will gather him and will keep him as a shepherd keeps his flock — is the divine shepherd promise. Ezekiel 34 develops this extensively; John 10 declares its fulfillment in Christ. The scatter-and-gather movement of divine judgment followed by divine compassion is resolved permanently in Christ's death and resurrection: the one who "was scattered" across the nations through judgment is gathered into the one body through the Spirit (1 Cor 12:13; Eph 2:14-16). The nations hearing of the shepherd's work anticipates the Gentile mission.
31.11: YHWH ransoming Jacob and redeeming him from hands too strong for him — the language of padah (ransom) and gaʾal (redeem) is the vocabulary of Exodus deliverance. Paul applies both to Christ's atoning work: "you were bought with a price" (1 Cor 6:20, using agorazō, the Greek equivalent of padah); "our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, who gave himself for us to redeem us" (Titus 2:13-14, using lytroō, equivalent of gaʾal). The ransom and redemption from forces too strong for humanity finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ's victory over sin and death.
31.12: The people coming to shout for joy on the heights of Zion, radiant over YHWH's goodness — grain, wine, oil, young animals — the eschatological feast of abundance. This is the great banquet image that Jesus uses for the kingdom (Matt 8:11: "many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven"; Luke 14:15-24). The grain, wine, and oil that mark covenant blessing become in the NT the eschatological feast where Christ himself is the host and bread (John 6:35; 1 Cor 11:26).
31.13: The promise that the virgin shall rejoice in the dance, and the young men and the old together; I will turn their mourning into joy, give them gladness instead of sorrow — the reversal of mourning is the eschatological sign. Jesus explicitly fulfills this: at the wedding of Cana (John 2:1-11) he transforms the water of purification into wine of celebration; at Nain (Luke 7:13-15) he stops a funeral procession and restores the son to his mother. The pattern culminates in Revelation 21:4: "he will wipe away every tear from their eyes." The eschatological joy-replacement is a work Christ performs in anticipation and will complete at the end.
31.14: The priests filled with abundance, and my people satisfied with my goodness — the satiation of both priestly service and common life. The NT presents Christ as the one who fills all things (Eph 1:23; 4:10). The priest-satisfaction that Jeremiah promises finds its fulfillment in Christ's high-priestly ministry (Heb 7:25-27): he makes continual intercession, is himself the unblemished offering, and guarantees a covenant that provides permanent access to YHWH's goodness. The priestly office and the people's satisfaction are both fulfilled in the one priest who is also the sacrifice.
31.15: A direct prophecy: "A voice was heard in Ramah, lamentation and bitter weeping. Rachel is weeping for her children; she refuses to be comforted for her children, because they are no more." Matthew 2:18 cites this verse explicitly as fulfilled in Herod's massacre of the male infants of Bethlehem (Matt 2:16-18). The typological logic is that the sorrow which accompanied Israel's exile (Ramah was the deportation staging point, 1 Sam 10:2) is recapitulated in the grief that surrounds Christ's birth. The infant Christ escapes; the children of Bethlehem do not — they share in the pattern of innocent suffering that characterizes the pre-resurrection age and points to the one innocent sufferer who bears it fully.
31.16: YHWH's word to Rachel — keep your voice from weeping and your eyes from tears, for your work will be rewarded; they will return from the land of the enemy — is the divine word of hope spoken into inconsolable grief. This is the pattern of the resurrection: against all visible evidence, the divine word promises return from the land of death. Jesus speaks similar words: "Do not weep" (Luke 7:13; 8:52); "your brother will rise again" (John 11:23). The divine refusal to let grief be the final word is the precursor to the Easter proclamation.
31.17: The promise — there is hope for your future; your children shall return to their own territory — is the covenant guarantee against despair. Hebrews 6:19 calls hope "an anchor for the soul, sure and steadfast, entering into the inner place behind the curtain" — Christ as the high priest who has entered the heavenly sanctuary guarantees the future hope. The children returning to their own territory finds its NT counterpart in the promise that the meek shall inherit the earth (Matt 5:5) and that Christ's people are heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ (Rom 8:17).
31.18: Ephraim's confession — I was like an untrained calf; turn me that I may be turned, for you are YHWH my God — is the prayer of the one who recognizes their incapacity for self-correction and appeals entirely to divine action. The image of the untrained calf is the image of human nature apart from grace. Paul describes the same condition in Romans 7:18: "I can will what is right but I cannot do it." The prayer "turn me that I may be turned" is the prayer for regeneration — the divine initiative that the NT identifies with the Spirit's work (Titus 3:5; John 3:5-8).
31.19: Ephraim's further confession — after I turned, I was sorry; after I was instructed, I struck my thigh; I was ashamed and also humiliated, because I bore the disgrace of my youth — is the pattern of genuine contrition. The shame and humiliation of Ephraim's confession is the mirror of the shame language of the NT: Paul says believers are not ashamed of the gospel (Rom 1:16) because the shame of sin has been transferred to Christ (Heb 12:2: "despising the shame"; 2 Cor 5:21). Genuine repentance involves this reckoning with the full weight of past dishonor, which Christ's cross definitively covers.
31.20: YHWH's response — Is Ephraim my dear son? Is he a darling child? For as often as I speak against him, I do remember him still. Therefore my heart yearns for him; I will surely have compassion on him — is one of the most tender divine self-disclosures in the OT. The divine heart that "yearns" (hamu meʿai, literally "my innards are stirred") is the deep compassion that Jesus embodies in the Gospels: "when he saw the crowds, he had compassion on them" (Matt 9:36; using splanchnizomai, the visceral-compassion word). The father's compassion in the prodigal son parable (Luke 15:20) directly echoes this verse.
31.21: The call to set up waymarks, make signposts; consider well the highway, the road by which you went — the returning exiles to mark the road home. The NT presents Christ himself as the road: "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). The "waymarks" and "signposts" of the prophetic tradition — all the types, shadows, and direct promises pointing toward Christ — are the signposts along the highway that leads to the Father. Hebrews 11 presents the whole of OT history as a path-marking toward the "better country" whose builder and maker is God.
31.22: The enigmatic sign — YHWH has created a new thing on the earth: a woman encompasses a man (neqevah tesovev gaver) — has generated many interpretations. The most defensible reading is that it announces a radical reversal of the social order: the exile feminized and weakened Israel ("woman"); the restoration will give Israel such security and vigor that the woman protects the man rather than fleeing — a reversal of the typical ancient world's vulnerability. At the deepest canonical level, the "new thing" (beritah ḥadashah) points to the new creation Christ inaugurates (2 Cor 5:17: "if anyone is in Christ, there is a new creation"). The unprecedented divine act that reverses normal categories is the pattern of the incarnation itself.
31.23: The restoration promise — they will use this speech in the land of Judah and in its cities when I restore their fortunes: "YHWH bless you, O habitation of righteousness, O holy mountain" — is the picture of the community greeting the land as righteous and holy after judgment and exile. The NT applies "righteousness" and "holiness" christologically: Christ is our righteousness (1 Cor 1:30; 2 Cor 5:21) and the one who sanctifies (Heb 2:11; 13:12). The restored community that speaks of righteousness and holiness as the character of its land finds its ultimate fulfillment in the new Jerusalem described in Revelation 21:27 — "nothing unclean will ever enter it."
31.24: The promise that farmers and those who wander with their flocks will live together in Judah — the reunification of agricultural and pastoral lifestyles in the land — represents the end of the exile's fragmentation. The NT vision of unity in Christ cuts across social and economic divisions: Galatians 3:28 ("neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male and female"); Colossians 3:11. The eschatological unity of the restored community is a pattern that anticipates the unity Christ creates in his body, where formerly divided communities share one life.
31.25: YHWH's declaration — I will satisfy the weary soul, and every languishing soul I will replenish — is a direct anticipation of Jesus's invitation: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matt 11:28). The weary and languishing soul that Jeremiah presents as the object of divine satiation is precisely the condition of those to whom Jesus directs his call. The satisfaction YHWH promises is the rest Christ provides — not merely comfort but genuine renewal of the exhausted soul by the one who is himself rest (Heb 4:9-10).
31.26: The mysterious parenthetical — at this I awoke and looked, and my sleep was pleasant to me — marks the dream-reception of this prophecy. The sleep and waking frame suggests the vision of restoration was given in a dream-like state, a mode of revelation prominent in the OT (Gen 28:10-17; Dan 7:1). The NT fulfillment of this prophetic vision is not a vision but a reality: what Jeremiah received as a dream-vision, the apostles witnessed in the flesh. John 1:14 ("we beheld his glory") is the waking counterpart to Jeremiah's pleasant dream.
31.27: The promise — I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with the seed of men and the seed of beasts — is the replanting of the people and the land after the devastation. The seed/sowing imagery runs through the NT as a Christological motif: the Parable of the Sower (Matt 13:3-23) describes the divine word as seed; John 12:24 uses the dying grain as the image of Christ's death and resurrection. The replanting of Israel that Jeremiah announces is the agricultural form of the resurrection hope — out of the dead soil of exile, new life springs through divine initiative.
31.28: The reversal promise — as I have watched over them to pluck up and to break down, to overthrow and to destroy and to bring harm, so I will watch over them to build and to plant — is the direct reversal of the prophet's own commission in 1:10 ("to pluck up and to pull down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant"). The same divine intentionality that brought judgment will now bring restoration. Paul applies this reversal to the cross: "he who was delivered up for our transgressions was raised for our justification" (Rom 4:25). The same God who brought the death of judgment is the same who brings the life of resurrection.
31.29: The abolition of the sour grapes proverb — "the fathers have eaten sour grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge" — announces a new era of direct individual accountability (paralleled in Ezek 18). The NT develops the tension between corporate solidarity and individual accountability through the lens of Christ: in Adam, all die (corporate solidarity with humanity's fall); in Christ, each one who believes is individually justified (Rom 5:12-21). The proverb's abolition points to the day when each individual stands directly before YHWH — which the NT identifies as the final judgment before Christ (2 Cor 5:10).
31.30: The positive statement — everyone who eats the sour grapes, his own teeth will be set on edge; everyone shall die for his own iniquity — establishes individual moral responsibility as the governing principle. This is the justice principle that the cross both upholds and transcends: Christ's atonement is not an evasion of individual accountability but its satisfaction — the penalty each person deserved was borne by the one who bore it representatively (2 Cor 5:21; 1 Pet 3:18). Individual responsibility is taken with full seriousness, and precisely because it is, the cross is costly.
31.32: The new covenant's second element — not like the covenant I made with their fathers when I took them out of Egypt, which they broke though I was a husband to them — establishes the contrast with the Sinai covenant. Hebrews 8:9 quotes this verse as the ground for arguing that the Levitical priesthood was provisional: the old covenant that was broken required a new covenant. The point is not that YHWH failed but that the external-law covenant was by design provisional — a tutor (Gal 3:24-25) that pointed forward to the internal-law covenant inaugurated by Christ's Spirit-giving ministry.
31.33: The new covenant's third element — I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts; I will be their God and they shall be my people — is the heart of the new covenant promise. Paul quotes this in 2 Corinthians 3:3: "you are a letter from Christ... written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts." The internalization of the law is the Spirit's work: Romans 8:4 says the righteous requirement of the law is fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit. The new covenant is inaugurated by Christ and implemented by the Spirit he gives.
31.34: The new covenant's fourth element — I will forgive their iniquity and I will remember their sin no more — is the ultimate basis for the entire covenant. Hebrews 10:17-18 quotes this verse as the proof that the new covenant has made the old sacrificial system obsolete: "where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer any offering for sin." The permanent, non-recalled forgiveness is what makes the new covenant genuinely new — not a temporary covering (as the Levitical sacrifices were) but a definitive removal. Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the event that produces this permanent forgiveness (Heb 9:26; 10:14).
31.35: The cosmic guarantees — if this fixed order of the sun, moon, and stars departs from before me, then the offspring of Israel shall cease to be a nation before me — grounds covenant faithfulness in the stability of creation. Paul applies this logic to the irrevocability of God's gifts in Romans 11:29: "the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable." The cosmic-stability argument establishes that the new covenant is as permanent as creation itself — and ultimately, Christ is the one through whom all things hold together (Col 1:17), so the guarantor of the covenant is the one who sustains the cosmos.
31.36: The extension of the cosmic guarantee — if the heavens above can be measured and the foundations of the earth below can be explored, I will cast off all the offspring of Israel for all they have done — is YHWH's emphatic no to Israel's permanent rejection. Paul's "Has God rejected his people? By no means!" (Rom 11:1) is the direct NT answer to the question this verse implicitly raises. The immeasurability of the heavens guarantees the indefeasibility of the covenant — which the NT locates in Christ as the one in whom all the promises are Yes (2 Cor 1:20).
31.37: The promise to rebuild Jerusalem from the Tower of Hananel to the Corner Gate — the specific geographical markers of the city's restoration — points to the eschatological city. Revelation 21:10-21 describes the New Jerusalem descending from heaven, with specific dimensions and gates — the spiritual fulfillment of Jeremiah's geographical promise. The rebuilt city of Jeremiah 31 is a historical type of the eternal city built by God, whose builder and maker is God (Heb 11:10).
31.38: The measuring line that goes straight to the hill Gareb and then shall turn to Goah — specific restoration geography for areas that were garbage dumps and waste places outside the walls — is the expansion of the holy city into previously defiled areas. The NT redemption of "unclean" areas is the theme of Acts 10-11 (the Gentile mission breaking purity barriers) and Revelation 21:27 (the New Jerusalem where nothing impure can enter precisely because impurity has been removed). The desolate places become holy places in the eschatological city.
31.39: The valley of dead bodies and ashes — the whole valley of the dead bodies and ashes, and all the fields as far as the brook Kidron — being made holy to YHWH represents the transformation of the place of death and defilement into sacred space. The Kidron Valley is where Jesus crossed on his way to Gethsemane (John 18:1) — the last boundary before his arrest and death. The valley associated with death and defilement becomes the path of the Son of God going to his atoning death, transforming the place of death into the ground of life.
31.40: The final promise — it shall not be plucked up or overthrown anymore forever — is the seal of permanence on the new Jerusalem. Revelation 21:4: "death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." The city that shall never again be plucked up or overthrown is the eternal city of God, whose permanence is secured not by political strength but by the permanent atonement of Christ and the permanent presence of God. The "forever" of the new Jerusalem finds its guarantee in the "forever" of Christ's priesthood (Heb 7:24) and resurrection (Rom 6:9).

7.1: The heading of the Temple Sermon establishes it as a direct divine communication: asher hayah el-Yirmiyahu me-et YHWH. This prophetic authority pattern is the same structure Jesus claims: 'You have heard it was said ... but I say to you' (Matt 5:21-48) — the direct divine voice that supersedes scribal transmission. Jesus's temple actions in the final week are the enacted fulfillment of this sermon.
7.2: Standing at the gate of the LORD's house to address those entering: Jesus's temple teaching also takes place in the temple courts (Matt 21-25; Mark 12; Luke 20-21). The audience is those entering to worship — those who assume that presence at the temple means covenant standing. Jesus also addresses the assumption that cultic proximity guarantees covenant security.
7.3: 'Reform your ways and your actions and I will let you live in this place' — the conditional covenant of habitation. Jesus fulfills the unconditioned side of this covenant through his perfect obedience: he is the one who dwells with the Father without condition (John 1:1-2, 18), and through him the conditional requirement is met for those who are united with him (2 Cor 5:21). His death at Passover in Jerusalem is the ultimate reformed act that satisfies what Jeremiah's audience refused.
7.4: 'Do not trust in these deceptive words: the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD.' The triple chant is religious formula emptied of reality — the building invoked but the covenant ignored. Jesus will identify himself as the true temple (John 2:21) and declare that this building will be destroyed (Matt 24:2). The deceptive confidence in the structure rather than in YHWH is exactly what Jesus dismantles.
7.5: The conditions: truly reform, administer justice, do not exploit the foreigner, fatherless, widow. The legal-social content of genuine worship. Jesus in Matt 23:23 condemns those who tithe but 'neglect the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, and faithfulness.' Both Jeremiah's Temple Sermon and Jesus's temple teaching identify the same failure: ritual compliance without covenantal justice.
7.6: Specific prohibitions: exploitation of the vulnerable, shedding innocent blood, following other gods. The shedding of innocent blood (dam naqi) points toward the ultimate innocent — Jesus, in whose death the long history of shedding innocent blood reaches its climax and its redemptive turning point. The disciples will later recall this context: 'I have sinned by betraying innocent blood' (Matt 27:4 — Judas using the exact phrase).
7.7: The land-promise: obedience leads to dwelling in the land given to the ancestors. Jesus reframes the land-promise eschatologically: 'the meek will inherit the earth' (Matt 5:5 = Ps 37:11); 'in my Father's house are many rooms ... I go to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The promised dwelling that Judah forfeits through disobedience is restored through Christ's obedience — but expanded: not a geographic territory but the new creation.
7.8: 'You are trusting in deceptive words that can do you no good.' The davar sheqer (deceptive/lying word) — the false security of temple-formula. Jesus warns against the same false security: 'not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven' (Matt 7:21); 'you are like whitewashed tombs' (Matt 23:27). Both Jeremiah and Jesus unmask the gap between religious performance and covenant reality.
7.9: The indictment catalog: steal, murder, commit adultery, swear falsely, sacrifice to Baal, follow other gods — and then come stand before YHWH in the temple. The Ten Commandments inverted: the worshippers break the covenant decalogue and then seek covenant blessing. Jesus's Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) internalizes the same commandments: 'you have heard it said you shall not murder, but I say to you everyone who is angry...' — reaching beneath the external act to the heart.
7.10: 'Come and stand before me in this house that bears my name and say: we are safe.' The performative 'we are safe' (nittzalnu) — ritual declaration of covenant standing. Jesus is the one in whose name all future prayer and covenant access is made (John 14:13-14; 16:23-24). His name replaces the temple as the locus of covenant presence.
7.11: 'Has this house, which bears my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes?' — Jesus quotes this verse exactly (Mark 11:17: spēlaion lēstōn = den of robbers) when he drives out the temple traders. This is the most explicit NT citation of the Temple Sermon. Jesus applies Jeremiah's judgment speech to his own contemporaries, claiming prophetic continuity: what Jeremiah charged Jerusalem with in 609 BCE, Jesus charges with again in 30 CE. The temple has become what Jeremiah warned it would become.
7.12: 'Go to Shiloh ... see what I did to it.' The Shiloh precedent: YHWH destroyed the first sanctuary when it was corrupted. Jesus's Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:1-2) gives the Jerusalem-temple equivalent: 'not one stone here will be left on another.' The destruction of 70 CE is the fulfillment of the Shiloh-pattern warning renewed by Jesus, who renewed Jeremiah's renewal of the same warning.
7.13: 'I spoke to you again and again, but you did not listen' — the persistent speaking (hashkem vedabber). Jesus identifies himself as the one in whom this persistent divine speech reaches its climax: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Heb 1:2). The Son is the final, definitive word after the long sequence of servants.
7.14: The destruction of the temple because of its misuse. Fulfilled in 586 BCE; renewed as warning by Jesus (Matt 24:2); fulfilled again in 70 CE. The theological logic: the temple exists to mediate YHWH's presence; when it becomes an obstacle to that presence (substituting ritual for relationship), it is destroyed. Jesus's body becomes the new temple (John 2:21) — the dwelling of divine presence that cannot be permanently destroyed (John 2:19: 'I will raise it in three days').
7.15: The expulsion from divine presence — just as YHWH drove out the offspring of Ephraim. The exile from the land is the covenant curse of the Most Terrible: exclusion from YHWH's presence (Lev 26:33; Deut 28:36-37). Jesus's atonement is the reversal of this expulsion: 'I go to prepare a place for you ... that where I am you may also be' (John 14:2-3). His crucifixion — the moment of maximum separation from the Father ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me') — is the bearing of this expulsion so that believers are never expelled.
7.16: 'Do not pray for this people.' The prophetic intercession ban — the point of no return where judgment becomes fixed. Jesus, by contrast, prays for his people even at the point of maximum sin: 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing' (Luke 23:34). He is the intercessor who continues to plead beyond the point where human intercession fails (Heb 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them').
7.17: YHWH directs Jeremiah to observe what the people are doing in the towns and streets. The omniscient divine attention to human behavior: 'Does he not see my ways and count my every step?' (Job 31:4). Jesus's omniscient observation is consistent: he 'knew all people and needed no one to testify about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:24-25).
7.18: The queen of heaven cult — the whole family participating in idolatry together: children gathering wood, fathers lighting the fire, women kneading dough. The comprehensive family participation in false worship is the opposite of the Shema's requirement to teach the commands to your children (Deut 6:6-9). Jesus comes to 'bring not peace but a sword' (Matt 10:34-36) — family division over allegiance to him — because idolatrous family systems must be disrupted for covenant faithfulness.
7.19: 'Is it really me they are provoking? ... Is it not themselves they are harming?' The logic of self-destructive sin: idolatry destroys the idolater, not YHWH. Jesus's teaching on sin follows the same logic: 'what comes out of a person is what defiles him' (Mark 7:20-23). Sin is self-contaminating, not God-insulting in any sense that threatens God; the harm is to the sinner.
7.20: The wrath poured out on the land — on human, animal, and tree alike. Creation suffers under human sin: Rom 8:19-22 (the creation groaning, subjected to futility because of human failure). Jesus's death and resurrection initiates the re-creation that undoes this comprehensive curse: 'the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay' (Rom 8:21).
7.21: YHWH's satirical invitation to add burnt offerings to sacrifices and eat the meat: when the covenant relationship is broken, sacrifices become meaningless dietary exercises. Jesus fulfills and supersedes the entire sacrificial system: 'I desire mercy and not sacrifice' (Matt 9:13; 12:7 quoting Hos 6:6), and his single offering perfects what the multiplied offerings could not (Heb 10:1-14).
7.22: 'On the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, I did not command them about burnt offerings and sacrifices.' The Exodus-covenant priority: relationship and obedience precede sacrifice. Hebrews 10:5-7 quotes Ps 40:6-8 to make the same point about Jesus: 'sacrifice and offering you did not desire ... then I said, here I am, I have come to do your will.' Jesus fulfills the original intention of the Sinai covenant by offering the obedience that the whole sacrificial system was pointing toward.
7.23: 'Obey me and I will be your God and you will be my people' — the covenant formula in its simplest form. Jesus is the obedient one who perfectly fulfills the 'obey me' side: 'not my will but yours be done' (Luke 22:42); 'I always do the things that please him' (John 8:29). His perfect obedience enacts the covenant formula on behalf of all who are in him; they become YHWH's people through his covenant-keeping.
7.24: The stubborn, evil hearts following their own plans — shreruyt libbam hara (the stubbornness of their evil hearts). Jesus diagnoses the same: 'from within, from the heart of man, come evil thoughts' (Mark 7:21). His new covenant promise (31:33-34) is the answer: 'I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts.' The new heart is the solution to the stubborn heart, accomplished through the Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2; Rom 8:3-4).
7.25: 'From the day your ancestors came out of Egypt until this very day, I have persistently sent all my servants the prophets.' The servant-prophet pattern. The Parable of the Tenants (Matt 21:33-46) uses this exact sequence: servants sent to the vineyard → rejected → finally the son → killed. Jesus identifies himself as both the final prophet and the Son who is the climax of the whole sequence. Stephen's speech in Acts 7:51-52 makes the same identification: 'which of the prophets did your ancestors not persecute?... and now they have betrayed and murdered the Righteous One.'
7.26: Each generation worse than the previous in not listening — the progressive hardening of Israel. Jesus observes the same: 'the sins of the fathers' accumulating, the 'blood of all the prophets shed since the foundation of the world' coming upon 'this generation' (Luke 11:50-51; Matt 23:35-36). The cross is where the accumulated rejection reaches its apex — and where it is absorbed rather than returned.
7.27: 'You will speak all these words but they will not listen; you will call but they will not respond.' The prophetic hardening oracle — given in advance so the prophet is not surprised by rejection. Jesus prepares his disciples for the same reception: 'if they persecuted me they will persecute you' (John 15:20); 'a servant is not greater than his master' (John 13:16). Rejection of the prophetic word is rejection of the sender.
7.28: 'This is the nation that did not obey the LORD their God and refused correction; faithfulness has perished, it has vanished from their lips.' The funeral announcement of faithfulness. Jesus comes as the faithful witness (ho martus ho pistos, Rev 1:5; 3:14) — the one in whom faithfulness has not perished. He is the faithful Israelite where Israel was unfaithful, making him the representative who can absorb the judgment and restore the relationship.
7.29: The shaving and lamenting on the hilltops — mourning rites for the LORD's rejection and abandonment of his generation. Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matt 23:37-39; Luke 19:41-44) takes up this same mourning: he weeps over the city whose generation has become the rejected generation of Jeremiah's vision. 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets ... your house is left to you desolate.'
7.30: Detestable idols in the house bearing YHWH's name — the temple defiled. Jesus enters the temple and drives out those who have made it a marketplace: 'my house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations' (Mark 11:17). He reclaims the holiness of YHWH's name-space. His own body is the holy temple (John 2:21) that replaces the defiled one.
7.31: Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom — child sacrifice to Molech, called 'the thing I did not command and that never entered my mind.' This valley (Gei Hinnom in Hebrew, Gehenna in Greek) becomes the NT's primary image of final judgment. Jesus uses Gehenna twelve times in the Gospels (Matt 5:22, 29-30; 10:28; 18:9; 23:15, 33; Mark 9:43-47; Luke 12:5) as the place of judgment — the place where fire consumed the defilements of the city. The valley's history of child sacrifice and later of burning the city's refuse gives Jesus's imagery its force.
7.32: The renaming: Topheth and the Valley of Ben Hinnom become the Valley of Slaughter because of the mass burial needed. The irony: the place where Israel sacrificed children to Molech becomes the mass grave of the Israeli dead. The NT's Gehenna incorporates this irony: the judgment image derives from the very place of Israel's worst sin. Jesus warns that the final judgment is more terrible than any earthly slaughter — the fire that never goes out (Mark 9:43-48).
7.33: Corpses as food for birds and beasts, with no one to frighten them away — the maximum desecration, the covenant curse of Deut 28:26. Jesus takes this curse in his own death: crucified outside the city, exposed (Col 2:15; Gal 3:13: 'cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree'). But his body is given an honorable burial (John 19:38-42) and raised — the reversal of the curse that those he represents deserved.
7.34: 'I will silence the voices of the bridegroom and bride.' The judgment-silencing of covenant joy. Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom (John 3:29; Matt 9:15; 25:1-13). His presence marks the return of wedding joy: 'while the bridegroom is with them, they cannot fast' (Mark 2:19). The silence of Jer 7:34 is what his arrival reverses; Rev 19:7 announces the final restoration of this joy: 'Let us rejoice and be glad and give him glory, for the wedding of the Lamb has come.'

8.1: The bones of the kings, priests, prophets, and inhabitants exposed — the judgment on the entire leadership and people of Judah. The exposure of bones (rather than burial) is the ultimate covenant-curse degradation. Jesus specifically protects even dead bodies: in John 19:31-37 the soldiers do not break his legs (fulfilling Ps 34:20: 'not one of his bones will be broken') — and he is buried with honor before the Sabbath. His resurrection is the first-fruits of the bodily restoration that reverses the bone-exposure judgment.
8.2: Bones spread before the sun, moon, and stars they worshiped — the divine irony: they loved and served the heavenly bodies, now their bones will be exposed to those same bodies as carrion. Jesus is the light that exceeds the sun, moon, and stars (Rev 21:23: 'the city does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp'). Worship directed to creation objects rather than the Creator is corrected in him.
8.3: 'Survivors of this evil family ... will prefer death to life.' The complete reversal of covenant blessing: instead of 'choosing life' (Deut 30:19), the judgment-generation prefers death. Jesus comes specifically to reverse this death-preference: 'I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full' (John 10:10). His resurrection is the proof that the preference for life is vindicated by God himself.
8.4: 'When people fall, don't they get back up? When someone turns away, don't they return?' The natural human reflex — fall, get up; stray, return — is inexplicably absent in Israel. Jesus is the one who enables the return: 'I am the way' (John 14:6); the good shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one who has strayed (Luke 15:4-7). He restores the capacity for return that Israel's stubbornness has disabled.
8.5: Perpetual backsliding — clinging to deception and refusing to return. Meshuvah netzachat (perpetual backsliding): the chronic, unreformable condition. Jesus addresses this with the new covenant promise: 'I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you; I will remove the heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh' (Ezek 36:26) — the cure for perpetual backsliding is heart-replacement, accomplished through the Spirit that Jesus gives (John 7:37-39; Acts 2:33).
8.6: 'No one repents of his wickedness and asks: What have I done?' The absent repentance. Jesus's ministry begins and centers on the call to repentance: metanoeite (repent!) is his opening word (Matt 4:17; Mark 1:15). His parables of the lost sheep, lost coin, and prodigal son (Luke 15) celebrate the repentance that Jeremiah found absent. He enables what Jeremiah could not find — genuine return to the Father.
8.7: 'The stork knows when to migrate... but my people do not know the ordinances of the LORD.' Animals follow their instincts given by the Creator; Israel has lost its covenant instinct. Jesus fulfills the covenant ordinances perfectly (Matt 5:17: 'I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it') and rebukes his contemporaries for not reading 'the signs of the times' as birds read seasonal signs (Matt 16:3; Luke 12:56): they have lost the ability to discern what is right before them.
8.8: 'We are wise and have the law of the LORD — but the lying pen of scribes has made it a lie.' Scribal distortion of the divine word. Jesus's repeated 'You have heard it said ... but I say to you' (Matt 5:21-48) addresses this directly: he cuts through the scribal accretion to YHWH's original intention. John 1:1, 14: the Logos who was with God from the beginning and became flesh is the authoritative interpreter who cannot be corrupted — the divine word unmediated through fallible scribes.
8.9: 'The wise will be disgraced... they rejected the word of the LORD — what wisdom do they have?' Paul's theology of the cross engages precisely this verse: 'where is the wise man? Where is the scholar? ... has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?' (1 Cor 1:20). The wisdom that rejects the divine word — whether Jeremiah's word or the cross — is self-refuting. Christ crucified is 'the power of God and the wisdom of God' (1 Cor 1:24) that exposes all other wisdom as insufficient.
8.10: Wives given to others, fields to new owners — the covenant curse of economic dispossession (Deut 28:30). The prophets from least to greatest practice deception; priests teach for profit. Jesus similarly indicts the religious leadership of his day: 'everything they do is done for people to see' (Matt 23:5); the scribes who 'devour widows' houses' (Mark 12:40). The leadership failure of Jeremiah's priests and prophets is the same failure Jesus confronts in the temple establishment.
8.11: 'They dress the wound of my people as though it were minor, saying: peace, peace, when there is no peace.' The false comfort of superficial healing — shalom shalom ve-ein shalom. Jesus refuses superficial comfort and names the real condition: 'I have not come to bring peace but a sword' (Matt 10:34) — not endorsing violence but refusing the false peace that avoids the real diagnosis. His peace is different: 'peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you' (John 14:27) — the authentic shalom that comes through dealing honestly with sin.
8.12: 'Were they ashamed? No, they felt no shame; they did not even know how to blush.' The loss of moral sensibility — lehikkalam lo yeda-u. Jesus identifies the same condition in Pharisees who are confident of their righteousness while actually being blind (John 9:39-41: 'for judgment I came into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind'). The shame-insensitive are not those who obviously sin but those who perform righteousness with pride; genuine shame before God is the beginning of repentance.
8.13: 'I will take away their harvest ... no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree.' The agricultural curse: the productive covenant land becomes barren. Jesus enacts this judgment symbolically in the cursing of the fig tree (Matt 21:18-22; Mark 11:12-14, 20-25) — the fig tree with leaves but no fruit represents Israel with religious form but no covenant reality. The withered tree is the enacted sign of what the temple represents.
8.14: The people's own despairing call to flee to fortified cities and die there — YHWH has given them poison to drink. The self-awareness of being under divine judgment: 'we have sinned against the LORD.' This admission of sin without return is the condition that Jesus comes to cure: he makes genuine repentance possible by taking the poison cup himself ('let this cup pass from me — yet not my will but yours', Matt 26:39) so that those who confess sin find mercy rather than death.
8.15: 'We hoped for peace, but no good has come; we waited for healing, but instead there is terror.' The exhausted, desolate waiting for shalom that never arrives. Jesus is the answer to this waiting: 'the kingdom of God has come near' (Mark 1:15) announces the arrival of the shalom that this verse says never came. His Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12) describe those who were waiting in exactly this posture: 'blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled.'
8.16: The northern invader — snorting warhorses heard from Dan, the whole land trembling. The terrifying invasion that the people feared and the prophets announced. Jesus's eschatological discourse (Matt 24; Luke 21) describes the coming of judgment in terms that echo these invasion oracles: 'when you see these things happening, you know that the kingdom of God is near.' The historical judgment on Jerusalem (70 CE) fulfills this pattern within the generation of Jesus's hearers (Matt 24:34).
8.17: 'I am sending poisonous snakes among you — vipers that cannot be charmed — and they will bite you.' The snake imagery of judgment. Jesus uses snake imagery for his opponents: 'you brood of vipers' (Matt 3:7; 12:34; 23:33); but he also takes up the snake-image for himself: 'as Moses lifted up the snake in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that everyone who believes may have eternal life in him' (John 3:14-15). He takes on the judgment-form to become the healing-form: the snake of judgment becomes the snake of salvation on the pole.
8.18: 'My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me.' Jeremiah's personal grief — the prophet sharing YHWH's own heartbreak. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) and expresses the divine sorrow: 'how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing' (Matt 23:37). The prophet's grief in ch. 8-9 is the prototype of the Son's grief over the same city — the same people, centuries later, in the same spiritual condition.
8.19: 'Is the LORD not in Zion? Is her King no longer there?' The desperate cry from exile wondering whether YHWH has abandoned his people. Jesus is the answer — the LORD who came to Zion in person (John 1:14: the Word eskēnōsen, tabernacled, among us; Matt 1:23: Emmanuel, God with us). The cry 'Is the LORD not in Zion?' is answered by the incarnation: yes, YHWH is in Zion — in the person of Jesus of Nazareth.
8.20: 'The harvest is over, the summer has ended — and we are still not saved.' The most despairing verse of the chapter: all the natural seasons of opportunity have passed; salvation has not come. Jesus announces the reversal of this despair: the kingdom of God is the harvest finally arriving (Matt 9:37-38: 'the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few'); he is the one who saves — Yeshua, whose name means YHWH saves. The summer is not over; the harvest has begun.
8.21: 'I am broken over the brokenness of my people; I mourn, and horror has gripped me.' The prophet's shared suffering with his people — he carries their pain. This is the prophet-as-intercessor pattern that anticipates Christ's high-priestly role: 'he is able to deal gently with those who are ignorant and are going astray, since he himself is subject to weakness' (Heb 5:2); 'we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses' (Heb 4:15). The broken prophet is the type of the broken priest-king who bears the people's brokenness himself.
8.22: 'Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there? Then why has the wound of my people not been healed?' — the most celebrated lament in Jeremiah, encapsulating the whole chapter's anguish. The tzori (balm) of Gilead (the aromatic resin-based healing substance from the Gilead region, cf. Gen 37:25; Jer 46:11) was the best medicinal treatment available. But no balm heals; no physician restores. Jesus comes as the divine physician: 'those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick do; I have not come to call the righteous but sinners' (Matt 9:12-13; Mark 2:17). He is the balm of Gilead; he is the physician. The unanswerable lament of 8:22 is answered by the incarnation.

9.1: 'Oh that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears, so that I could weep day and night for the slain of my people!' Jeremiah's tears are a prophetic type of Christ's tears. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41: eklausen ep' autēn, he wept over it) with the same combination of prophetic grief and divine anguish. John 11:35 ('Jesus wept') — the shortest verse in the NT — shows the same tear-fountain at the death of Lazarus. The weeping prophet is fulfilled in the weeping Son: YHWH's grief for his people becomes incarnate grief.
9.2: 'Oh that I had a lodging place in the wilderness, so that I could leave my people and go away — for they are all adulterers, a crowd of treacherous people!' The prophet's exhausted longing for escape from a faithless people. Jesus never expresses this longing — he goes through the temptation to withdraw (Gethsemane) but chooses to stay and drink the cup. Where Jeremiah longed to leave, Christ chose to remain: 'I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you' (John 14:18). His determination to remain with his people fulfills what the prophet yearned to abandon.
9.3: The tongue as a bow shooting falsehood (yidreku et-leshonam qeshtam sheqer). The community of deceit — powerful in the land but not by truth. James 3:2-12 elaborates the tongue-as-weapon image: the tongue is 'a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body.' Jesus addresses the tongue-and-truth issue directly: 'let your yes be yes and your no be no; anything more comes from the evil one' (Matt 5:37). He himself is the Truth (John 14:6) — the counterpart of the land of deceit.
9.4: 'Every brother is a schemer' (kol-ach aqov ya-aqov — playing on 'Jacob', the heel-grabber/schemer). The universal distrust within the covenant community. Jesus enters this community of treachery and is himself betrayed by a close companion (John 13:18; Ps 41:9 — 'even my close friend who shared my bread has lifted his heel against me'). He knows the Jacob-pattern from inside human experience — and his self-giving into betrayal reverses the pattern: the one who could have deceived chose radical transparency.
9.5: Universal deception, tongues trained to lie, exhausted by iniquity. Jesus's testimony: 'I have spoken openly to the world; I always taught in synagogues or at the temple... I said nothing in secret' (John 18:20). He is the anti-type of the deceptive tongue: total transparency, full disclosure, the word that cannot be falsified. In him the community of trained liars encounters the one who cannot lie (Titus 1:2: 'God, who does not lie').
9.6: 'You live in the midst of deception; through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD.' The knowledge-of-God failure at the root of all the deception. Jesus is the definitive revelation of the Father: 'Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9); 'No one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him' (Matt 11:27). Refusing to know Jesus is refusing to know the LORD — the same refusal Jeremiah identifies as the community's core failure.
9.7: 'I will refine them and test them — for what else can I do with my people?' The divine refining through judgment — the only remaining option when all other means of restoration have failed. Jesus himself is the refiner: 'he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Matt 3:11-12); 'his winnowing fork is in his hand' (Matt 3:12). The refinement through the cross — bearing the judgment fire himself — is the ultimate form of YHWH's refining of his people.
9.8: The tongue as lethal arrow, speaking peace with the mouth while ambushing in the heart. Judas's betrayal kiss (Matt 26:49: 'Greetings, Rabbi!' — peace-speech with a treacherous purpose) is the exact fulfillment of this pattern. The one who kisses Jesus to betray him enacts the 'peace in the mouth, ambush in the heart' dynamic of 9:8. Jesus receives the ambush in the mouth and bears it without retaliating in kind.
9.9: 'Should I not punish them? Should I not take vengeance on such a nation?' The divine justice that demands accountability. Jesus takes the full weight of this divine vengeance in his own person on the cross: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13). The vengeance YHWH takes on the faithless nation is absorbed by the faithful Son — so that the nation does not bear it, but lives through his bearing.
9.10: Weeping and wailing for the mountains, lament for the wilderness pastures: the land is desolate because everyone has left. Jesus's lament over the cities of Galilee (Matt 11:20-24: 'woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida!') uses the same lament-over-land pattern: the city that rejected his ministry will be more desolate than Sodom and Gomorrah. The land suffers when YHWH's word is rejected.
9.11: Jerusalem a heap of rubble, a haunt of jackals; cities of Judah desolate. Fulfilled in 70 CE — the destruction of Jerusalem that Jesus announced (Matt 24:2; Luke 21:20-24: 'let those in Judea flee to the mountains...'). The jackals-in-Jerusalem image that Jeremiah saw in vision, Jesus sees approaching and announces as imminent. The gap between Jeremiah's oracle and the destruction of Jerusalem mirrors the gap between Jesus's oracle and 70 CE.
9.12: 'Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it?' The call for an interpreter of disaster. Jesus is the ultimate interpreter of the divine purpose in judgment: he explains to the disciples on the Emmaus road 'everything in the Scriptures concerning himself' (Luke 24:27), including why it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer. He has received the word from the LORD — 'my teaching is not my own; it comes from the one who sent me' (John 7:16).
9.13: The LORD's explanation: they abandoned my law (torati) that I placed before them, did not obey, and did not walk in it. Jesus frames his own teaching as the internalized Torah: 'I will put my laws in their minds and write them on their hearts' (Heb 8:10 quoting Jer 31:33). The external Torah abandoned by Jeremiah's generation becomes the internal Torah inscribed by the Spirit — the new covenant solution to the old covenant failure.
9.14: They followed the stubbornness of their hearts and went after the Baals as their ancestors taught them. The intergenerational transmission of idolatry — taught apostasy rather than taught Torah (cf. Deut 6:7). Jesus teaches his disciples to make new disciples and teach them (Matt 28:19-20) — the intergenerational transmission of covenant faithfulness that reverses the intergenerational transmission of Baal worship.
9.15: Wormwood and poisoned water fed to the people because of apostasy. The bitter waters of judgment. Revelation 8:10-11 uses the wormwood image for the end-time judgment: 'a great star ... fell on a third of the rivers ... the name of the star is Wormwood.' Jesus, at the cross, is offered wine mixed with gall (Matt 27:34) — the bitter draught, which he initially refuses — then drinks the full cup of suffering (v. 42: 'let him come down from the cross'). He drinks the wormwood so the covenant people do not have to drink it permanently.
9.16: Scattered among nations, pursued by the sword until consumed. The comprehensive exile. Jesus's own flight to Egypt as an infant (Matt 2:13-15) makes him a participant in the diaspora pattern — 'out of Egypt I called my son' (Hos 11:1, quoted in Matt 2:15). He is the true Israel in exile who returns; his resurrection is the return from the ultimate 'exile' of death.
9.17: Summon the mourning women — the professional lament-singers. The organized mourning liturgy of the ancient Near East: trained women who led communal grief. Jesus encounters such mourning in the NT: at Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:38-39: 'people crying and wailing loudly') and at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:31-33). In both cases he transforms mourning into resurrection hope: 'she is not dead but sleeping' (Mark 5:39); 'your brother will rise again' (John 11:23). The mourning-women tradition is not abolished but superseded.
9.18: Tears streaming from eyes, eyelids overflowing with water — the lament that YHWH calls for. Jesus in Gethsemane: 'being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly, and his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground' (Luke 22:44) — the physical intensity of his grief prefigures the weeping and tears that Heb 5:7 attributes to him: 'during the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears.' The weeping of 9:18 has its NT fulfillment in the Son's tears.
9.19: 'We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land and our houses have been torn down.' The exile's lament — loss of land and dwelling as the expression of covenant loss. Jesus promises a dwelling that no exile can take away: 'in my Father's house are many rooms ... I am going to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The permanence of the dwelling he prepares reverses the impermanence of the houses torn down in exile.
9.20: Women commanded to hear the word of the LORD — teach your daughters to mourn; teach one another the lament. The female transmission of grief. The women at the cross (Luke 23:27-31) enact this: Jesus addresses them directly: 'daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me; weep for yourselves and for your children.' He directs their grief toward the coming destruction of 70 CE — the lament-teaching that Jeremiah prescribes is here applied by Jesus to his own generation.
9.21: Death climbing through windows, entering palaces, cutting down children in the streets. The invasion of death into every space — no fortress sufficient. Jesus faces death in its full power and rises: 'Death has been swallowed up in victory. Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' (1 Cor 15:54-55 citing Isa 25:8 and Hos 13:14). Death's climb through the windows of v. 21 is reversed by Christ's victory over death in the resurrection.
9.22: 'Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field, like grain stalks left behind the reaper, with no one to gather them.' The exposed, ungathered dead of judgment. Jesus comes as the gatherer: 'the Son of Man will send out his angels and they will weed out of his kingdom everything that causes sin ... and gather his elect from the four winds' (Matt 13:41; 24:31). The no-one-to-gather of 9:22 becomes the final gathering of the Son's harvest.
9.23: 'Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their riches.' The threefold prohibition of self-boasting. Paul cites this as the foundation of his entire theology of the cross: 'it is because of him that you are in Christ Jesus, who has become for us wisdom from God — that is, our righteousness, holiness and redemption. Therefore, as it is written: 'Let him who boasts boast in the Lord'' (1 Cor 1:30-31). The wisdom, strength, and riches that cannot be boasted are redefined and concentrated in Christ crucified.
9.24: 'But let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me — that I am the LORD who exercises steadfast love, justice, and righteousness on earth, for in these I delight.' To know YHWH as the one who does chesed, mishpat, and tzedaqah — and to boast only in that knowing. Jesus is the knowledge of God made accessible: 'anyone who has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9); 'now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent' (John 17:3). Boasting in knowing YHWH is now, in the NT, boasting in knowing Christ.
9.25: 'The days are coming when I will punish all who are only outwardly circumcised.' The critique of outward-only circumcision. Paul elaborates this in Rom 2:28-29: 'a person is not a Jew who is one outwardly, nor is circumcision merely outward and physical; no, a person is a Jew who is one inwardly, and circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit.' Col 2:11-12 applies this to Christ: 'in him you were also circumcised with a circumcision not performed by human hands.' Christ provides the true circumcision of the heart that Jeremiah and Deuteronomy (10:16; 30:6) required.
9.26: Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and the clipped-haired Arabs — all circumcised in flesh but uncircumcised in heart. The comprehensive list of the outwardly-circumcised but inwardly-uncircumcised dismantles any ethnic pride in covenant signs. Paul in Galatians and Romans makes the same argument globally: circumcision neither confers righteousness nor does its absence disqualify from covenant standing (Gal 5:6: 'neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value; the only thing that counts is faith expressing itself through love'). The true Israel is defined by the heart-circumcision that Christ provides.

10.1: The opening command to 'hear what YHWH speaks to you, O house of Israel' prefigures the Christological claim that YHWH's ultimate speech has come in his Son: 'In many and various ways God spoke of old to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Heb 1:1-2). The word to Israel in Jeremiah's time is preliminary; the full Word is the incarnate Logos (John 1:1,14).
10.2: 'Do not learn the way of the nations, and do not be dismayed at the signs of the heavens.' The contrast between Israel's calling and the nations' astrology anticipates Paul's contrast between the wisdom of the world and the wisdom of God revealed in Christ crucified (1 Cor 1:20-25). The 'signs of the heavens' that terrify pagans are subject to the Creator whom Christ embodies (Col 1:16-17).
10.3: The idol-making process described here — cutting a tree, shaping it, adorning it with silver and gold — is the polemic background for Paul's indictment in Rom 1:21-23: 'They exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling mortal man and birds and animals.' The creation-to-idol trajectory that Jeremiah mocks is the spiritual condition from which Christ comes to rescue humanity.
10.5: 'They cannot speak... they cannot walk... do not be afraid of them, for they cannot do evil, neither is it in them to do good.' The absolute impotence of idols establishes by contrast the power of Christ — who speaks with authority (Matt 7:29), heals, casts out evil, and does good everywhere he went (Acts 10:38). The idol is nothing; Christ is the power of God and the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24).
10.6: 'There is none like you, O YHWH; you are great, and your name is great in might.' The incomparability of YHWH confessed here is re-centered in Christ in the NT — 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Col 2:9). The divine name's greatness is revealed in the name of Jesus, at which every knee bows (Phil 2:9-11).
10.7: 'Who would not fear you, O King of the nations? For this is your due.' The kingship of YHWH over all nations is the premise for the Great Commission: Christ as risen Lord sends his disciples to all nations precisely because 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matt 28:18-20) — the universally-due fear of the divine King is now channeled through the Son's reign.
10.10: The living God (Elohim chayyim) who makes the earth quake at his wrath — this title is specifically applied to Jesus in his encounter with Peter: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God' (Matt 16:16). The confession identifies Jesus as the incarnate presence of the very God whom the nations have no right to worship but must. 'The true God and eternal life' of 1 John 5:20 identifies the Son with this title.
10.11: This verse, uniquely in Aramaic within Jeremiah's Hebrew text, declares the end of gods who did not make heaven and earth. The NT judgment on 'so-called gods' (1 Cor 8:5-6) reaches its definitive expression in Christ: 'yet for us there is one God, the Father... and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.' What Jeremiah announced in Aramaic to the nations, Christ's lordship enacts eschatologically.
10.12: 'It is he who made the earth by his power, who established the world by his wisdom, and by his understanding stretched out the heavens.' The Creator-God of Jeremiah's doxology is identified in the NT as the one through whom the Father created — 'through him all things were made' (John 1:3); 'in him all things were created, in heaven and on earth' (Col 1:16). The Logos who was with God in creation became flesh in Jesus.
10.13: The thunder, rain, lightning, and wind deployed by YHWH the creator prefigure Christ's authority over weather — stilling the storm (Mark 4:39-41), prompting the disciples' question: 'Who then is this, that even wind and sea obey him?' The answer Jeremiah already knows: this is the incomparable God, maker of all (v6-7). Jesus's nature miracles are epiphanies of his creative identity.
10.14: 'Every man is stupid and without knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his idols.' The comprehensive stupidity of human wisdom without God is the condition Christ addresses in the incarnation. 'The world did not know God through wisdom' (1 Cor 1:21) — the failure of human knowledge (da'at) calls for the Teacher who is himself the truth (John 14:6).
10.16: 'Not like these is he who is the portion of Jacob, for he is the one who formed all things, and Israel is the tribe of his inheritance; YHWH of Hosts is his name.' The God who is both creator of all and personal chelek (portion/inheritance) of his people is the pattern for Christ who is 'all, and in all' (Col 3:11) and the inheritance of the saints (Eph 1:11). The name YHWH Tzvaot is the name behind the name of Jesus.
10.21: 'The shepherds are stupid and do not inquire of YHWH; therefore they have not prospered, and all their flock is scattered.' The failure of Israel's shepherds sets up the promised contrast: YHWH himself will shepherd (Ezek 34:11-16), fulfilled in Christ who declares 'I am the good shepherd' (John 10:11). Every scattered flock in the OT is a preparation for Christ's commission to gather 'the lost sheep of the house of Israel' (Matt 15:24) and then 'other sheep' beyond the fold (John 10:16).
10.23: 'I know, O YHWH, that the way of man is not in himself, that it is not in man who walks to direct his steps.' This confession of human incapacity to self-direct anticipates Christ's claim 'I am the way' (John 14:6). The person who cannot find their own way needs the Way himself — not a teaching about direction but the personal guide who leads to the Father. Jeremiah's prayer for YHWH to correct (yasser) him gently becomes the ministry of the Spirit who leads into all truth (John 16:13).
10.24: 'Correct me, O YHWH, but in justice; not in your anger, lest you bring me to nothing.' The prayer for correction in mishpat (justice) rather than af (wrath) anticipates the NT distinction: 'There is no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus' (Rom 8:1). Christ absorbs the wrath so that the correction remaining for believers is the loving discipline of the Father, not forensic punishment (Heb 12:5-11).
10.25: 'Pour out your wrath on the nations that know you not... for they have devoured Jacob.' The prayer for divine wrath on the nations reaches its eschatological fulfillment through Christ — not the wrath of military judgment (the Babylonian pattern) but the wrath of the final day. Simultaneously, Christ's mission is that those 'nations that do not call on your name' should come to know YHWH (Matt 28:19), transforming Jeremiah's enemies-of-Jacob into worshipers of God.
10.4: A shadow: the idol adorned with silver and gold, fastened with nails so it cannot topple. The idol is static, manufactured, and powerless — a thing made by human hands rather than a person who acts. Christ is the antithesis: he is “the image of the invisible God” (Col 1:15), not crafted but begotten, not nailed into place but nailed to the cross by his own willing submission. The nails that secure the idol in verse 4 are echoed by the nails of the Passion — but their purpose is inverted. The idol is nailed down so it cannot move; Christ is nailed up so that he can draw all people to himself (John 12:32).
10.8: A theme: the worthlessness of idol instruction — “the instruction that comes from idols is nothing but worthless wood.” The idols teach nothing; they have no word of life to give. Christ is the Word who gives life: “The words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life” (John 6:63). Where idol instruction is wood — inert, lifeless, combustible — Christ’s word is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword (Heb 4:12). Jeremiah’s polemic against idol-instruction prepares for the NT’s proclamation of the Logos who speaks with divine authority.
10.9: A shadow: the craftsman-made gods — beaten silver, worked gold, clothed in violet and purple. These gods are the product of the craftsman (maʿăśēh ḥārāš); their existence depends entirely on human artisanship. Christ is the maker, not the made: “all things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made” (John 1:3). The contrast in Jer 10:9 between the elaborately dressed idol and the living God of v.10 finds its ultimate form in the incarnation paradox: the one through whom all things were made clothed himself in human flesh — not because he needed to be dressed by human hands, but to dwell among us and redeem the human artisan who had turned to idol-making.
10.15: A direct anticipation: “They are worthless, a product of delusion; when they are called to account, they will be destroyed.” The Hebrew bᵉʿēṯ pᵉquddāṯām yōḇēdû — “at the time of their visitation/reckoning they will perish” — uses the same word pᵉquddāh (visitation) that the NT uses for the coming of the Messiah and the judgment he brings. Christ’s first coming is the “visitation” of God to his people (Luke 1:68; Luke 19:44); his second coming is the final reckoning at which all that is false and worthless — every idol of the heart — will be destroyed. The idols that fail their worshipers at the time of visitation are the shadows of every substitute for Christ that will not survive the final judgment.
10.17: A theme: the summons to gather belongings in preparation for exile — “gather your belongings from the ground, O inhabitant of the besieged city.” Christ echoes this summons in the Olivet Discourse, warning of the coming destruction of Jerusalem: “let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains; let those who are inside the city get out” (Luke 21:21). Jesus reads the coming Roman siege through the lens of Jeremiah’s Babylonian siege, applying the same pattern of divine judgment to the generation that rejected the Messiah. The exile-warning of 587 BCE and the Jerusalem-warning of 70 CE are both expressions of the same covenantal dynamic Jeremiah announces here.
10.18: A theme: the divine act of hurling inhabitants from the land — “I am going to hurl the inhabitants of the land out at this very time.” The verb qālaʿ (to hurl, sling) is the same root used for David’s sling in 1 Sam 17:49-50. The divine slinging of Judah into exile is an act of covenant judgment. Christ absorbs the covenant wrath this judgment represents: he becomes “a curse for us” (Gal 3:13), taking the exile’s penalty so that those in him are not hurled away but drawn near. Those in Christ are not subject to being “slung out” — they are held by the one who himself bore the sling.
10.19: A theme: the lament “Woe to me for my wound! My injury is severe.” This is Jeremiah speaking as the embodiment of the wounded city and people. The confessional poems of Jeremiah (the “laments” or “confessions”) are regularly read as anticipating Christ’s Passion in the NT — the righteous sufferer who bears the wound of corporate sin. The phrase “I must bear it” (wᵉʾanî ʾāmartî ʾaḵ zeh ḥŏlî wᵉʾeśśāʾennû) echoes Isa 53:4: “he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows.” Christ is the one who says the same — taking the wound that belongs to others and bearing it as his own burden.
10.20: A theme: the tent ruined, ropes snapped, children gone. The image of the destroyed tent anticipates the destroyed temple — and beyond that, the body of Christ, the true temple: “destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up” (John 2:19). The scattered children whom the tent-dweller mourns become the scattered flock Christ comes to gather: “how often I would have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!” (Matt 23:37). The ruin of the tent is real, but Christ’s resurrection is the re-pitching of the eternal dwelling among his people (Rev 21:3).
10.22: A direct anticipation: “Listen! A report is coming — a great commotion from the north — to turn the cities of Judah into a desolate waste, a lair of jackals.” Christ echoes this desolation in his apocalyptic discourse, citing the Danielic “abomination of desolation” (Dan 9:27; Matt 24:15) that will render Jerusalem desolate. Jesus weeps over Jerusalem specifically because it did not recognize the time of its visitation (Luke 19:41-44) — the commotion that Jeremiah hears coming from the north (Babylon) is the distant rumble of the same pattern that will come again in 70 CE. Both desolations find their meaning in the refusal to receive the one whom YHWH sent.

11.1: The word 'that came to Jeremiah from YHWH' frames the chapter as legal covenant-prosecution. The Christological fulfillment runs through Heb 8-10: Christ is the mediator of the new covenant that supersedes the broken Sinai covenant. The repeated accusation 'they did not listen' (vv4,7,8) that triggers covenant judgment is the same hardness Jesus laments: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children... but you were not willing' (Matt 23:37).
11.4: 'Obey my voice, and do all that I command you. So shall you be my people, and I will be your God.' The covenant formula (I will be your God; you shall be my people) that the Sinai covenant could not permanently secure is the content Christ comes to establish permanently. Rev 21:3: 'Behold, the dwelling of God is with man... he will be their God and they will be his people.' The formula Jeremiah quotes from Sinai is fulfilled in the new creation.
11.5: 'That I may confirm the oath that I swore to your fathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey.' The land promise swore to Abraham finds its NT reinterpretation in Heb 11:16 and Rev 21: the patriarchs were looking for 'a better country, that is, a heavenly one.' Christ fulfills the land promise by giving not Canaan but the new creation — the 'heavenly homeland' that the material land always symbolized.
11.8: 'They did not obey or incline their ear, but every one walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart.' The anthropological diagnosis — the stubborn, autonomous heart — is precisely what the new covenant addresses (31:33: 'I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts'). Christ came not just to announce but to enact the heart-transformation that Sinai could not produce, through the Spirit he pours out (Titus 3:5-6).
11.11: 'I will bring disaster upon them that they cannot escape. Though they cry to me, I will not listen to them.' This divine refusal to hear echoes in Jesus's warning about the prayer of the unrepentant (Matt 7:21-23: 'I never knew you') and the closed door after the wedding (Matt 25:10-12). Covenant abandonment has a real telos — the moment when mercy's time has run out — which Christ announced was coming on that generation (Matt 23:38).
11.14: 'Therefore do not pray for this people, or lift up a cry or prayer for them, for I will not listen when they call to me in their trouble.' The prohibition of intercession marks the depth of the breach — but Christ's intercession transcends this. Where Jeremiah was forbidden to intercede for stubborn Israel, Christ 'always lives to make intercession' for those who come to God through him (Heb 7:25). The new covenant opens an intercession that the old covenant's failure could not sustain.
11.15: 'What right has my beloved in my house?' The beloved (yedidati) — Israel — has become unfaithful in YHWH's house (temple). Christ comes as the Son who has every right in the Father's house (John 2:16: 'Do not make my Father's house a house of trade'); he is the true yedid (beloved) who cleanses the temple and his own body becomes the true temple (John 2:19-21).
11.19: 'I was like a docile lamb led to the slaughter' (keves aluf yuval litvoach): one of the most direct proto-passion statements in the OT. Isaiah 53:7 uses the same lamb-to-slaughter image for the Suffering Servant, and John 1:29 identifies Jesus explicitly as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.' Jeremiah's experience of innocent suffering at the hands of his own people — from Anathoth, his hometown — prefigures Christ's rejection in Nazareth and Jerusalem. 'I did not know it was against me they devised schemes': the ignorance of the plot parallels the innocence of the Lamb.
11.20: 'O YHWH of Hosts, who judges righteously, who tests the heart and the mind, let me see your vengeance upon them, for to you have I committed my cause.' Jeremiah entrusts his cause to YHWH the just judge — as Peter describes Christ's passion: 'He committed no sin... but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly' (1 Pet 2:22-23). Christ fulfills the pattern not just by suffering innocently but by entrusting himself to the Father through the passion (Luke 23:46: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit').
11.21: 'Do not prophesy in the name of YHWH, or you will die by our hand.' The threat of death for speaking YHWH's word — from Jeremiah's own hometown Anathoth — is a narrative rehearsal of the Nazareth rejection (Luke 4:24-29: 'No prophet is acceptable in his hometown') and ultimately of the Sanhedrin's determination to kill Jesus for his prophetic-messianic claims. The prophet's life is at stake for naming YHWH in the world.
