11.23: The destruction of the men of Anathoth in the year of their punishment (shnat pequddatam) — the appointed accountability day — anticipates the theme of the 'day of visitation' that Jesus places in Jerusalem's future (Luke 19:44: 'because you did not know the time of your visitation'). The paquddah that Anathoth missed is the same visitation-logic applied to the generation that rejected its Messiah.
11.2: A theme: “Hear the terms of this covenant.” The Sinai covenant is announced and its terms proclaimed. Christ comes as the covenant mediator who does not merely announce terms but fulfills them in his own person: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them” (Matt 5:17). The covenant of Sinai, whose terms Jeremiah is commissioned to proclaim, is the shadow of the new covenant that Christ inaugurates in his blood (Luke 22:20; Heb 9:15). The command to “hear” (šimʿû) the covenant terms is fulfilled in the Shema and finds its NT form in the command to hear the Son (Matt 17:5).
11.3: A theme: “Cursed is anyone who does not obey the terms of this covenant.” The covenant curse is the penalty for disobedience. Christ becomes the bearer of this curse: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree’” (Gal 3:13). The curse of Jeremiah 11:3 is not a threat hanging over the obedient but the covenant penalty that the disobedient people have accumulated — and which Christ absorbs at the cross. The “cursed is anyone who” formula from Deut 27 runs through Jeremiah as a live threat and through Galatians as an absorbed judgment.
11.6: A theme: “Proclaim all these words in the towns of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem: Hear and obey.” The prophetic commission to preach the covenant words throughout the land anticipates the apostolic commission: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations... teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you” (Matt 28:19-20). Jeremiah preaches the old covenant words in the towns; the apostles preach the new covenant word — Christ himself — to the nations. Both are “herald” missions, and both involve the suffering of rejection by those who refuse to hear.
11.7: A revelation of God: “For I repeatedly warned your ancestors when I brought them out of Egypt, warning them urgently right up to this day.” The divine persistence in warning — hāšēm wᵉhāʿēḏ bᵉʾaḇôṯêhem, “I persistently warned your ancestors” — is the OT form of the Father’s patient sending: “He sent them another servant, and that one they beat; he sent still another, and that one they killed” (Mark 12:5). Christ himself is the last and greatest envoy in this long sequence of warnings — the Son sent after the servants, whose rejection is qualitatively different because of who he is (Heb 1:1-2).
11.9: A direct anticipation: “There is a conspiracy among the people of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.” The conspiracy against Jeremiah in this chapter (v.19-21) prefigures the conspiracy against Jesus. The word qešer (conspiracy/plot) is the same word used for the assembly of those who plotted to eliminate Jeremiah (vv.9, 19). In the NT, the conspiracy against Jesus is described in identical terms: “the chief priests and the elders of the people plotted together to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him” (Matt 26:3-4). Jeremiah’s experience of conspiracy is a type of Christ’s Passion — the prophet whose words provoke a plot against his life.
11.10: A theme: “They have returned to the sins of their ancestors who refused to listen to my words.” The pattern of apostasy — receiving the covenant, then returning to the same rebellion — is the diagnostic of human incapacity for covenant keeping. This is why the new covenant in Jer 31:31-34 must write the law on the heart rather than on stone. Christ is the mediator of this new covenant: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts” (Jer 31:33) — made possible through the Holy Spirit who is poured out from Christ’s glorified body (John 7:39; Acts 2:33).
11.12: A theme: the futility of crying to the gods they have served — “they will cry out to them, but they will certainly not save them.” The idols that cannot save contrast with Christ who is able to save to the uttermost: “he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to make intercession for them” (Heb 7:25). The idol that cannot save when cried out to is the negative testimony to the necessity of the one Mediator. Jeremiah’s repeated insistence on idol-impotence (chs. 2, 10, 11) is the prophetic preparation for the NT’s proclamation of the one name given among men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).
11.13: A shadow: “as many gods as you have cities, O Judah; as many altars to Baal as streets in Jerusalem.” The multiplication of worship objects — localized, plural, competing — is the inverse of the one God in one temple in one city. Christ consolidates and fulfills this singularity: there is one God and one mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5). The replacement of Jerusalem’s many Baal altars with the one altar of the cross shows how the divine judgment of Jeremiah 11 is not merely destructive but preparatory — clearing the ground for the one offering that actually avails.
11.16: A shadow: “the LORD once called you a flourishing olive tree, beautiful with fine fruit; but with the roar of a great storm he will set it on fire, and its branches will be broken.” Paul uses precisely this olive-tree image in Romans 11 — Israel as the cultivated olive tree, with some branches broken off, and Gentiles as a wild branch grafted in. The burning branches of Jeremiah 11 become the broken-off branches of Rom 11:17. Christ is the root that supports the tree: “do not boast over those branches... it is not you who support the root, but the root that supports you” (Rom 11:18). The olive-tree judgment of Jeremiah opens into the olive-tree mercy of Romans.
11.17: A theme: “The LORD of hosts, who planted you, has pronounced disaster on you because of the evil that the house of Israel and the house of Judah have done.” The parable logic — the planter who planted his vine/tree and finds it faithless — runs from Ps 80 through Isa 5 to Jer 11-12 to Jesus’ parable of the vineyard (Matt 21:33-41). In each case, the owner of the vineyard/tree expects fruit, finds faithlessness, and pronounces judgment. Christ embodies both the Owner who pronounces judgment and the new Planting — the true vine (John 15:1) who produces the fruit that Israel failed to yield.
11.18: A revelation of God: “The LORD revealed it to me, and I knew; you opened my eyes to their actions.” The prophetic knowledge of hidden conspiracy comes by divine revelation, not human intelligence. This is a pattern that reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who “knew all people” and “did not need anyone to tell him what was in a person, for he himself knew what was in a person” (John 2:24-25). Jesus knows the conspiracy against him before it unfolds (John 13:1-3; 18:4); his foreknowledge is not merely prophetic warning but omniscience — YHWH’s own attribute of knowing all hearts (Acts 1:24).
11.22: A theme: “their young men will die by the sword; their sons and daughters will die by famine.” The judgment on those who conspired against Jeremiah extends to their households. This principle of covenant consequence — that the covenant penalty reaches those who reject the prophets — finds its most serious expression in Christ’s pronouncement over Jerusalem: “The blood of all the prophets shed since the foundation of the world will be charged against this generation” (Luke 11:50). Those who reject the last and greatest Prophet bear the accumulated weight of all prophetic rejection. The judgment on Anathoth’s conspirators is a small-scale enactment of this larger covenantal logic.

12.1: 'Righteous are you, O YHWH, when I complain to you; yet I would plead my case before you.' Jeremiah's bold theodicy prayer — 'Why does the way of the wicked prosper?' — is the prayer of the righteous sufferer that reaches its ultimate expression in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39-44) and on the cross (Psalm 22:1/Matt 27:46). Christ did not suppress the genuine anguish of innocent suffering but brought it fully before the Father, as Jeremiah models.
12.3: 'But you, O YHWH, know me; you see me, and test my heart toward you.' The claim to be known by YHWH in the midst of suffering anticipates Christ's certainty of the Father's knowledge even in abandonment. The Father knows the Son completely (John 10:15); the resurrection is the Father's public vindication of this mutual knowledge, confirming that the righteous sufferer's trust was not misplaced.
12.5: 'If you have raced with men on foot, and they have wearied you, how will you compete with horses?' YHWH's counter-challenge to Jeremiah — you have not yet seen real suffering — has a Christological echo in Heb 12:3-4: 'Consider him who endured such hostility from sinners against himself, so that you may not grow weary or fainthearted. In your struggle against sin you have not yet resisted to the point of shedding your blood.' The Christ-model of endurance surpasses all prophetic suffering.
12.7: 'I have forsaken my house; I have abandoned my heritage.' The divine abandonment of the temple and land — the most agonizing theological claim in Jeremiah (cf. Ezek 10-11) — finds its personal NT concentration in Christ's cry: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matt 27:46). The azav (abandon/forsake) of the sanctuary in Jeremiah 12 is the same root as in Psalm 22:1 that Christ utters on the cross. The macroscopic abandonment of the land is concentrated and borne personally by Christ.
12.10: 'Many shepherds have destroyed my vineyard; they have trampled down my portion.' The destroyed vineyard is the direct background for Christ's parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33-46) — YHWH's vineyard was entrusted to tenant-shepherds who destroyed it. Christ's parable updates the Isaianic and Jeremianic vineyard metaphor (Isa 5; Jer 12): the son is sent last, and they kill him too. The vineyard will be given to others (Matt 21:43).
12.11: 'They have made it a desolation; desolate, it mourns to me. The whole land is made desolate, but no man lays it to heart.' The mourning land (avlah alehu ha'aretz) parallels the cosmic lamentation at Christ's death: 'The sun's light failed, and the curtain of the temple was torn in two' (Luke 23:44-45). Creation's mourning at sin's devastation reaches its apex at the cross, where the fullness of human covenant-breaking is concentrated.
12.14: 'I will pluck up the house of Judah from among them, and after I have plucked them up I will return and have compassion on each of them, and I will bring them again, each to his heritage and each to his land.' The restoration promise — uprooting leading to replanting — operates on the pattern Christ enacts: through death (uprooting) comes resurrection life (replanting). Paul uses the olive-tree metaphor for the same logic: broken off to be grafted in (Rom 11:17-24).
12.15: 'And it shall come to pass after I have plucked them up, I will again have compassion on them, and I will bring them again, each to his heritage.' The extension of restoration to the nations — if they learn the ways of YHWH — anticipates the eschatological mission: Christ's commission is that all nations be discipled (Matt 28:19), bringing them from false gods to the true God, and thus into the inheritance.
12.17: 'But if any nation will not listen, then I will utterly pluck up and destroy that nation, declares YHWH.' The conditional judgment on non-listening nations is the same covenantal logic Jesus applies in Matthew 10:14-15: shaking the dust, declaring that Sodom and Gomorrah will fare better than cities that do not receive the word. The nata' (uproot/pluck) of the non-listening nation is the outcome Christ warns against.
12.2: A theme: “You planted them, and they have taken root; they grow and produce fruit; yet you are near to their lips but far from their hearts.” Jeremiah’s lament over hypocrites — prospering outwardly but distant from God inwardly — is the setting for Christ’s sharpest criticism of religious externalism: “This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me” (Matt 15:8, quoting Isa 29:13). The prophetic complaint of Jeremiah, picked up and deepened by Isaiah, is the direct precursor to Christ’s denunciation of scribal hypocrisy. The one who plants truly — Christ as the true vine (John 15:5) — produces genuine fruit precisely because the heart is united with the root, not merely the lips.
12.4: A theme: creation mourning because of the wickedness of its inhabitants — “the grass of every field dries up.” Paul draws on this same creation-suffering motif: “the creation groans in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time... waiting eagerly for the revelation of the sons of God” (Rom 8:19-22). Creation mourns under the curse of human sin in Jeremiah; it awaits liberation from the bondage of decay in Paul. Christ is the one through whom creation’s restoration comes — “all things were made through him... in him all things hold together” (Col 1:16-17) — and at his return the creation itself will be liberated into “the glorious freedom of the children of God” (Rom 8:21).
12.6: A direct anticipation: “Even your own brothers and your father’s household have betrayed you; even they have raised a loud cry after you.” Jeremiah’s betrayal by his own family from Anathoth is a type of Christ’s betrayal. Jesus experiences the same pattern: “For even his brothers did not believe in him” (John 7:5); and more precisely, betrayal by one of his inner circle (John 13:21). The prophet betrayed by his household is the template; the Messiah betrayed by his disciple is the fulfillment. Psalm 41:9 — “even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me” — bridges Jeremiah’s experience and Christ’s Passion.
12.8: A shadow: “my heritage has become like a lion in the forest to me; she has raised her voice against me; therefore I hate her.” This is YHWH’s lament over Israel turned enemy — the heritage that was beloved (v.7: “the one I deeply love”) has become a threatening lion. In Christ, the one who was “hated” in judgment becomes the Lion of Judah who vindicates his people: “weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered” (Rev 5:5). The rebelling lion-heritage of Jeremiah 12 is redeemed by the conquering Lion-Christ of Revelation. The judgment-hatred of Jer 12:8 gives way to the love that sent the Son (John 3:16).
12.9: A shadow: “is my heritage like a speckled bird of prey to me, with birds of prey all around attacking her?” The image of the speckled bird — distinctive, alien-looking, attacked by others — anticipates Christ as the rejected outsider. Jesus is attacked by the religious establishment, dismissed by his own people, “despised and rejected by men” (Isa 53:3). The birds gathered to attack the speckled one find their NT counterpart in the coalition of priests, scribes, Pharisees, and Romans assembled against Christ. Yet the attacked one is the one whom YHWH calls “my heritage” — precious, owned, ultimately vindicated.
12.12: A theme: “the sword of the LORD devours from one end of the land to the other.” The sword of divine judgment runs through the prophetic corpus as the image of eschatological reckoning. Christ identifies himself with this sword-dimension of the divine: “Do not think that I have come to bring peace to the earth. I have not come to bring peace, but a sword” (Matt 10:34). The sword Christ brings is not violence but the word that divides truth from falsehood, the faithful from the faithless. Hebrews 4:12 identifies this sword with the living and active word of God. The sword of Jeremiah 12:12 finds its NT fulfillment in the sword proceeding from the mouth of the glorified Christ (Rev 19:15).
12.13: A theme: “they planted wheat but harvested thorns; they wore themselves out for nothing.” The futility of the faithless farmer is the obverse of Christ’s parable of the sower, where good seed on good ground yields thirty-, sixty-, and a hundredfold (Matt 13:8). The curse of thorns — “cursed is the ground because of you; through painful toil you will eat food from it until you return to the ground... it will produce thorns and thistles for you” (Gen 3:17-18) — is what Jeremiah’s farmers encounter. Christ wears the crown of thorns at his Passion, absorbing the curse-of-the-ground into his own body, so that those who sow in him may harvest life.
12.16: A fulfillment: “And if they will diligently learn the ways of my people... then they will be built up among my people.” This is the promise that Gentile nations who embrace covenant life will be incorporated into the covenant community. In Christ, this promise is fulfilled universally: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations” (Matt 28:19); “In Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith” (Gal 3:26). The nations who “learn the ways of my people” in Jeremiah 12:16 become the nations “built up among my people” — a direct anticipation of the one new humanity formed in Christ from both Jew and Gentile (Eph 2:14-15).

13.1: The linen waistband (ezor pishta) pressed against YHWH's loins — the most intimate garment — is the sign-act of Israel's covenantal purpose: to cling to YHWH as a waistband clings to the body (v11). In the NT, the covenant-garment metaphor is transformed: believers 'put on Christ' (Gal 3:27; Rom 13:14), Christ becomes the garment of righteousness. The sign-act of the ruined waistband is reversed by the new-covenant garment that cannot be corrupted.
13.9: 'Thus will I ruin the pride of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem.' The destruction of national pride is the condition for receiving grace. The Beatitudes begin 'Blessed are the poor in spirit' (Matt 5:3) — the bankruptcy of self-reliance that the ruined waistband enacts is the prerequisite for the kingdom. Christ brings not the reinforcement of human pride but its deconstruction as the path to covenant restoration.
13.11: 'For as the waistband clings to the loins of a man, so I made the whole house of Israel and all of Judah cling to me, declares YHWH, that they might be for me a people, a name, a praise, and a glory.' The original covenant purpose — intimate adhesion to YHWH, being his glory and praise — is what sin has destroyed and Christ comes to restore. The church as 'a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' (1 Pet 2:9) is the restored covenant people whose clinging to Christ fulfills what the linen waistband could not.
13.13: 'I will fill with drunkenness all the inhabitants of this land.' The divine wine of judgment — here judgment on covenant-breakers — becomes in the NT the Supper's wine that is the blood of the new covenant (Matt 26:27-28). The cup that the wicked receive as wrath (Rev 14:10) is the same cup Christ drains for his people (Mark 10:38; 14:36), converting the judgment-cup into the covenant-cup.
13.14: 'I will not pity or spare or have compassion, that I should not destroy them.' The suspended compassion that Jeremiah announces as judgment on the unrepentant is the necessary backdrop for Paul's declaration: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' (Rom 5:8). Compassion is not merely withheld — it is channeled through the cross, where the wrath that would destroy the unrepentant falls on the Son, allowing compassion to flow to the repentant.
13.15: 'Hear and give ear; be not proud, for YHWH has spoken.' The repeated call to humble hearing throughout Jeremiah finds its NT fulfillment in Christ as the One to whom all must listen: 'This is my beloved Son; listen to him' (Mark 9:7; cf. Deut 18:15 — the prophet like Moses). The pride that refuses to listen is the same pride that puts the Son to death; the humility that hears becomes salvation.
13.16: 'Give glory to YHWH your God before he brings darkness, before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains.' The call to give glory before judgment — before the darkness closes in — is the missionary urgency of the NT. In Rev 14:7, the angel flying in midheaven calls: 'Fear God and give him glory, for the hour of his judgment has come.' Christ opens the window of grace; the call to glorify YHWH before darkness is the shape of every evangelistic appeal.
13.17: 'But if you will not listen, my soul will weep in secret for your pride.' Jeremiah's secret weeping over the pride that will bring destruction is an unmistakable type of Christ's weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44): 'When he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it, saying, “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!”' Both Jeremiah and Jesus weep over the same stubborn city for the same fatal pride.
13.23: 'Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil.' The impossibility of self-reformation — the entrenched habit of sin that cannot be self-corrected — is the problem Christ addresses not by human self-improvement but by new creation: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come' (2 Cor 5:17). What the leopard cannot do, the Spirit effects.
13.25: 'This is your lot, the portion I have measured out to you, declares YHWH, because you have forgotten me and trusted in lies.' Forgetting YHWH — the covenant amnesia that produces idolatrous trust in lies — is addressed by Christ who calls himself 'the truth' (John 14:6). The Spirit he sends is the Spirit of truth who will 'bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you' (John 14:26) — the covenantal remembering that reverses Jeremiah's covenantal forgetting.
13.27: 'Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will it be before you are made clean?' The closing lament — the city that cannot cleanse itself — anticipates both John the Baptist's baptism of repentance and Christ's declaration 'I will; be clean' to the leper (Mark 1:41). The impossibility of self-cleansing that Jeremiah laments is resolved by the one who washes his disciples' feet (John 13:8-10) and whose blood cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7).
13.2: A theme: the first stage of the linen sash sign-act — buying and wearing the sash according to YHWH’s command. The linen sash (ʾēzôr) was the undergarment worn against the skin — an intimate article of clothing. The sign-act begins with perfect obedience: “I bought the sash, as the LORD directed.” Christ’s obedience follows the same pattern: “I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 6:38). The prophet who acts at YHWH’s command models the Servant who “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Heb 5:8).
13.3: A theme: the second divine word comes to Jeremiah. The sign-act is multi-staged, requiring repeated divine instruction. God’s pedagogy through enacted parables — commanding his prophet to live out the message — anticipates Christ’s own use of enacted signs: the cleansing of the temple, the cursing of the fig tree, the foot-washing. The prophet’s life becomes a living parable of judgment and mercy. In this sense, Jeremiah’s prophetic career is a corporate “type” that Christ fulfills — not one enacted sign but an entire life that enacts the word of God.
13.4: A theme: hiding the sash at the Euphrates — YHWH’s people hidden and ruined among the nations. The Euphrates is the emblem of exile — Babylon lies along it. The sash is to be buried there, symbolizing that Israel will be “hidden” in Babylon, spoiled by exile. Christ uses the hiding-and-finding motif in his parables: the treasure hidden in a field (Matt 13:44), the lost coin (Luke 15:8-10). The thing that is hidden is the thing of greatest value — found by the one who seeks diligently. The “hidden” people of Israel, spoiled in Babylon, are found and restored by the one who sought the lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7).
13.5: A theme: “so I went and hid it at the Euphrates, as the LORD commanded.” Jeremiah’s complete obedience to the enacted sign — including the physical act of travel to the Euphrates (whether literal or visionary) — underlines the cost of prophetic obedience. The prophets did not merely speak; they lived the message. Christ embodies this principle at the highest possible level: the incarnation is the ultimate enactment of the divine message, the Word made flesh dwelling among us (John 1:14). YHWH does not merely describe exile; he sends his prophet to enact it. The Father does not merely describe redemption; he sends his Son to accomplish it.
13.6: A theme: the divine command to retrieve the ruined sash — “go to the Euphrates; retrieve the sash I told you to hide there.” The retrieval is the return from exile, the restoration after judgment. This two-stage movement — hiding (exile) and retrieval (restoration) — is the deep structure of the exile-return narrative. Christ himself enters the “exile” of death and is retrieved/raised: the resurrection is YHWH retrieving from death the one he had given over. The resurrection as the divine act of recovery mirrors the retrieve-the-sash command: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption” (Acts 2:27).
13.7: A direct anticipation: “I took the sash from the place where I had hidden it — and the sash was ruined; it was no longer useful for anything.” The sash ruined by burial — unable to serve its purpose of clinging to YHWH — is a type of Israel in exile, marred and made useless through covenant unfaithfulness. In Christ, the ruined humanity is restored: “if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come” (2 Cor 5:17). The sash that was useless is restored not by the sash reforming itself but by the divine act of re-creation in Christ — the ruined garment replaced by the robe of righteousness (Isa 61:10; Rev 3:5).
13.8: A theme: the word of YHWH comes again, interpreting the sign. The pattern of action followed by divine interpretation is the structure of prophetic sign-acts: the act is performed, then YHWH explains its meaning. Christ uses this same pattern — healing, then teaching; feeding, then explaining. John’s Gospel in particular organizes around “signs” followed by interpretive discourses. The structure of enacted parable (sign-act) followed by divine word is one of the deep continuities between Jeremiah’s prophetic method and Jesus’s teaching method.
13.10: A theme: “this wicked people who refuse to listen to my words, who follow the stubbornness of their heart... will be like that ruined sash, good for nothing.” The interpretive conclusion of the sash sign-act applies the image to the spiritually hardened community. Christ identifies the same hardness: “Though they have seen all these signs, they still would not believe” (John 12:37); and attributes it to the prophetically predicted hardening (Isa 6:10; John 12:40). The sash good for nothing is the person who refuses to cling to YHWH — and the only remedy is not reformation of the old sash but new creation in Christ, who makes all things new.
13.12: A shadow: “every wineskin will be filled with wine.” The people’s response (“we know that”) treats this as a truism, but YHWH transforms it into a judgment metaphor: they will be filled with the wine of confusion and staggering. Christ uses the new wine / new wineskin image to describe his own coming: “No one puts new wine into old wineskins; if he does, the new wine will burst the wineskins” (Luke 5:37). The wine of Jeremiah’s judgment-filling (drunken staggering) contrasts with the new wine of the kingdom (joy and abundance at the wedding feast, John 2:1-11; Rev 19:9).
13.18: A direct anticipation: “Say to the king and to the queen mother: Take a lower place and sit down; your positions of authority will come down off your heads.” The humiliation of the Davidic king — Jehoiachin and his mother before Nebuchadnezzar — is part of the low point of the Davidic line that Christ reverses. Jesus is born into the humbled Davidic line: no throne, no palace, “no place to lay his head” (Luke 9:58), born to a young woman in an occupied land. Yet this humiliation is the path to the exaltation: “God highly exalted him and gave him the name that is above every name” (Phil 2:9). The crown taken off at Jer 13:18 is the crown that Christ restores in glory.
13.19: A theme: “The towns of the Negev will be locked shut with no one to open them; all of Judah will be taken into exile.” The total exile — no one left to open the locked towns — is the nadir of the Davidic covenant. Christ is the key of David: “the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one will shut, who shuts and no one opens” (Rev 3:7). The locked towns of Jeremiah’s judgment are the shadow of every door closed by human sin; Christ is the one who opens what no one can shut — beginning with the sealed tomb from which he rises.
13.20: A theme: “Where is the flock that was entrusted to you — the beautiful flock?” The scattered flock is the people of God in the hour of their judgment. Christ the Good Shepherd answers this question definitively: “I am the good shepherd; I know my own and my own know me” (John 10:14); “I lay down my life for the sheep” (John 10:15). The beautiful flock scattered in Jeremiah’s day is gathered by the Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7), ultimately laying down his life to gather the flock that had been entrusted to him.
13.21: A theme: “What will you say when he punishes you — when those you trained as your allies are placed over you as head?” The reversal — trusted allies become masters — is the logic of covenant curse (Deut 28:44). Christ’s kingdom operates by the same reversal-logic but in grace: the first will be last and the last first (Matt 20:16); the one who humbles himself will be exalted (Luke 14:11). Where Jeremiah’s reversal is punitive (Judah’s allies become her rulers), Christ’s reversal is redemptive (the servant becomes the heir, Gal 4:7).
13.22: A theme: “it is because of the magnitude of your iniquity that your skirts were lifted up.” The exposure of shame as covenant penalty appears throughout the prophets (Ezek 16; Hos 2). Christ provides the covering for human shame: “I counsel you to buy from me white garments so that you may clothe yourself and the shame of your nakedness may not be seen” (Rev 3:18). The shame exposed in Jeremiah’s judgment is the same shame that Christ covers at the cross — putting on himself the nakedness of crucifixion so that his people might be clothed in the righteousness he earned (2 Cor 5:21; Isa 61:10).
13.24: A theme: “I will scatter them like chaff blown away by a desert wind.” The winnowing image — separating wheat from chaff, the genuine from the worthless — runs from the prophets to John the Baptist to Christ: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor, gathering his wheat into the barn and burning up the chaff with unquenchable fire” (Matt 3:12). The scattering by desert wind in Jeremiah 13 is a preview of the eschatological winnowing at which Christ presides. The same Christ who is himself the grain of wheat that falls into the earth and dies (John 12:24) will gather the final harvest and separate chaff from grain.
13.26: A theme: “so I will pull your skirts up over your face and expose your shameful nakedness.” The prophetic use of exposure imagery as covenant judgment is widespread (Ezek 16; Nah 3:5). It speaks of the stripping away of the pretensions of the covenant people who have acted as faithless. Christ on the cross enacts the ultimate exposure of shame — stripped of his garments while soldiers cast lots (John 19:23-24; Ps 22:18) — not because of his own shame but to bear the shame of others. His exposure becomes the covering: “put on the Lord Jesus Christ” (Rom 13:14) is Paul’s application of the garment-theology that runs from Genesis through Jeremiah to the cross.

1.1: The book opens with a title that names Jeremiah as "son of Hilkiah, of the priests in Anathoth." Anathoth was a Levitical city in Benjamin (Josh 21:18), where Abiathar the priest had been exiled by Solomon (1 Kings 2:26-27). The priestly lineage ties Jeremiah to the Mosaic-Levitical covenant tradition. Christologically, this lineage anticipates Jesus, who is both the fulfillment of the prophetic office and the new High Priest — the one who unlike Abiathar is not replaced but holds his priesthood permanently (Heb 7:24). The opening genealogy grounds the prophet's authority in covenant history; Christ's genealogies (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38) ground his authority in both royal and priestly covenant lines.
1.2: "The word of the LORD came to him in the days of Josiah" — the prophetic reception formula marks the beginning of forty years of ministry. Josiah's reign (640-609 BCE) is the context of early Jeremianic prophecy, a period of covenant renewal under the rediscovered Torah (2 Kings 22-23). Christologically, the phrase dĕḇar-YHWH (word of YHWH) that "comes" to Jeremiah anticipates the climactic coming of the Word himself. John 1:14 reverses the formula: the Word that had been coming to prophets in words "became flesh and tabernacled among us." Every prophetic reception formula in Jeremiah is a penultimate form of the ultimate coming of the Word incarnate.
1.3: The ministry spans the reigns of Jehoiakim and Zedekiah, concluding with the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BCE. The temporal frame — from covenant renewal under Josiah to exile under Zedekiah — covers the complete arc of Israel's final preexilic history. Christologically, this arc is the OT type of which the NT is the antitype: the period of prophetic warning, rejection, and judgment that concludes in the exile corresponds typologically to the period of John the Baptist and Jesus, whose warnings were similarly rejected, whose rejection led to the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE (Matt 24:1-2; Luke 21:20-24). The temporal frame of Jeremiah's ministry is itself a Christological type.
1.4: "The word of the LORD came to me" — the first-person shift signals the transition to the call narrative (vv4-19). Jeremiah is now narrator and protagonist. The personal reception of the divine word — the prophet as the chosen vessel of the dĕḇar-YHWH — is the OT form of what Jesus claims absolutely: "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away" (Matt 24:35). The difference is structural: Jeremiah receives the word; Jesus is the Word (John 1:1-3). The same eternal divine speech that comes to Jeremiah in a moment of historical calling is, in Christ, personally and permanently embodied.
1.5: "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you; I appointed you a prophet to the nations." This verse is among the most Christologically dense in Jeremiah. The prenatal divine knowing (yĕdaʿtîḵā) and consecration (qiddaštîḵā) find their deepest fulfillment in Christ: Eph 1:4 ("chosen in him before the foundation of the world") and 1 Pet 1:20 ("foreknown before the foundation of the world, but revealed in the last times for you") apply precisely this pre-temporal divine appointment to Christ. Christ is not merely appointed to his prophetic office before birth; he exists before creation and is set apart for his redemptive mission in the eternal counsel of God. The appointment "to the nations" (lāggôyim) finds direct fulfillment in the Great Commission (Matt 28:19-20) and in Paul's self-description as apostle to the nations (Rom 1:5; Gal 1:16) — which Paul reads as his own participation in the prophetic calling that was first Christ's.
1.6: Jeremiah's objection: "I do not know how to speak, for I am only a child (naʿar)." The self-deprecation follows the pattern of Moses (Exod 4:10: "I am not a man of words") and Gideon (Judg 6:15: "I am the least in my family"). Christ makes no such objection — he is not a prophet called to a task beyond himself but the eternal Logos who is himself the divine speech. The contrast between Jeremiah's inadequacy and Christ's sufficiency is Pauline: "Not that we are sufficient in ourselves to claim anything as coming from us, but our sufficiency is from God, who has made us sufficient to be ministers of a new covenant" (2 Cor 3:5-6). Jeremiah's inadequacy models the insufficiency of all human agents; Christ models the sufficiency of the divine agent.
1.7: "You shall go to everyone I send you to, and you shall speak everything I command you." The divine commissioning overrules Jeremiah's self-assessment. The language of sending (šālaḥ) is foundational to the prophetic office — the prophet is a sent one, a šālûaḥ. Jesus uses this same vocabulary of divine sending throughout John's Gospel to describe his own mission: "The Father who sent me has himself commanded me what to say and what to speak" (John 12:49); "As the Father has sent me, so I am sending you" (John 20:21). Jeremiah's commission is the OT model for the apostolic pattern — "sent" on the Father's behalf to speak the Father's words — which reaches its ultimate form in the Son's own mission.
1.8: "Do not be afraid of them, for I am with you to rescue you." The ʾal-tîrāʾ (do not fear) formula combined with the divine presence promise (kî ʾittĕḵā ʾānî, "for I am with you") is the standard commissioning assurance of the OT (Josh 1:5, 9; Isa 41:10, 13; 43:1-5). Jesus's final words to his disciples repeat the same formula: "Behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age" (Matt 28:20). The divine presence that accompanied Jeremiah's prophetic ministry is the same presence Christ promises to his church — now mediated through the Spirit who is Christ's own presence (John 14:16-18). The presence-promise does not guarantee absence of opposition (as Jeremiah's bitter experience shows) but guarantees ultimate rescue (nāṣal).
1.9: "The LORD reached out his hand and touched my mouth. The LORD said: See, I have placed my words in your mouth." The physical gesture of divine touch — YHWH's hand on Jeremiah's mouth — is the prophet-consecration act. Isaiah's lips were touched with a burning coal (Isa 6:7); Ezekiel ate the scroll (Ezek 3:1-3). The mouth becomes the conduit for the divine word. Christologically, this is the OT form of what John 1:14 expresses in reverse: rather than divine words placed in a human mouth, in Christ the divine Word becomes human flesh entire. The instrumentality of the prophet's mouth yields to the identity of the incarnate Word. Jesus does not receive words to speak; he speaks as one who has authority in himself (Matt 7:29: "he taught as one who had authority, not as their scribes").
1.10: "I have appointed you this day over nations and kingdoms, to uproot and to tear down, to destroy and to demolish, to build and to plant." The six-infinitive commission (four of destruction, two of construction) defines the entire scope of Jeremiah's prophetic ministry. Christologically, this commission finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ's own authority over the nations. Jesus claims "all authority in heaven and on earth" (Matt 28:18) — the universal scope of Jeremiah's commission but without limitation. The "uproot and tear down" side is fulfilled in Jesus's announcement of Jerusalem's destruction (Matt 24:1-2) and in the cross, which dismantles the old order; the "build and plant" side is fulfilled in the resurrection and in the new covenant community (Matt 16:18: "I will build my church"). Paul applies the same vocabulary to his own apostolic authority (2 Cor 10:8; 13:10), understanding his ministry as a continuation of the Jeremianic commission through participation in Christ.
1.11: "I see a branch of an almond tree (šāqēd)." The first vision — the almond branch — is paired with the divine response in v12. The almond tree (šāqēd) is the earliest-blooming tree of the Israeli spring, a sign that dormancy has ended and new life is beginning. In the Christological reading, the almond branch as first sign of divine watchfulness over the prophetic word anticipates Christ as the one in whom YHWH's long-dormant promises burst into fulfillment. Luke 1:26-38 presents the annunciation as the sudden, surprising arrival of the long-expected new beginning — God's own branch appearing at the appointed moment.
1.12: "I am watching (šōqēd) over my word to carry it out." The wordplay (šāqēd/šōqēd) grounds the vision's meaning: YHWH's attentiveness to his own spoken word guarantees its fulfillment. This is the foundational claim of biblical prophecy and, Christologically, of the entire NT. Jesus's repeated use of hina plērōthē ("so that it might be fulfilled") and his statement "I have not come to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them" (Matt 5:17) are explicit claims that YHWH's watchfulness over his word (Jer 1:12) is being enacted in his own person and ministry. Every OT prophecy that Jesus fulfills is a demonstration that YHWH has been watching over his word from the moment it was spoken.
1.13: "I see a boiling pot with its mouth turned from the north." The second vision — a cauldron tipped to pour from the north — inaugurates the northern-foe motif that structures the entire book of Jeremiah. The boiling, scalding contents about to flood from the north mirror the language of divine wrath executed through historical instruments. Christologically, the judgment this cauldron represents — ultimately enacted in the Babylonian invasion — is the OT form of the eschatological judgment Jesus announces: the coming of the Son of Man (Matt 24:30-31) and the final pouring out of divine judgment (Rev 14:10; 16:1-21). The tilting cauldron is a type of the eschatological cup of wrath.
1.14: "Disaster will break out from the north against all who live in this land." The divine interpretation of the boiling-pot vision establishes the northern-threat as YHWH's own action, not merely political contingency. Christologically, the announcement of coming judgment is a pattern Christ replicates and intensifies: his laments over Jerusalem (Matt 23:37-39; Luke 19:41-44) announce the coming disaster in the same posture — YHWH/Christ as both the one who judges and the one who grieves. The northern disaster that Jeremiah announces is thus the OT type of the Roman disaster of 70 CE, which Jesus announces as the fulfillment of both Jeremianic and Danielic patterns.
1.15: "I am summoning all the peoples of the kingdoms of the north... they will each set up his throne at the entrance to the gates of Jerusalem." The image of foreign thrones at Jerusalem's gates represents the ultimate humiliation of the city that was to be YHWH's throne (3:17; Ps 2:6). Christologically, the thrones set up against Jerusalem anticipate the eschatological reversal in which Christ himself sits on the throne in the restored Jerusalem (Rev 22:3: "the throne of God and of the Lamb"). The human thrones displacing YHWH's reign in Jeremiah are the negative image of the divine throne that will ultimately displace all human thrones in the consummation.
1.16: "I will pronounce my judgments against them for all their wickedness — they have forsaken me, burned incense to other gods, and bowed down to the works of their own hands." The three-fold indictment (forsaking, burning incense to other gods, bowing to human-made objects) defines covenant unfaithfulness as its core. Christologically, these three forms of apostasy are the negative context that makes Christ's covenant faithfulness intelligible. Where Israel forsook YHWH, Christ in his earthly life was absolutely faithful to the Father (John 8:29: "I always do what pleases him"); where Israel burned incense to other gods, Christ refused all temptation to serve another (Matt 4:8-10: "Worship the Lord your God and serve only him"). The indictment of Israel's covenant failure is the dark background against which Christ's covenant obedience shines.
1.17: "Get yourself ready. Arise and speak to them everything I command you. Do not be terrified by them, or I will terrify you before them." The command to Jeremiah is a commissioning for conflict — the prophet must not capitulate to human fear, or YHWH's own reaction to capitulation will be more terrifying than the opponents. Jesus warns his disciples with similar urgency: "Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell" (Matt 10:28). The alignment of divine and human will — Jeremiah must not let human fear override divine commission — is the OT form of Gethsemane's resolution: "Not my will, but yours be done" (Luke 22:42).
1.18: "See, I have made you today a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls to stand against the whole land." YHWH's protective action transforms Jeremiah's weakness into divine strength. The three architectural images — fortified city (ʿîr miḇṣār), iron pillar (ʿammûd barzél), bronze walls (ḥōmôt nĕḥōšet) — describe a structure impervious to human assault. Christologically, Christ is himself the foundation and fortress of the new covenant community. Paul's prison letters envision the church's steadfastness as participation in Christ's own unshakeable strength (Phil 4:13; Eph 6:10-11); Peter describes Christ as the cornerstone against whom all opposition shatters (1 Pet 2:6-8). Jeremiah's individual strengthening is the type; the church's corporate strengthening in Christ is the fulfillment.
1.19: "They will fight against you, but they will not overcome you, for I am with you to rescue you." The promise of v8 is now restated as the closing frame of the call narrative, creating an inclusio of divine presence and protection. The guarantee — opposition guaranteed, but ultimate rescue also guaranteed — is the pattern of the apostolic and prophetic experience throughout Scripture. Jesus promises his disciples the same paradoxical assurance: "In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world" (John 16:33). The "overcoming" that is denied to Jeremiah's opponents is the overcoming Jesus claims for himself — and through which his people share in the victory. Rev 3:21 ("the one who conquers... as I also conquered") makes this typological connection explicit.

2.1: "The word of the LORD came to me" — the standard prophetic reception formula opening the covenant-lawsuit section (chs 2-3). This formula recurs throughout Jeremiah as the structural signal of a new divine address. Christologically, every instance of the word of YHWH coming to Jeremiah is a subordinate form of the final, definitive coming of the Word in Christ. As Hebrews 1:1-2 frames it: "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets" — each formula-instance is one of those many-times — "but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." The repetition of the reception formula across Jeremiah's forty years underscores the accumulation of partial speech that points to the final and complete speaking in Christ.
2.2: "I remember the devotion of your youth, the love of your bridal days — how you followed me through the wilderness." YHWH's covenant memory (zāḵartî) of the betrothal period — Israel's following YHWH through the wilderness — establishes the marriage metaphor that governs chs 2-3. Christologically, this divine remembering of covenant love finds its eschatological fulfillment in Christ as the Bridegroom who loved his bride before she existed. Eph 5:25-27 applies this pre-temporal love to Christ: "Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her." The bridal devotion that Israel failed to sustain, the church sustains through Christ's own faithfulness imputed to her. Rev 19:7-8 ("the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready") is the restoration of exactly this bridal devotion, achieved through the Bridegroom's own work.
2.3: "Israel was holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of his harvest." The qōdeš (holy, set apart) status of Israel as YHWH's rēʾšît (firstfruits) positions Israel as the dedicated portion belonging exclusively to YHWH. Those who consume what is dedicated to YHWH incur guilt. Christologically, Christ is himself the ʾappîryon rēʾšît (firstfruits) of the new creation — the resurrected firstfruit of those who have died (1 Cor 15:20, 23). Israel's firstfruits status was provisional and violated; Christ's firstfruits status is permanent and never violated. The church, in Christ, participates in his firstfruits consecration (James 1:18: "a kind of firstfruits of his creatures").
2.4: "Hear the word of the LORD, O house of Jacob, all the families of Israel" — the formal summons to the covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) opening. The legal address to "house of Jacob... all the families of Israel" encompasses the entire covenant people. Christologically, the same summons to hear the divine word reaches its most urgent form in the words of Jesus: "Truly, truly I say to you..." (the Johannine amen amen) and in the Synoptic "He who has ears, let him hear" (Matt 11:15; 13:9, 43). The prophetic call to hear the LORD's word is intensified in Christ because the speaker is himself the Word: to hear Christ is to hear the Father directly (John 14:9).
2.5: "What fault did your fathers find in me, that they went so far from me, following worthless idols and becoming worthless themselves?" The rhetorical question of the divine plaintiff invites Israel to state her charges against YHWH — the implication being that none can be produced. YHWH is faultless; Israel's abandonment is groundless. Christologically, this same groundless abandonment reaches its climactic expression in John's passion narrative: the crowds choose Barabbas over Jesus (John 18:38-40), and Pilate — echoing the divine plaintiff — can find no fault in him (John 18:38: "I find no guilt in him at all"). The Jeremianic covenant lawsuit is the OT form of the Johannine passion trial: the faultless one is rejected without cause.
2.6: "They did not ask: Where is the LORD who brought us up out of Egypt, who led us through the wilderness?" The failure to ask after YHWH — to seek him, to inquire of his presence — is the covenant violation that underlies all the others. The wilderness guidance (pillar of cloud/fire, the manna, the water from the rock) is the specific divine provision forgotten. Christologically, Paul identifies the wilderness-provision with Christ explicitly: "all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ" (1 Cor 10:4). Israel's failure to ask "Where is YHWH who provided?" is therefore a failure to seek Christ himself, who was present in the wilderness provision. The seeking that Israel failed to do is the seeking Jesus invites: "Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find" (Matt 7:7).
2.7: "I brought you into a land of plenty, to enjoy its fruit and abundance. But when you entered, you defiled my land and made my heritage loathsome." The gift of the land — YHWH's inheritance given to Israel — is defiled by the very recipients. Christologically, this pattern of gift-defiled-by-recipients is the universal human story that only Christ breaks. Adam received the garden and defiled it; Israel received the land and defiled it; but Christ receives the inheritance of all creation (Ps 2:8; Heb 1:2: "appointed the heir of all things") and does not defile it. The defilement-of-inheritance pattern anticipates the need for a new Adam and a new Israel who will receive the inheritance faithfully — which is Christ's role (Rom 5:17-19; 1 Cor 15:45-49).
2.8: "The priests did not ask, 'Where is the LORD?'... the prophets prophesied by Baal." Every appointed guardian of the covenant has failed: priests, Torah-teachers, rulers, and prophets alike. The comprehensive leadership failure creates a vacuum of faithful covenant mediation. Christologically, this vacuum is precisely what Christ fills as the new and better covenant mediator: he is simultaneously the faithful High Priest who always asks after the Father (John 11:41-42; 17:1-26), the true Prophet who speaks only what the Father has given him (John 12:49-50), and the righteous King who executes justice (Rev 19:11). Where every human covenant official failed, Christ alone fulfills all three offices — the threefold office (prophet, priest, king) is the Christological answer to the threefold institutional failure of v8.
2.9: "Therefore I will bring charges against you again... and against your grandchildren will I bring charges." The intergenerational scope of the covenant lawsuit — extending through generations — is the legal form of what Paul addresses in Rom 1-3: the universal human condition of covenant violation, cumulative and intergenerational. The need for an answer to this intergenerational indictment is the need that Christ's work addresses: his obedience is credited to all generations of faith (Rom 5:18-19; Gal 3:13-14). The legal charges are answered not by human defense but by the one who takes the covenant-violation penalty himself.
2.10: "Cross to the shores of Cyprus and look; send to Kedar and examine carefully." The invitation to a cross-cultural survey — from Cyprus (westernmost of the ANE) to Kedar (Arab tribes to the east) — establishes the universality of the charge: no nation has abandoned its gods. Christologically, this universal survey anticipates the NT's universal proclamation. The nations that were examined for loyalty to their false gods are the same nations to whom Christ is proclaimed as the living God (Acts 17:22-31). What the pagan nations did not do for their idols — abandon them — the church does for Christ: holds to him despite all opposition (Phil 1:27-28).
2.11: "Has any nation changed its gods, even though they are not real gods? But my people have exchanged their glory for things that are worthless." The irony is cutting: pagan nations at least maintain loyalty to their non-existent gods, while Israel abandons the real and living God for nonentities. kĕḇôdām (their glory) — Israel has exchanged YHWH himself, who is their glory (Ps 106:20 quotes this verse directly). Christologically, Paul applies exactly this exchange to the universal human condition in Rom 1:23 ("exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images"), reading Jer 2:11 as the diagnosis of the universal human problem that only the revelation of Christ's glory answers (2 Cor 4:6: "the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ").
2.12: "Be appalled at this, O heavens; shudder with great horror." The call for the heavens to witness and react to Israel's apostasy invokes the cosmic lawsuit of Deut 32:1 ("Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak"). The heavens as witnesses in the covenant lawsuit are a standard OT legal convention. Christologically, this cosmic horror at covenant violation finds its ultimate expression at the cross, where the heavens respond to the crucifixion: the midday darkness (Matt 27:45), the earthquake, and the torn veil (Matt 27:51-52). The cosmic šāmmâ (horror) provoked by Israel's covenant violation in Jeremiah is fulfilled and surpassed by the cosmic response to the one who bore that violation in himself.
2.13: "My people have committed two evils: they have abandoned me, the spring of living water, and dug their own cisterns — cracked cisterns that cannot hold water." The most Christologically explicit verse in ch2. Jesus applies both sides of the contrast to himself in conversation with the Samaritan woman: he is the one who gives "living water" (mayim ḥayyîm) that becomes "a spring of water welling up to eternal life" (John 4:10-14). In John 7:37-38, Jesus stands at the Feast of Tabernacles — the water-drawing festival — and cries: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink... out of his heart will flow rivers of living water." The spring of living water that Israel abandoned in Jer 2:13 is identified as Christ himself. The cracked cisterns of human religious self-sufficiency (whether idolatry or second-temple Judaism) are contrasted with the inexhaustible spring that is Christ. Rev 22:1 ("the river of the water of life... flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb") is the eschatological fulfillment of Jer 2:13's positive image.
2.14: "Is Israel a slave? Was he born into slavery? Then why has he become plunder?" The rhetorical questions address the scandal of Israel's historical vulnerability — given her covenant status as YHWH's firstborn (Exod 4:22), why is she treated like a slave? Christologically, this paradox of the covenant son becoming a slave anticipates Phil 2:6-8 (Christ "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but... taking the form of a servant"). The Son who should be free from slavery becomes a slave — but in Christ's case, voluntarily, for redemptive purpose. Gal 4:4-5 makes the connection explicit: God sent his Son "born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons."
2.15: "Young lions have roared against him and growled; they have laid his land waste; his cities have been burned." The image of marauding lions destroying the land is the military-desolation metaphor. Christologically, the lion imagery has a double resonance in the NT: Peter warns that the devil "prowls around like a roaring lion" (1 Pet 5:8), using the same image for destructive opposition; but Rev 5:5 presents Christ as "the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David" who has conquered — reversing the imagery. The lion that destroys Israel becomes, in Christ, the Lion who defeats all enemies.
2.16: "The people of Memphis and Tahpanhes have shaved the top of your head." Egypt's treatment of Judah — the head-shaving as sign of disgrace and subjugation — is the immediate geopolitical humiliation. Christologically, the head-shaving (crown-of-head dishonor) anticipates the crown of thorns placed on Christ at his passion (Matt 27:29; John 19:2-3). The dishonor inflicted on the covenant people foreshadows the dishonor inflicted on the covenant representative — except that in Christ's case, the dishonor is reversed into glory (Phil 2:9-11).
2.17: "Have you not brought this on yourself by forsaking the LORD your God when he led you on the way?" The rhetorical question places the responsibility for the covenant disaster squarely on Israel's own unfaithfulness — the self-caused suffering of forsaking the divine guide. Christologically, Christ embodies the reversal: rather than Israel who forsook the divine guide on "the way," Christ declares himself to be "the way" (John 14:6) and never departs from it. The disciples who forsook Jesus at Gethsemane (Matt 26:56) re-enact the Jeremianic pattern; the resurrection reverses it, as Christ pursues and restores the scattered disciples (John 21:1-19).
2.18: "Why go down to Egypt to drink from the waters of the Nile? Or why go to Assyria to drink from the Euphrates?" The double question addresses Judah's political strategy of seeking alliances with the two great powers rather than trusting YHWH. Drinking from the Nile or Euphrates is political dependence dressed as wisdom. Christologically, this false seeking of foreign water is the foil to Jesus's offer at the well: "Everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again" (John 4:13-14). The choice between the Nile/Euphrates and the spring of living water is the OT form of the choice between all human resources and Christ.
2.19: "Your own wickedness will punish you, and your turning away will rebuke you." The principle of intrinsic moral consequence — sin as its own punishment — is a consistent wisdom theme (Prov 1:31; 5:22). Christologically, Gal 6:7-8 ("whatever a man sows, that he will also reap") formulates the same principle. But the gospel's radical move is that Christ enters into the consequence of others' sowing — he who sowed only righteousness reaps the harvest of others' wickedness (Gal 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us"). The intrinsic-consequence principle of Jer 2:19 is not negated by the cross but absorbed into it: Christ's death is the enacted consequence of humanity's turning away.
2.20: "For long ago I broke your yoke and tore off your chains. But you said, 'I will not serve!'" The exodus liberation — YHWH breaking Israel's yoke of slavery in Egypt — is met with refusal to serve YHWH as liberator. The paradox: freed from one yoke, Israel refuses the yoke that is the covenant. Christologically, Jesus offers a different yoke: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you... for my yoke is easy and my burden is light" (Matt 11:28-30). The refusal to serve YHWH in Jer 2:20 is the form of the same rebellion against Christ's gentle yoke. The difference: the new covenant yoke is borne by the one who offers it — Christ carries his own yoke.
2.21: "I had planted you as a choice vine (śōrēq), of pure and wholesome seed. How then have you turned against me into the wild shoots of a foreign vine?" The vineyard imagery is one of the most developed Christological types in the OT. Isaiah 5:1-7 expands it into the Song of the Vineyard (the beloved's vineyard producing wild grapes). Jesus explicitly takes up this imagery in John 15:1: "I am the true vine" (hē ampelos hē alēthine), deliberately contrasting himself with the false vine of Israel's covenant failure. The choice vine planted by YHWH in Jer 2:21 but producing wild growth is answered by Christ who is himself the true vine producing true fruit. John 15:5 ("I am the vine; you are the branches") completes the metaphor: the restoration of Israel-as-vine happens through union with Christ-as-vine.
2.22: "Even if you scrubbed yourself with lye and used much soap, the stain of your guilt would still be before me." The indelibility of moral guilt — no human cleansing agent removes the māʾôr ʿăwōnēḵ (stain of your iniquity) — is the diagnosis that only the cross answers. Christologically, Heb 9:13-14 contrasts the ritual purification of the Levitical system with the blood of Christ: "How much more will the blood of Christ... purify our conscience from dead works." 1 John 1:7 ("the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin") is the direct answer to Jer 2:22's impossible standard. The cleansing that lye and soap cannot achieve, Christ's atoning death accomplishes.
2.23: "How can you say, 'I am not defiled, I have not chased after the Baals'?" The self-justification that denies evident guilt — the animal imagery of the camel and wild donkey (vv23-24) developing the point — is the moral blindness that accompanies persistent sin. Christologically, this self-justification is what Jesus confronts in the Pharisees (Luke 18:9-14: the self-justifying Pharisee and the tax collector who knows his guilt) and in the rich young ruler (Matt 19:16-22: the one who claims to have kept all the commandments). The self-deception of Jer 2:23 is not merely Israel's problem but the universal human condition that the NT identifies as the prerequisite blindness to grace.
2.24: "Like a wild donkey at home in the desert, sniffing the wind in her craving — who can hold her back when she is in heat?" The wild donkey image for uncontrollable sexual desire applied to Israel's idolatry is a deliberately shocking metaphor for covenant unfaithfulness. The inability to hold back (yiśśĕrennāh, Qal imperfect, who can restrain her?) describes the compulsive quality of idolatrous desire. Christologically, Rom 1:24-27 describes exactly this compulsive, craving disorder as the consequence of idolatry: God "gave them up to dishonorable passions" as the judicial consequence of exchanging the Creator for the creature. The wild-donkey craving of Jer 2:24 is the OT image of what Paul diagnoses as the theological root of human sexual disorder.
2.25: "But you said: It is hopeless! I love other gods, and after them I will go." The declaration of hopeless addiction to false gods — "it is hopeless (nôʾāš)" — is the voice of entrenched covenant rebellion. The despair of the one who has abandoned YHWH and cannot conceive of return is the condition Paul describes in Eph 2:12 ("without hope and without God in the world"). Christologically, the gospel's answer to this despairing self-assessment is the hope offered in Christ: "You who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ" (Eph 2:13). The hopelessness of Jer 2:25 is answered not by human self-reform but by the divine initiative in the cross.
2.26: "As a thief is put to shame when caught, so the house of Israel will be shamed — they, their kings, their officials, their priests, and their prophets." The comprehensive shame covering all Israel's leadership is the diagnosis that makes the new covenant's comprehensive address necessary. Christologically, the shame (bōšet) that covers the entire community — every official level, every institution — is what Christ absorbs at the cross (Heb 12:2: "who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame"). The communal shame of Jer 2:26 is answered by Christ's personal assumption of shame, and his resurrection is the reversal of that shame into glory.
2.27: "They say to a tree, 'You are my father,' and to a stone, 'You gave me birth.' They have turned their backs to me and not their faces. Yet in the time of their trouble they will cry, 'Rise up and save us!'" The hypocrisy is complete: YHWH is addressed only in crisis, while trees and stones receive the paternal/natal devotion that belongs to him. The crisis-prayer to YHWH after devoted loyalty to idols is the pattern Jesus addresses in Matt 7:21-23 ("Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom"). The turning of backs in normal time and faces only in trouble is the OT form of the false discipleship Christ identifies as dangerous. The authentic relationship involves facing toward God not only in crisis but continuously — which only the indwelling Spirit of the new covenant enables (John 14:17).
2.28: "But where are the gods you made for yourself? Let them rise up, if they can rescue you in the time of your trouble." The challenge to idols to rise up (yāqûmû) and rescue is the theological polemic that frames the entire OAN tradition. Christologically, this challenge is answered by Christ's resurrection: the one YHWH raised from the dead does in fact save in the time of trouble. Acts 2:24 ("God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death") and Rom 10:9 ("if you... believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved") present the resurrection as the definitive answer to every "can your god rise up and save?" challenge in the OT. The idols cannot rise; Christ does.
2.29: "Why do you bring a complaint against me? You have all rebelled against me." YHWH as the defendant in Israel's counter-suit — she accuses YHWH of injustice even as she has rebelled — is the rhetorical posture of covenant inversion. Paul addresses the same counter-accusation in Rom 9:19-21 ("Why does he still find fault? Who can resist his will?") and in the diatribe of Rom 2-3. The answer in both Jeremiah and Paul is the same: the charge of divine injustice is groundless because the covenant violation was entirely human. Rom 3:4 ("Let God be true though every man be a liar") is the Pauline form of Jer 2:29.
2.30: "I have struck your people in vain; they accepted no correction. Your own sword has consumed your prophets like a ravaging lion." The killing of YHWH's prophets by Israel's own sword is the culminating indictment of v30 and is the pattern Jesus identifies as the characteristic sin of Jerusalem. Matt 23:37: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those who are sent to you!" — a direct citation of the Jeremianic pattern. Acts 7:52 (Stephen's speech) makes the same charge: "Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?" The sword that consumed the prophets in Jer 2:30 is the same sword that will be raised against Christ, the last and greatest of the sent prophets (and more than a prophet).
2.31: "O generation, take heed of the word of the LORD: Have I been a desert to Israel or a land of thick darkness? Why do my people say, 'We are free; we will come to you no more'?" The divine challenge — YHWH as the one who has provided, not withheld — uses the wilderness metaphor: has YHWH been midbār (desert, wilderness, deprivation) to Israel? The answer is no; YHWH has been the spring of living water (v13). Christologically, Jesus's claim "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst" (John 6:35) is the self-application of YHWH's challenge in Jer 2:31. The declaration "We will come to you no more" is the ultimate form of covenant rejection that finds its NT form in those who "went away" from Jesus (John 6:66).
2.32: "Does a young woman forget her jewelry, or a bride her wedding attire? Yet my people have forgotten me — for more days than can be counted." The absurdity of Israel's forgetfulness — more remarkable than a bride forgetting her wedding dress — is the rhetorical vehicle for the depth of the betrayal. Christologically, this contrast between bridal devotion and Israel's forgetfulness anticipates the church's calling to undivided devotion to Christ as the bride to her husband. Paul uses the bridal imagery in 2 Cor 11:2: "I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ." The church's forgetting of Christ would be the NT counterpart of Israel's forgetting — which is why Paul's pastoral letters constantly recall, remind, and re-present the gospel.
2.33: "How skilled you are at pursuing love; you have become expert enough to teach the wicked your ways." The ironic praise of Israel's expertise in pursuing false loves — she has become a teacher of wickedness — inverts the covenant calling to be a light to the nations (Isa 49:6). Instead of teaching the nations YHWH's ways, Israel has become a curriculum in unfaithfulness. Christologically, Christ reverses this irony: where Israel taught wickedness, Christ is himself the way, truth, and life that teaches the nations (Matt 28:20: "teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you"). The church's vocation to teach the nations is grounded in Christ's own teaching authority, not in human expertise.
2.34: "On your garments is found the lifeblood of innocent poor people — you did not catch them breaking in." The blood of innocent people — wrongfully shed outside of the self-defense exception of Exod 22:2-3 — is on Israel's hands. The shedding of innocent blood defiles the land (Num 35:33-34). Christologically, Matt 27:24-25 presents Pilate washing his hands of "innocent blood" (the only NT use of haima athōon, lit. "innocent blood") while the crowd accepts responsibility. Jesus is the innocent one whose blood is shed — but unlike the innocent poor of Jer 2:34, whose blood condemns those who shed it, Christ's innocent blood is the means of covenant cleansing (Heb 9:14; 1 John 1:7).
2.35: "Yet you say, 'I am innocent; surely his anger has turned from me.' But I will bring judgment against you because you say, 'I have not sinned.'" The refusal to acknowledge sin — the double self-justification ("I am innocent" and "I have not sinned") — is the state that makes repentance impossible. 1 John 1:8-10 directly addresses this: "If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves... If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins." The covenant lawsuit's requirement that the defendant acknowledge guilt is the OT form of the NT's requirement of confession for forgiveness. Christ's work makes forgiveness possible; the acknowledgment of sin makes it accessible.
2.36: "Why do you rush about so much, changing course? You will be disappointed by Egypt just as you were disappointed by Assyria." The political strategy of cycling through great-power alliances — Egypt to Assyria and back — is the foreign-policy form of spiritual unfaithfulness. Neither alliance saves; neither power proves reliable. Christologically, this disappointment by all human powers and alliances is the universal experience that drives the seeking of the divine king. Ps 146:3-5 ("Put not your trust in princes... blessed is he whose help is the God of Jacob") makes the theological application; Jesus's kingship is the one power that never disappoints (Rom 10:11: "everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame").
2.37: "You will leave Egypt too with your hands on your head in defeat, for the LORD has rejected the ones you are trusting." The hands-on-head gesture (yādêḵā ʿal-rōʾšēḵ) is the posture of total humiliation and defeat (2 Sam 13:19; Tamar's grief). YHWH rejects the objects of Israel's trust — the great empires she courted — because he is the only trustworthy power. Christologically, this rejection of false trust-objects is the theological ground of Christ's exclusive claim. John 14:6 ("No one comes to the Father except through me") is not merely soteriological exclusivism but a statement about the trustworthiness of all other refuges: hands on heads, all of them fail. Only Christ, rejected and crucified, is vindicated by the Father and established as the sole reliable foundation (1 Pet 2:6).

3.1: "If a man divorces his wife and she goes and marries another, can he take her back? Would not the land be totally defiled? But you have given yourself to many lovers — yet return to me." The Deuteronomic divorce law (Deut 24:1-4) forbids a divorced woman's return to her first husband after she has remarried. YHWH applies this law to his own situation with Israel — then overturns it with the unexpected divine call to return. Christologically, this is the OT statement of the gospel's scandalous grace: the law says return is impossible; grace says return is invited. The "yet return to me" is the OT form of the prodigal son's father running to meet the returning child (Luke 15:20). Christ himself embodies the divine overturning of the law's impossibility: his atoning work makes reconciliation possible where the law says it cannot happen. Eph 5:25-27 (Christ giving himself for the church "to present her... without spot or wrinkle") is the fulfillment of the divine return-invitation of Jer 3:1.
3.2: "Raise your eyes to the bare hilltops and look — is there any place you have not been violated?" The rhetorical survey of Judah's spiritual promiscuity — every hilltop a site of covenant violation — establishes the universality of the corruption. The image of violation at every road and hilltop also evokes the vulnerability of those who travel without protection. Christologically, the comprehensive defilement of the land anticipates the comprehensive redemption Christ accomplishes — not a partial cleansing of some hilltops but a total renewal. 2 Cor 5:17 ("the old has gone, the new has come") and Rev 21:5 ("I am making all things new") are the eschatological fulfillment of the comprehensive spiritual cleansing that begins at the cross.
3.3: "Therefore the rain has been withheld, and the spring rain has not come; yet you have a prostitute's brazenness — you refuse to be ashamed." The ecological consequence of covenant unfaithfulness — withheld rain — reflects the Deuteronomic blessing-and-curse structure (Deut 28:12, 23-24). The brazenness (meṣaḥ ʾiššâ zônâ, prostitute's forehead) in refusing to be ashamed recalls Ezek 16:30 and the stubbornness diagnosed throughout the prophetic tradition. Christologically, the withheld rain — the absence of divine blessing as consequence of rebellion — is reversed in Christ: the pouring out of the Spirit at Pentecost (Acts 2:17: "I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh") is the restoring of the withheld blessing. The brazenness that prevented shame is overcome by the Spirit-given repentance that produces godly sorrow (2 Cor 7:10).
3.4: "Have you not just been calling out to me, 'My Father, you are my closest companion from my youth'?" The address to YHWH as Father (ʾāḇî) and guide-from-youth is a cry of covenant intimacy invoked at moments of need — but hollow when the daily devotion belongs to idols. Christologically, Christ teaches his disciples to pray "Our Father" (Matt 6:9) — but the Abba-address is only authentic when grounded in covenant relationship through the Spirit (Rom 8:15-16: "you have received the Spirit of adoption as sons, by whom we cry, 'Abba! Father!'"'). The hollow father-address of Jer 3:4 is contrasted with the genuine Spirit-prompted father-cry that is the mark of adoption in Christ.
3.5: "Will he be angry forever? Will he stay angry to the end? But see — you have spoken, and you have done all the evil you could." The question appeals to YHWH's covenantal patience — will his anger be permanent? — while the oracle simultaneously exposes the hypocrisy: the appeal to divine patience is made while continuing in evil. Christologically, this tension between appealing to divine patience and continuing in sin is precisely what Paul addresses in Rom 6:1 ("Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound? By no means!"). The appeal to long-suffering grace as license for continued sin is as old as Jer 3:5. Christ's atoning death is not an extension of license but a transformation of the will through the Spirit.
3.6: "Have you seen what faithless Israel has done? She has gone up to every high mountain and under every spreading tree and committed adultery there." The geographic description of Israel's apostasy — high mountains and spreading trees were the standard sites of Canaanite fertility cult worship — establishes the comparison between faithless Israel (northern kingdom) and unfaithful Judah. Christologically, the adulterous Israel/Judah comparison creates the backdrop for Christ's own use of "adulterous generation" language (Matt 12:39; 16:4; Mark 8:38). Jesus's contemporaries who seek signs rather than faithfulness are the inheritors of the Jeremianic pattern of covenant apostasy disguised as religious activity.
3.7: "I said, after she had done all this, 'Return to me,' but she did not return." The repeated divine call to return (šûḇî) that goes unheeded is the pattern of the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33-46): servant after servant is sent, all rejected, until finally the son is sent. The refusal to return in Jer 3:7 is the cumulative record of covenant rejection that culminates in the rejection of the Son himself. Jesus explicitly reads his own rejection as the climactic instance of this pattern (Matt 21:42: "The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone").
3.8: "I saw that because faithless Israel had committed adultery, I had sent her away and given her a certificate of divorce; yet her unfaithful sister Judah had no fear, but went and became a prostitute too." The historical sequence — Israel divorced, Judah observing and learning nothing — establishes covenant unfaithfulness as a teachable sin that spreads from the example of others. Christologically, this contagion of unfaithfulness is the opposite of the contagion of faithfulness Christ establishes. John 17:20-21 (Jesus praying for those who will believe through the disciples' word) describes the spread of covenant faithfulness through testimony — the positive antitype of Judah's learning of unfaithfulness from Israel.
3.9: "She defiled the land by the brazen ease of her prostitution, committing adultery with stones and wooden posts."
