The renaming of Tophet as the "valley of Slaughter" is a prophetic omen-name: the place of false sacrifice becomes the place of true judgment. Christologically, the NT's use of Gehenna (from Ben-hinnom) as the image of final judgment completes this renaming: the valley where Israel played at sacrifice (child-burning) becomes the symbolic site of genuine divine reckoning. Jesus's Gehenna-warnings are the fullest development of this Jeremianic renaming.
19.7: "I will empty out the plans of Judah and Jerusalem in this place." The verb bāqaq (to empty) is the same root as baqbûq (the flask of v1) — YHWH will empty out Judah's plans just as the flask will be emptied and then smashed. The wordplay (baqbûq/bāqaq) is the sign-act's own linguistic interpretation. Christologically, the emptying of human plans before divine judgment mirrors the emptying of Christ at the incarnation (Phil 2:7: ekenōsen, "he emptied himself") — except that Christ's self-emptying is voluntary and redemptive, while Judah's emptying is involuntary and penal.
19.8: "I will make this city a scene of horror and contempt; all who pass by it will be horrified and will hiss in scorn." The formula of horror and hissing recurs from 18:16. Christologically, the passersby who mock the ruined city (Lam 1:12: "Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by?") are a type of the passersby who mock the crucified Christ (Matt 27:39: "those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads"). The lament formula of the destroyed city becomes the lament formula of the suffering servant.
19.9: "I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters." The siege-cannibalism announced as the extreme of covenant curse (Deut 28:53-57; Lam 4:10) is the most shocking element of the oracle. Christologically, this extreme physical curse is the negative context that makes Jesus's eucharistic language scandalous and profound in John 6:53-56: "Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you." Where covenant violation produces the horror of literal cannibalism in the siege, the new covenant produces the mystery of eucharistic communion — feeding on the life of Christ rather than destroying one another's lives.
19.10: "Then break the flask in front of the men who have come with you." The performative destruction of the flask before witnesses is a legal and prophetic act — a sign-act that enacts the word it illustrates. Christologically, the public nature of the sign-act ("in front of the men") anticipates the public nature of the cross: Christ was crucified openly, "publicly portrayed as crucified" (Gal 3:1), not in private. The sign-act performed before witnesses enacts the prophetic word; the cross enacted before witnesses fulfills the prophetic word.
19.11: "I will smash this people and this city just as one smashes a potter's vessel, so that it can never be repaired again." The irreparability (lōʾ yûḵal lĕhērāpēʾ ʿôd, it cannot be repaired again) is the key contrast with ch18: the unfired clay can be remade; the fired and broken flask cannot. Christologically, this irreparability is the language of eschatological judgment (Rev 2:27: "as when earthen pots are broken in pieces"; Rev 19:15: Jesus smashes the nations with a rod of iron). The broken flask that cannot be repaired is the OT type of the final judgment that cannot be reversed.
19.12: "This is what I will do to this place and its inhabitants... I will make this whole city like Tophet." The scope extends from the specific site (Tophet) to the whole city — the sign-act becomes a comprehensive oracle. Christologically, the expansion from a specific place to the whole city mirrors the NT's expansion of judgment and salvation from specific to universal: Christ's death at a specific place (Golgotha, outside Jerusalem) becomes the definitive event for the whole world (John 12:32: "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself").
19.13: "The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah will be defiled just like Tophet — all the houses on whose rooftops they burned incense to all the starry host of heaven." The rooftop incense to the host of heaven (astral worship) is the Babylonian-influenced cult that penetrated even the royal houses. Christologically, this worship of created cosmic powers (stars and astral deities) is precisely what Paul addresses as the enslaved state from which Christ liberates (Gal 4:3, 9: "weak and worthless elementary principles... enslaved to those that by nature are not gods"; Col 2:8, 20). The cosmic powers worshiped on Jerusalem's rooftops are the OT form of the "elemental spirits" that Christ disarmed at the cross (Col 2:15).
19.14: "Jeremiah came back from Tophet, where the LORD had sent him to prophesy, and stood in the court of the LORD's temple." The prophetic return from the place of enacted judgment to the temple court — to speak the same word in the most sacred space — mirrors the pattern of prophetic courage that Christ embodies in his temple-cleansing (Matt 21:12-13; John 2:13-17). The prophet who proclaims judgment in the valley then moves to the temple; Jesus who drives out the money-changers in the temple uses the same prophetic-judgment vocabulary: "My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of thieves" — citing Jer 7:11.
19.15: "I am going to bring upon this city and all its surrounding towns every disaster I have announced against them, because they have stubbornly refused to listen to my words." The summary statement of irreversible judgment due to stubborn refusal is the final word of the Tophet oracle. Christologically, Jesus uses the same "stubbornly refused" language in his final lament over Jerusalem: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... How often would I have gathered your children together... and you were not willing!" (Matt 23:37). The accumulation of refused prophetic words reaches its climax in the refusal of the Word himself.

20.1: Pashhur son of Immer, the chief temple officer, represents the institutional religious establishment in its most direct confrontation with Jeremiah's prophetic ministry. The name Pashhur (possibly meaning "prosperity on every side") will be inverted into a curse-name (v3). Christologically, the religious official who arrests and beats the prophet prefigures Caiaphas and the Sanhedrin who arrest and try Jesus (Matt 26:57-67). In both cases the institutional religious authority violently opposes the one YHWH sent.
20.2: "Pashhur had Jeremiah the prophet beaten and put in the stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate." The beating (hikkâ) and public humiliation (stocks, maḥpeket) of the prophet for speaking YHWH's word is the OT pattern of prophetic suffering. Christologically, Jesus's beating and public humiliation at the hands of the religious authorities (Matt 27:26-31; John 19:1-3) is the fulfillment of this pattern. The prophet beaten for speaking the divine word becomes the type of the Son beaten for being the divine Word.
20.3: "The LORD has not named you Pashhur, but Magor-Missabib — Terror-on-Every-Side." The prophetic omen-name inversion: Pashhur's pleasant name is replaced by the terror-judgment name that Jeremiah himself has been applying to Judah (6:25; 46:5). Christologically, the naming of the persecutor with the judgment-name he sought to silence recalls Jesus's own naming of opponents: "Brood of vipers" (Matt 23:33), "Whitewashed tombs" (Matt 23:27) — the one who is persecuted has the authority to rename his persecutors with the truth of their condition.
20.4: "I am going to make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends." The oracle against Pashhur turns the institutional persecutor's own security against him — the very power-structure he defended will be the instrument of his destruction. Christologically, this reversal anticipates the pattern of Acts: the Sanhedrin that condemned Jesus becomes the institution that loses its own members to the gospel (Acts 6:7: "a great many of the priests became obedient to the faith"). The persecutors of the prophet become, in many cases, recipients of the very word they tried to silence.
20.5: "I will also hand over all the wealth of this city... to their enemies." The comprehensive confiscation of Jerusalem's treasures to Babylon is the covenant-curse consequence of institutional unfaithfulness. Christologically, the stripping of institutional wealth and prestige echoes Jesus's prediction of Jerusalem's complete desolation (Matt 24:2: "there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down"). The wealth that the temple establishment tried to protect by killing the prophet is precisely what is lost when the prophet is silenced.
20.6: "And you, Pashhur, with everyone in your household, will go into exile. You will go to Babylon, and there you will die." The personal exile-oracle against Pashhur extends to his entire household and concludes with death in exile — which is also the fate of the false prophets he supported. Christologically, the judgment pronounced on the persecutor is the OT form of Jesus's judgment-word against those who kill the prophets: "Truly, I say to you, all these things will come upon this generation" (Matt 23:36). Condemnation follows the killers of prophets across generations.
20.7: "O LORD, you deceived me, and I let myself be deceived; you overpowered me and you won." The Sixth Confession opens with the most extreme accusation in the Psalter/Prophetic tradition against YHWH — the language of seduction/deception (pittîtanî, Piel of pātâ, used for erotic seduction in Exod 22:16; Judg 14:15). Jeremiah accuses YHWH of luring him into the prophetic office and then leaving him to suffer its consequences. Christologically, this raw prophetic cry of abandonment is the OT form of the cry of dereliction: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46; Ps 22:1). Jesus takes this cry further — not mere accusation but the actual experience of divine abandonment that Jeremiah only feared. Heb 5:7-8 ("in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears") applies this confessional pattern to Christ's passion prayer.
20.8: "Every time I speak, I have to cry out — 'Violence! Destruction!' — and the word of the LORD has become for me nothing but reproach and mockery all day long." The word of YHWH that Jeremiah cannot escape becomes the source of his reproach. Christologically, John 1:11 ("he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him") and Heb 13:13 ("let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured") identify Christ as the supreme bearer of reproach for speaking and being the divine word. The ḥerpâ (reproach, mockery) of v8 is the same ḥerpâ that Ps 69:7-9, 20 identifies as the central suffering of the righteous sufferer — cited in the NT passion tradition (Rom 15:3; John 2:17).
20.9: "When I think, 'I will not mention him, I will speak in his name no more,' then there is in my heart something like a burning fire shut up in my bones." The burning-fire-in-the-bones is the most vivid description of prophetic compulsion in the OT — the word of YHWH is not an option Jeremiah can refuse, even when it costs him everything. Christologically, Heb 12:1-2 ("looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame") describes Christ's own movement through suffering by means of a fixed purpose that could not be extinguished. Jesus's statement in Luke 12:49 ("I came to cast fire on the earth, and would that it were already kindled!") echoes the burning-fire compulsion — the mission could not be held back. John 4:34 ("My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work") expresses the same irresistible forward movement.
20.10: "For I hear many people whispering, 'Terror on every side! Report him — let us report him!' All my trusted friends are waiting for me to slip." The betrayal by trusted friends (ʾănšê šĕlōmî, lit. men of my peace) anticipates Ps 41:9 ("even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me") — cited by Jesus in John 13:18 for Judas's betrayal. The pattern of betrayal by trusted friends in Jer 20:10 is the same pattern that Jesus identifies as the specific form his betrayal will take. The intimate betrayal is the darkest dimension of prophetic suffering, and Christ's experience of it is the fulfillment of both Jeremiah's and the psalmist's anticipation.
20.11: "But the LORD is with me like a mighty and terrible warrior; therefore my persecutors will stumble and not prevail." The divine warrior (gibbôr ʿārîṣ, mighty terrible one) who is Jeremiah's champion is the positive anchor amid the Confession's anguish. Christologically, this confident assertion of divine vindication — in the middle of the deepest prophetic suffering — anticipates Phil 2:9-11 ("therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name"). The divine warrior who vindicates Jeremiah against his persecutors is the Father who vindicates Christ against death itself. The persecutors who stumble and do not prevail are the precise language of the resurrection's reversal.
20.12: "O LORD of hosts, who tests the righteous and sees the innermost being — the heart and mind — let me see your vengeance on them, for to you I have committed my case." The appeal to YHWH as the one who sees the klāyôt wālēḇ (kidneys/heart, the innermost self) is the appeal to the divine judge who sees what human courts cannot. Christologically, Rev 2:23 attributes this same omniscient inner-seeing to the risen Christ: "I am he who searches mind and heart" — Jesus takes YHWH's prerogative of omniscient judgment as his own. The case committed to YHWH in Jer 20:12 is the case committed to the Father by Christ in Luke 23:46 ("Father, into your hands I commit my spirit").
20.13: "Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD! For he has rescued the life of the poor from the hand of evildoers." The sudden eruption of praise — after the depths of vv7-12 — is the structural pattern of the Individual Lament psalms (Ps 22: anguish → confidence → praise; Ps 69: complaint → trust → praise). Christologically, this movement from dereliction to praise is the passion-resurrection pattern: the cry of abandonment (v7 = Matt 27:46) gives way to the praise of vindication (Heb 2:12: "I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise" — citing Ps 22:22, itself the praise-section of the lament that begins with the cry of dereliction). The "rescued the life of the poor" in v13 anticipates Acts 2:32 ("God raised this Jesus").
20.14: "Cursed be the day I was born!" The sudden reversal from praise (v13) to self-curse (vv14-18) is the most psychologically stark transition in Jeremiah. The birth-curse parallels Job 3:3-10 (Job's identical curse of his birth-day). Christologically, the wish that one had never been born — that the incarnation/birth had never happened — is the OT extreme of prophetic despair. But Christ's response to his own suffering is the exact inverse of the birth-curse: in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39) he says "not as I will, but as you will" — accepting the cup rather than cursing the day. The birth-curse of Jeremiah is the OT extreme that Christ's Gethsemane prayer refuses.
20.15: "Cursed be the man who brought the news to my father — 'A son has been born to you!' — and made him so happy." The imprecation extends to the messenger of birth-joy — the very announcement of the prophet's birth is now cursed. Christologically, the contrast with the annunciations of the NT is total: the angel announces Jesus's birth with "Fear not, for behold, I bring you good news of great joy" (Luke 2:10) — precisely the birth-joy that Jeremiah curses. The cursed birth-announcement in Jeremiah is the OT shadow of the blessed birth-announcement in Luke 2. Christ's birth inaugurates the joy that Jeremiah's birth led only to suffering.
20.16: "Let that man be like the cities the LORD destroyed without pity; let him hear a cry of distress in the morning and a battle alarm at noon." The curse invokes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah ("the cities the LORD destroyed without pity"). Christologically, Jesus himself uses Sodom as the standard of judgment (Matt 10:15; 11:23-24) — the most extreme OT image of divine wrath without pity. The fact that Jeremiah appeals to Sodom-level destruction as the standard for his personal distress measures the depths of his prophetic suffering.
20.17: "Because he did not kill me in the womb, so that my mother would have been my grave." The wish for pre-birth death — that the womb had been a tomb — is the ultimate withdrawal of consent from the covenant mission. This is the anti-life cry that stands as the OT's darkest moment of prophetic despair. Christologically, it is also the most precise foil for the incarnation: Christ entered the womb voluntarily (Heb 10:5-7: "a body you have prepared for me... I have come to do your will, O God"). Where Jeremiah wishes the womb had been his tomb, Christ accepted the womb as his path to the tomb — and through the tomb to resurrection.
20.18: "Why did I ever come out of the womb? All I have known is trouble and sorrow, and my life will end in disgrace." The closing cry of the Sixth Confession — unanswered, unresolved, without a restoration-word — is the most open-ended of the Confessions. It does not end in praise (contrast v13). Christologically, the unanswered cry is the OT type of Holy Saturday — the silence between the cry of dereliction (Good Friday) and the resurrection announcement (Easter Sunday). The Confessions' refusal to resolve this cry on its own terms is itself the theological statement that only the resurrection can answer what v18 asks.

21.1: "This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD when King Zedekiah sent Pashhur son of Malkijah and the priest Zephaniah son of Maaseiah to him." The royal delegation to Jeremiah at the crisis point of the Babylonian siege (588 BCE) marks the transition from the Confessions (chs 11-20) to the royal oracles (chs 21-24). The same establishment that persecuted Jeremiah now seeks his prophetic counsel. Christologically, this pattern of seeking the prophet's word in crisis after rejecting it in peace is the pattern Jesus identifies: "You will not see me until you say, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord'" (Matt 23:39) — the recognition that comes too late.
21.2: "Please seek a word from the LORD for us, because Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is attacking us. Perhaps the LORD will do something miraculous for us." Zedekiah's request invokes the môpēt (miracle, wonder) pattern of the exodus — "perhaps YHWH will do one of his wonders" — in hopes of a Hezekiah-style deliverance (2 Kings 19:35). Christologically, this demand for a miraculous sign in crisis is the pattern Jesus refuses: "An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign" (Matt 12:39). The miracle requested is the wrong question; the right question is whether the asker has responded to the prophetic word that came in peace.
21.3: "Jeremiah answered them: Tell Zedekiah this" — the prophet's response is not the hoped-for assurance but the word of judgment. The prophetic daring required to deliver an oracle of doom to the besieged king mirrors the boldness Christ displays before Pilate (John 18:37: "You say that I am a king. For this purpose I was born and for this purpose I have come into the world — to bear witness to the truth"). Both Jeremiah before Zedekiah and Jesus before Pilate speak an uncomfortable truth rather than a comfortable falsehood.
21.4: "I am going to turn back the weapons in your hands — the very weapons with which you are fighting the king of Babylon." The grotesque reversal: YHWH will redirect Judah's own weapons against herself. The military equipment meant for defense becomes the instrument of self-destruction through divine sovereign redirection. Christologically, the cross enacts the same reversal: the powers that executed Jesus used the instruments of death (the cross) only to find that the very death they inflicted became the instrument of their own defeat (Col 2:15: "He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him").
21.5: "And I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand and a mighty arm, in burning anger and fury and great wrath." The exodus formula inverted: the "outstretched arm and mighty hand" that delivered Israel from Egypt (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 26:8) is now turned against Israel. Christologically, the inverted-exodus formula is the OT form of divine wrath that Christ absorbs on behalf of the new covenant people. Gal 3:13 ("Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us") means that Christ takes the divine wrath that the inverted exodus formulas represent — so that for those in Christ, the formula is re-inverted: the mighty arm that was against us is now for us (Rom 8:31: "if God is for us, who can be against us?").
21.6: "I will strike down everyone living in this city — both people and animals — and they will die from a terrible plague." The covenant-plague vocabulary (deber) is the Deuteronomic curse-fulfillment (Deut 28:21-22). The comprehensive judgment — people and animals alike — echoes the totality of judgment in the exodus plagues. Christologically, the totality of judgment here is what Christ's atonement addresses: not partial sin-coverage but comprehensive bearing of the curse that extends through the entire created order (Rom 8:19-22: "the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption").
21.7: "After that... I will hand over Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, and those who survive... to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, to their enemies who seek their lives. He will put them to the sword without pity, without mercy, without compassion." The threefold negation — no pity, no mercy, no compassion — is the polar opposite of the divine character of Exod 34:6-7. Christologically, this absence of divine compassion in judgment is what Christ absorbs in his passion: the cross is the place where divine mercy and divine wrath meet, where pity, mercy, and compassion operate precisely by placing their cost on the innocent one.
21.8: "I am setting before you the way of life and the way of death." The Deuteronomic two-ways formula (Deut 30:15-19: "I set before you today life and prosperity, death and destruction") is applied to the immediate crisis: surrender to Babylon (counterintuitive life) or resist (apparent strength leading to death). Christologically, Jesus explicitly takes up the two-ways formula and applies it to himself: "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The Jeremianic "way of life" is an external path to follow; Christ IS the way, the life itself. John 10:10 ("I came that they may have life and have it abundantly") is the definitive statement of what the Deuteronomic and Jeremianic two-ways formula always pointed toward.
21.9: "Whoever stays in this city will die by the sword, famine, or plague; but whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans who besiege you will live — his life will be his prize." The counterintuitive way of life — surrendering to the enemy as the path to survival — requires trust in the prophetic word against all human instinct. Christologically, this is the pattern of the cross: "For whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will find it" (Matt 16:25). Surrendering to the Chaldean enemy to live is the OT type of surrendering oneself to the apparent defeat of the cross to gain resurrection life. The nefesh lĕšallāl (life as plunder/prize) of the one who surrenders is the OT image of the eternal life gained through faith in the crucified one.
21.10: "For I have fixed my gaze on this city for disaster and not for good, declares the LORD." The divine fixing of the gaze (śamtî pānay, I have set my face) for disaster is the reverse of the priestly blessing (Num 6:25: "the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you"). Christologically, the divine face set against Jerusalem is turned from its object and toward Christ at the cross — who receives the curse in himself so that the face of divine grace can turn toward those who were under judgment. Isa 53:3 ("he was despised... as one from whom men hide their faces") is the servant's experience of the hidden divine face that Jer 21:10 threatened.
21.11: "And to the house of the king of Judah, say: Hear the word of the LORD." The transitional verse addresses the Davidic royal house specifically. Christologically, the address to "the house of the king of Judah" is the address to the lineage from which Christ descends (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 1:32-33: "the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David"). The prophetic word to the Davidic house reaches its fulfillment in the one born in David's city (Luke 2:11) who is himself the definitive heir of David's throne.
21.12: "House of David! This is what the LORD says: Administer justice every morning; rescue from the oppressor those who have been robbed, or my anger will break out like an unquenchable fire." The justice-standard addressed to the house of David — daily morning justice for the oppressed — is the covenant calling of the Davidic king. Christologically, this is the Davidic standard that only Christ fully meets: he is the one who "proclaims justice to the nations" (Matt 12:18-21, citing Isa 42:1-4), who "has been anointed... to proclaim good news to the poor, to set at liberty those who are oppressed" (Luke 4:18, citing Isa 61:1-2). Jesus's morning teaching in the temple (Luke 21:37-38: "every day he was teaching in the temple") is the fulfillment of the "every morning" standard of Davidic justice.
21.13: "I am against you, Jerusalem — you who sit enthroned in the valley, on your rocky plateau... who say, 'Who can attack us? Who can breach our strongholds?'" The pride of Jerusalem — trusting in her geographic and architectural defenses — is the self-confidence that YHWH opposes. Christologically, this is the form of institutional pride that Jesus confronts in his temple-cleansing and in his prediction of the temple's destruction (Matt 24:1-2). The "Who can breach our strongholds?" boast of the Jerusalem establishment in Jeremiah's day is the same boast implicit in the claim that the temple and its institutions are inviolable — a claim that both Jeremiah and Jesus reject.
21.14: "I will punish you as your deeds deserve, declares the LORD. I will set fire to your forest — and it will burn up everything around you." The concluding judgment — fire consuming the palace-forest (yaʿar may refer to the House of the Forest of Lebanon, the royal armoury of 1 Kings 7:2) — is the OT form of the judicial fire that consumes what does not endure. Christologically, 1 Cor 3:13-15 uses the same "fire" imagery for the testing of human work on the day of judgment. But more immediately, the fire that burns Jerusalem's palace-forest is the fire that Jesus says he came to cast on the earth (Luke 12:49) — the purifying, consuming fire of divine judgment that the mission of Christ brings and that is the ultimate grounds for both the cross (where Christ absorbs the fire) and the final judgment (where all things are tested by fire).

14.1: The word concerning the drought — habatstsoret — opens a lament cycle where the land itself is dying for lack of rain. The drought is covenant curse made visible (Deut 28:22-24). In the New Testament, Jesus presents himself as the resolution to every drought of the soul: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink" (John 7:37). The physical drought of Jeremiah 14 finds its ultimate answer in Christ, who is the spring of living water that will never fail (John 4:14).
14.2: The gates of Judah languish and the city laments — a picture of communal spiritual death that the prophets consistently associate with covenant rupture. The NT uses the city-of-mourning image to describe the spiritual condition before the gospel (Eph 2:12: "without hope and without God in the world"). Christ entering Jerusalem on Palm Sunday — where the city did not recognize its hour of visitation — is the inverse of this mourning (Luke 19:41-44).
14.3: The servants sent for water return with empty vessels, ashamed. This material drought signifies the deeper spiritual thirst that no human resource can satisfy. The woman at the well in John 4 comes to draw water with an empty vessel; Jesus redirects her to the water he gives, which permanently resolves thirst. The shame of the empty jar is replaced by the testimony of the woman who leaves her jar behind because she has found the true source.
14.4: The farmers are dismayed and cover their heads because there is no rain. In ANE thought, rain was the sign of divine favor; its absence was divine withdrawal. The prophet Amos (4:7-8) describes YHWH withholding rain as a form of covenant discipline. Christ fulfills the promise of the latter rain (Joel 2:23; Zech 10:1) — the outpouring of the Spirit (Acts 2:17-18) that satisfies the drought of the soul and brings the harvest of the kingdom.
14.5: Even the doe in the field forsakes her newborn fawn because there is no grass — the drought is so severe that maternal instinct fails. This image of broken natural bonds under covenant curse contrasts with Christ as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) who does not forsake his own even in the worst conditions. Where the doe abandons her young, Christ lays down his life for his sheep.
14.6: The wild donkeys stand on the bare heights, panting for air like jackals — creatures of the desert, adapted to scarcity, now gasping even in their habitat. The image of desperate panting for breath recalls Psalm 42:1 ("As a deer pants for water, so my soul pants for you, O God"). The NT answer is the Spirit as breath: in John 20:22, Jesus breathes on the disciples and gives them the Holy Spirit — the divine answer to every gasping creature.
14.7: The community's confession — our iniquities testify against us; act for your name's sake — acknowledges that the drought is deserved. The appeal is to YHWH's name, not Israel's merit. This is precisely the gospel logic: Christ acts not on the basis of human merit but for the sake of the Father's name and glory (John 12:28; 17:1). The prayer for divine action despite human failure is answered in the incarnation — God acting for his own name's sake through Christ's atonement.
14.8: The title hope of Israel, Savior in time of trouble — miqweh Yisraʾel moshiʿo beet tsarah — is a messianic divine title. Moshiʿa (Savior) becomes the basis for the name Jesus (Yeshua, "YHWH saves") in Matthew 1:21: "you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins." The one addressed as "Savior in time of trouble" in Jeremiah becomes incarnate as the one whose very name embodies that saving function.
14.9: The cry — why are you like a man confused, like a mighty man who cannot save? — expresses the community's anguished sense of divine absence. Yet YHWH's answer to this cry is the incarnation itself: God does not remain at a distance like a confused bystander but enters human history in Christ. The "mighty man who cannot save" becomes the Mighty God of Isaiah 9:6, who saves with a strength that surpasses human expectation.
14.10: YHWH's refusal to accept the unfaithful people — he will now remember their iniquity and punish their sins — establishes that sin cannot be overlooked. This is the condition that makes propitiation necessary. Paul's argument in Romans 3:25-26 is precisely that the cross demonstrates YHWH's justice: God had "passed over former sins" in patience; in Christ he demonstrates his righteousness as the just and justifying one. The judgment pronounced here is the judgment borne by Christ.
14.11: YHWH's command to Jeremiah — do not pray for this people's good — represents a prophetic impossibility: intercessory prayer blocked. Jeremiah's intercession is refused because the covenant has been so thoroughly violated (cf. 7:16; 11:14). This prepares theologically for the unique intercessory work of Christ, whose prayers are always heard (John 11:42; Heb 7:25): where Jeremiah's intercession is refused, Christ's intercession is eternally effective. He is the intercessor who cannot be turned away.
14.12: The triple refusal — I will not hear when they fast, I will not accept their burnt offerings and grain offerings — mirrors the post-Temple situation where the old sacrificial system is permanently discontinued. The author of Hebrews develops this logic: the old sacrifices were repeated precisely because they could not finally accomplish what they represented (Heb 10:1-4). Christ's one sacrifice does what all those offerings could not: it actually purges the conscience and accomplishes what the fasting and offerings only pointed toward.
14.13: Jeremiah's complaint about the false prophets who say: no sword or famine will come upon you — the peace-prophets who contradict genuine divine warning — anticipates Jesus's warning about false prophets who come in his name saying "peace, peace" (Matt 24:11, 24). The criterion for distinguishing true from false prophecy — does it align with the reality of human sin and the demand for genuine repentance? — is consistently the test from Jeremiah to the NT.
14.14: The false prophets are prophesying falsehood, divination, worthless things, the deceit of their own minds — not sent by YHWH. Jesus's warning about false Christs and prophets (Matt 24:24) applies the same criterion: self-generated religious reassurance versus genuine word from God. The NT test — "by their fruits you will know them" (Matt 7:16) — matches Jeremiah's test (does the word align with the actual covenant reality?).
14.15: The false prophets who say "no sword or famine will reach this land" will themselves die by sword and famine — they experience the reality they denied. The principle of receiving what one proclaimed falsely is a form of the measure-for-measure justice that runs through scripture. Jesus pronounces a similar woe on the scribes and Pharisees who "shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces" (Matt 23:13) — those who block others from truth are themselves shut out.
14.16: The people who accepted the false prophecies being thrown out in the streets of Jerusalem, their corpses without burial — the ultimate covenant shame — represents the rejection of those who preferred comfortable lies over demanding truth. Jesus's warning in Matthew 7:21-23 — many will say "Lord, Lord" but be told "I never knew you" — applies to those who received false assurance without genuine repentance. The self-inflicted consequences of false religion have eschatological weight.
14.17: The virgin daughter of my people struck down with a crushing blow — YHWH weeping over the broken nation — prefigures Christ's weeping over Jerusalem: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets... How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!" (Matt 23:37; Luke 19:41-44). The divine weeping over the judgment that must come is one of the deepest continuities between Jeremiah and the passion narratives.
14.18: The land filled with slain by sword and famine, with both prophet and priest going about not knowing what to do — the complete collapse of prophetic and priestly leadership. Into this vacancy, Christ comes as the prophet who speaks with authority (Matt 7:29), the priest who makes atonement (Heb 4:14), and the king who governs righteously. The triple office of prophet, priest, and king — all in crisis in Jeremiah — is perfectly and permanently filled by one person.
14.19: The question have you utterly rejected Judah? Does your soul loathe Zion? Why have you struck us down so that there is no healing for us? is one of the most anguished questions in scripture. Paul addresses this directly in Romans 11:1: "Has God rejected his people? By no means!" The healing that was denied in Jeremiah's time is accomplished in Christ's atonement — the word marpeʾ (healing/cure) that echoes the "balm in Gilead" question of 8:22 finds its fulfillment in the Wounded Healer.
14.20: The confession — we acknowledge our wickedness, YHWH, and the iniquity of our fathers — is the pattern of genuine repentance that the NT requires as the precondition for receiving grace. The acknowledgment of ancestral sin as well as personal sin parallels the NT understanding that humanity participates in corporate solidarity: Paul's "all have sinned" (Rom 3:23) encompasses both personal and inherited guilt. Christ's atonement addresses both dimensions: the guilt accumulated across generations and the sins of the individual.
14.21: The appeal — do not spurn us, for your name's sake; do not dishonor your glorious throne; remember and do not break your covenant with us — grounds the prayer in covenant faithfulness rather than human merit. This is the basis for NT confidence before God: not our merit but Christ's covenant faithfulness. The "glorious throne" (kisse' kevod) finds its NT fulfillment in the throne of grace before which believers may approach boldly (Heb 4:16).
14.22: The closing confession — can any of the false gods of the nations bring rain? Can the skies themselves send showers? Are you not the one, O YHWH our God? — is a declaration of exclusive YHWH-dependence. It frames all creation as the arena of divine gift: rain, life, all good things come from YHWH alone. The NT parallel is James 1:17 ("every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights") — and ultimately Christ as the one through whom all things come (John 1:3; 1 Cor 8:6).

15.1: YHWH's declaration that even Moses and Samuel standing before him could not turn away the judgment establishes that no human intercessor is sufficient for the crisis. Moses, the greatest intercessor of the Torah (Exod 32:11-14; Num 14:13-20), and Samuel, the quintessential prophet-intercessor (1 Sam 7:5-9; 12:23), are both declared insufficient. The NT answer is found in Hebrews 7:25: Christ "always lives to make intercession" for us — he is the intercessor who succeeds where all others fail, because his intercession is based on his completed atoning work.
15.2: The four fates — those destined for death, to death; plague, to plague; sword, to sword; exile, to exile — map onto the four covenant curses. The early church reading of Christ's death understood him as taking upon himself all four fates simultaneously: dying the covenant death, bearing the disease of sin, suffering the sword of judgment (John 18:11), and experiencing exile from the Father's presence (Matt 27:46). The comprehensive scope of judgment becomes the comprehensive scope of redemption.
15.3: The four kinds of destroyers — sword to kill, dogs to drag away, birds of the heavens to devour, beasts of the earth to destroy — are the instruments of divine judgment through Israel's history and ultimately through the fall of Jerusalem. The author of Revelation draws on this imagery in describing the eschatological judgment (Rev 6:8). In Christ's death, this judgment is absorbed and its power broken; the one who bore the curse (Gal 3:13) sets free those who were under it.
15.4: The reference to Manasseh son of Hezekiah, king of Judah as the precipitating cause of the judgment — his idolatries making Judah an object of horror to all kingdoms — shows how one leader's faithlessness can condemn a community across generations. The NT inverts this: where Adam's one sin brought condemnation to all, Christ's one act of righteousness brings justification for all (Rom 5:18-19). The devastating power of solidarity in sin finds its redemptive counterpart in solidarity in Christ.
15.5: The rhetorical question — who will have pity on you, Jerusalem? Who will mourn for you? Who will stop to ask how you are? — assumes a "no one." In Christ, the answer is dramatically reversed: Jesus "had compassion on the crowds" repeatedly (Matt 9:36; 14:14; Mark 6:34); he wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41). The one who stops to ask how Jerusalem is, who mourns over her, who has pity when no one else does — this is Jesus, fulfilling what YHWH alone could fulfill in Jeremiah's lament.
15.6: YHWH's declaration — you have rejected me; you keep going backward, so I have stretched out my hand against you and destroyed you — describes the divine response to persistent rejection. The NT warns that persistent rejection of Christ leads to hardening (Heb 3:7-8; 10:26-29). But the same chapter of Hebrews (3:13) urges the community to exhort one another daily so that none is hardened — the gospel creates a community of mutual accountability that resists the backward-going pattern condemned here.
15.7: The winnowing with a winnowing fork in the gates of the land — separating grain from chaff in the place of judgment — is the image John the Baptist applies directly to Jesus: "His winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor, gathering his wheat into the barn and burning the chaff with unquenchable fire" (Matt 3:12). The eschatological winnowing judgment that Jeremiah announces as imminent becomes the final judgment that Christ executes at the end of the age.
15.8: The widows made more numerous than the sand of the sea by the sword — the community decimated by divine judgment — creates the mass vulnerability that the covenant community was supposed to protect (Deut 14:29; 24:17). Jesus consistently identifies himself with the vulnerable: "I was hungry and you gave me food, I was a stranger and you welcomed me" (Matt 25:35). The widow (Luke 7:11-15; 18:1-8; 21:1-4) is a figure Jesus specifically honors and defends, reversing the abandonment described here.
15.9: The mother of seven growing pale, languishing — her sun going down while it is still day — is an image of premature death and hope extinguished. The seven-child mother represented maximum blessing in Israel; her bereavement represents total covenant loss. The NT answer is the resurrection: Christ's rising from the dead at the third hour reverses the "sun going down at noon" (Amos 8:9) — the darkness that fell at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45) is overcome by the dawn of Easter.
15.10: Jeremiah's personal lament — woe is me, my mother, that you bore me, a man of strife and contention to the whole land — is one of the most Christ-typological verses in the OT. The prophet born to be a man of strife, rejected by his own people, suffering without deserving it, is the pattern that culminates in Jesus: "he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him" (John 1:11). The "whole land" that contests with Jeremiah becomes the world that rejects the Son.
15.11: YHWH's assurance — surely I have intervened for your good (ʾim lo sheritikha letov) — is the divine word spoken to the suffering prophet that his suffering is purposeful. The same assurance underlies the NT theology of the cross: the suffering of Christ is not purposeless but divinely planned for our good ("for our benefit," 2 Cor 5:21). The divine assurance to the suffering prophet finds its ultimate form in the resurrection vindication of the crucified Son.
15.12: The question — can iron break northern iron and bronze? — speaks to YHWH's absolute authority over history. The invincibility of the divine decree is the theological basis for Paul's confidence in Romans 8:31-39: "if God is for us, who can be against us?" The iron that cannot be broken is ultimately Christ's lordship — his kingdom that will not be shaken (Heb 12:28), against which even the gates of Hades will not prevail (Matt 16:18).
15.13: The threat of handing over Judah's wealth and treasures as plunder because of sin throughout all the territory — the covenant's economic curse (Deut 28:31-33) — contrasts with the NT promise of being "heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ" (Rom 8:17). Christ becomes poor so that we might become rich (2 Cor 8:9); the plundering that is judgment becomes the enrichment that is grace. The economic metaphor of blessing versus curse is consistently reappropriated in NT soteriology.
15.14: Being made to serve your enemies in a land you do not know — exilic servitude in a foreign land — is the condition that the new exodus reverses. Paul describes it theologically in Galatians 4:1-7: before Christ, believers were "enslaved to the elementary principles of the world"; through Christ's redemption they receive adoption as sons. The exile in a strange land becomes the condition from which Christ liberates, bringing his people into their inheritance.
15.15: Jeremiah's prayer — know that for your sake I bear reproach — grounds the prophet's suffering in divine calling: he suffers reproach because he carries YHWH's word. Paul applies this framework to apostolic suffering: "We are fools for Christ's sake" (1 Cor 4:10); "I bear on my body the marks of Jesus" (Gal 6:17). The apostolic community participates in the reproach-bearing that Jeremiah inaugurated and Christ perfected. The prayer for remembrance is answered in Christ's promise never to leave or forsake.
15.16: Your words were found and I ate them; your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart — the prophet feasting on the divine word. This is fulfilled in multiple NT directions: Jesus is the living Word made flesh (John 1:14); "Man shall not live by bread alone but by every word that comes from the mouth of God" (Matt 4:4 = Deut 8:3); in Revelation 10:9-10, John eats a scroll that tastes sweet as honey. The communion imagery — eating the word, the bread of life — grounds the Eucharist in this prophetic tradition of word-as-nourishment.
15.17: Jeremiah's sitting alone because your hand was upon me — isolated by the weight of prophetic calling — foreshadows Jesus's own chosen solitudes: the forty days of wilderness testing (Matt 4:1-2), the nights alone in prayer (Luke 6:12; Mark 1:35), the anguish of Gethsemane where the disciples could not watch with him (Matt 26:40-45). The prophet who sits alone because he is filled with YHWH's indignation anticipates the Son who bears the full weight of divine holiness in solitary anguish.
15.18: The most anguished confession — why is my pain unceasing, my wound incurable, refusing to be healed? Will you be to me like a deceptive brook, like waters that fail? — is the prophet's dark night of the soul, which finds its ultimate resolution only in Christ. Jesus, the one who cried "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46), transforms this question by passing through it and coming out the other side. The "deceptive brook that fails" becomes the "river of the water of life" flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb (Rev 22:1).
15.19: YHWH's call to Jeremiah to return and I will restore you — and then you will stand before me — is the pattern of prophetic restoration that the NT reads as a type of resurrection. The gospel movement from abandonment to return to restoration (shub, return/repent) is precisely the Easter trajectory: the disciples scattered at the cross return after the resurrection (Matt 28:16-17) and are restored to their calling (John 21:15-17, Peter restored three times). The prophetic restoration pattern is fulfilled in the apostolic renewal.
15.20: The divine promise — I will make you to this people a fortified wall of bronze — against which they will fight and not prevail, because YHWH is with him, echoes Christ's promise to Peter: "On this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it" (Matt 16:18). The fortified witness that cannot be overcome despite all opposition is the pattern of prophetic ministry from Jeremiah through the apostolic community. The criterion is divine presence, not human strength.
15.21: The assurance — I will deliver you from the hand of the wicked and redeem you from the grasp of the ruthless — is the covenant promise of divine protection that runs from the Exodus to the resurrection. The verb padah (redeem) is the ransom-language that Paul applies to Christ's work: "you were bought with a price" (1 Cor 6:20; 7:23). The prophet's deliverance from those who seek his life is a historical type of the ultimate deliverance Christ accomplishes from sin, death, and the power of the enemy.

16.1: YHWH's word comes to me — the divine word addressed to the individual prophet — in this chapter to command an extraordinary lifestyle of prophetic sign. The commands that follow (no marriage, no mourning, no feasting) mark Jeremiah as entirely consecrated to the proclamation of coming disaster. Jesus similarly identified himself as one whose life was wholly devoted to the Father's mission (John 4:34; 17:4), and Paul can describe himself as having given up ordinary domestic relationships for apostolic calling (1 Cor 7:7-8, 32-35).
16.2: The prohibition of taking a wife and having sons and daughters in this place — Jeremiah's celibacy as prophetic sign of the coming judgment where family life would be cut off — finds its NT resonance in Jesus's own celibate life entirely dedicated to the kingdom. Paul presents celibacy as a charismatic gift that enables undivided devotion to the Lord (1 Cor 7:32-35). The sign-value of celibacy in Jeremiah — pointing to catastrophe — is reinterpreted by Jesus and Paul as pointing to the eschatological kingdom.
16.3: The oracle about sons and daughters born in this place, their mothers who bore them, their fathers who begot them in this land dying terrible deaths — no mourning, no burial — describes the complete rupture of natural community. The NT answer to the rupture of natural community is the new family created by faith: Jesus declares that "whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother and sister and mother" (Matt 12:50). The natural family broken by judgment is replaced by the covenant family united in Christ.
16.4: The dead not being mourned or buried, becoming dung on the surface of the ground — the ultimate covenant curse — is the reverse of resurrection hope. The NT inverts this: Christ's resurrection is the guarantee that the dead will not remain unburied and unmourned. Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15:52-54 is that the final trumpet will reverse the judgment of death — the bodies condemned to lie like dung will be raised imperishable. The resurrection hope is the direct answer to this covenant curse.
16.5: The prohibition on entering a house of mourning or lamenting — YHWH having withdrawn his peace (shalom), steadfast love (ḥesed), and compassion from this people — marks a radical divine withdrawal. Jesus enters houses of mourning repeatedly and restores what was withdrawn: he enters Jairus's house of mourning and raises his daughter (Mark 5:38-42); he raises Lazarus from the dead (John 11); at Nain he interrupts a funeral procession (Luke 7:13-15). Where YHWH withdraws his peace from Jeremiah's generation, Jesus restores it.
16.6: The dead who cannot be mourned, buried, or lamented — the community denied the normal rituals of grief — represents social death on top of physical death. The early church's practice of burying its dead with dignity and hope (as attested in the NT and early Christian sources) was a direct expression of resurrection faith — a counter-testimony to the finality of death. Christ's own burial in a rich man's tomb (Isa 53:9; Matt 27:57-60) and the women's mourning presence (Matt 27:61; John 19:25) restore the dignity denied in this oracle.
16.7: No one will break bread for the mourner to comfort him for the dead; no cup of consolation will be offered — the ritual of community consolation suspended. The NT Lord's Supper is precisely the sharing of bread and cup in memory of the dead Christ (1 Cor 11:23-26), creating exactly the consolation that is denied here. The broken bread and the cup of the new covenant become the ultimate consolation — not just for the dead but through the death that conquers death.
16.8: The prohibition on entering the house of feasting, sitting with them to eat and drink — Jeremiah denied the pleasures of normal community life — reflects the prophet's complete separation from the condemned community's social life. Jesus takes the opposite approach: he ate and drank with sinners so consistently that he was accused of being "a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners" (Matt 11:19). The Good News permits Jesus to enter the feast; his presence transforms the table rather than avoiding it.
16.9: YHWH's declaration that the voice of mirth and gladness, the voice of bride and bridegroom will cease in this place — the suspension of the wedding feast — is the opposite of the eschatological promise. Christ's first miracle is at a wedding feast (John 2:1-11); he presents the kingdom itself as a wedding banquet (Matt 22:1-14; 25:1-13); the consummation of history is the marriage of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9). The silence of the wedding voice that Jeremiah announces is the negative that the gospel definitively reverses.
16.10: When the people ask why has YHWH pronounced all this great disaster against us? — the question of theodicy — the prophet is to explain the covenant cause. The NT addresses the same question of human suffering through the lens of the cross: God did not spare his own Son (Rom 8:32), entering the disaster himself to redeem those caught within it. The great disaster becomes the occasion for the greater grace — the theodicy resolved not by explanation but by divine self-giving.
16.11: The answer — your fathers have forsaken me and gone after other gods; they have not kept my law — traces the catastrophe to ancestral covenant violation. Paul's argument in Romans 1-3 gives this the fullest NT development: all humanity has exchanged the truth of God for idols (Rom 1:23-25); the covenant violation is universal, not just Israelite. The scope of the problem matches the scope of the solution: Christ comes for a world-wide covenant violation that requires a world-wide atonement.
16.12: You have behaved worse than your fathers — the escalation of sin across generations — mirrors the NT's diagnosis of humanity as unable to improve itself. Paul's argument that the law could not enable the righteousness it required (Rom 7:12-25; 8:3) parallels this: even with the Torah, the covenant people did worse than their predecessors. The new covenant solution (Jer 31:31-34) is not a new demand but a new divine act — writing the law on the heart through the Spirit (2 Cor 3:3-6).
16.13: The exile — I will hurl you out of this land into a land that neither you nor your fathers have known — becomes in the NT the image of spiritual alienation resolved in Christ. Paul uses exile language for the pre-Christian condition: "alienated from the commonwealth of Israel" (Eph 2:12); "you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ" (Eph 2:13). The exile from the land is also exile from God; Christ is the one who ends both by reconciling humanity to the Father.
16.14: The stunning promise — therefore the days are coming when it shall no longer be said, as YHWH lives who brought up the people of Israel out of Egypt — announces a new defining act of salvation greater than the Exodus. The NT explicitly identifies this greater act with Christ's redemption: Hebrews 3:1-6 presents Jesus as greater than Moses; Luke 9:31 uses the word exodos for Jesus's death and departure; Revelation 15:3 calls the redeemed the song of Moses and the Lamb. The new exodus is the redemption Christ accomplishes.
16.15: The new defining oath — "as YHWH lives who brought up Israel from the north country and all the countries" — envisions a gathering-from-all-nations greater than the Sinai generation. Jesus's command to make disciples of all nations (Matt 28:19) and the Pentecost gathering from every nation under heaven (Acts 2:5-11) inaugurate this worldwide gathering. The new exodus creates a new Israel drawn from every people — the eschatological fulfillment of this promise.
16.16: YHWH's declaration — I will send for many fishers and they will fish them; then I will send for many hunters and they will hunt them — becomes the foundation for Jesus's calling of fishermen disciples with the words "Follow me and I will make you fishers of men" (Matt 4:19; Mark 1:17). The divine mission of gathering Israel from the nations is delegated to the disciples-as-fishermen. The mission Jesus commissions directly fulfills this prophetic vision of worldwide divine fishing for scattered humanity.
16.17: YHWH's declaration — my eyes are on all their ways; they are not hidden from me, nor is their iniquity concealed from my eyes — is the divine omniscience claim. Christ exercises this same divine prerogative: "I am he who searches mind and heart, and I will give to each of you according to your works" (Rev 2:23). In John 2:24-25, Jesus "knew all people" and "knew what was in man." The one who searches hearts in Jeremiah is the one who searches hearts in the NT letters to the churches.
16.18: The double payment — I will doubly repay their iniquity and their sin, because they have polluted my land with the carcasses of their detestable idols — describes covenant justice. The NT reframes double payment christologically: Isaiah 40:2 ("she has received from YHWH's hand double for all her sins") is the context for the voice in the wilderness that precedes John the Baptist (Isa 40:3-5). The double payment is absorbed in Christ's atoning death, after which Paul can declare "there is therefore now no condemnation" (Rom 8:1).
16.19: The Gentile nations confessing — O YHWH, my strength and my stronghold, my refuge in the day of trouble; to you shall the nations come from the ends of the earth and say: our fathers have inherited nothing but lies — is the prophetic vision of worldwide Gentile repentance and recognition of YHWH. Paul quotes this trajectory in Romans 15:8-12, arguing that Christ has come to confirm the promises to the patriarchs and that the Gentiles glorify God for his mercy — the fulfillment of exactly this prophecy in the mission of the church.
16.20: The rhetorical question — can man make for himself gods? Such are not gods! — is the idol-polemic's punchline: the self-evident absurdity of human-manufactured divinity. Paul deploys this logic in Acts 17:29: "being God's offspring, we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man." The NT mission confronts both Jewish and Gentile idolatry with the same insight: the living God cannot be fashioned by human hands.
16.21: The promise — therefore I will make them know, this once I will make them know my power and my might, and they shall know that my name is YHWH — is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, the supreme demonstration of divine power and might. Paul grounds the gospel proclamation in "the power of God" (Rom 1:16) and prays that believers would know "the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead" (Eph 1:19-20). The revelation of YHWH's name and power is accomplished in the resurrection.

17.1: The sin of Judah written with an iron pen, engraved with a diamond point on the tablet of their heart — sin inscribed permanently in the heart — establishes the depth of the problem. The NT answer is the new covenant's counter-inscription: "you show that you are a letter from Christ delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Cor 3:3). What sin writes on the heart with iron, the Spirit rewrites with life.
17.2: The children remembering their altars and Asherah poles beside every green tree and on the high hills — idolatry taught and practiced across generations — shows the multigenerational transmission of false worship. Jesus addresses this pattern when he says "you nullify the word of God for the sake of your tradition" (Matt 15:6). The renewal of worship requires not just individual repentance but the breaking of inherited idolatrous patterns — the work the Spirit accomplishes in regenerating communities.
17.3: The wealth and all the treasures given as plunder, the high places stripped, because of sin throughout the territory — economic loss as covenant consequence — contrasts with Paul's vision of true wealth in Christ: "though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich" (2 Cor 8:9). The plundering of material wealth is answered by Christ's enrichment of those who receive him — spiritual wealth that cannot be confiscated.
17.4: The people letting go of their heritage, to serve enemies in a foreign land, because YHWH's anger has been kindled as a fire that will burn forever — the covenant wrath described as perpetual fire — is the context into which Christ's atonement speaks. The "eternal fire" motif (cf. Isa 66:24; Matt 25:41) is resolved by Christ's bearing of the covenant curse: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Gal 3:13). The fire of judgment is absorbed in the one who was made sin for us (2 Cor 5:21).
17.5: Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from YHWH — this verse is directly engaged in Paul's argument in Galatians 3:10-13. Paul cites Deuteronomy 27:26 (in a parallel argument) to show that all who rely on works of the law are under a curse; Christ has redeemed us from the curse. The "trust in flesh" that Jeremiah diagnoses as the root of covenant failure is precisely the religious self-reliance that Paul declares ineffective for righteousness.
17.6: The shrub in the desert who shall not see good when it comes — inhabiting the parched places, the salt land — is the image of the man-truster cut off from life and blessing. Paul develops the same contrast in Romans 8:1-11: those who live according to the flesh cannot please God; they are on the path toward spiritual death. The contrast with the tree planted by water in v. 8 is the contrast Paul draws between Spirit-life and flesh-life — the difference being not effort but orientation.
17.7: Blessed is the man who trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH — the double use of "trust" (batach) establishes that blessed life is defined entirely by the object of one's confidence. This is the OT statement of what faith means in the NT: "whoever believes in him will not be put to shame" (Rom 10:11, citing Isa 28:16). The beatitude structure ("blessed is...") connects directly to Jesus's Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12), which map the same terrain of who is truly blessed.
17.8: The tree planted by water, sending out its roots by the stream, not fearing when heat comes, bearing fruit even in drought — is the image of the fully flourishing covenant life. Jesus identifies himself as the true vine (John 15:1-11), and Paul's fruit-of-the-Spirit passage (Gal 5:22-23) describes the same kind of continuous fruitfulness that comes from abiding in Christ. Psalm 1:3 uses identical imagery; Jeremiah and the Psalter together establish that rootedness in YHWH is the source of perpetual fruitfulness.
17.9: The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick — who can understand it? — the most searching anthropological statement in the OT. Paul develops this across Romans 1-3: the heart darkened by idolatry (Rom 1:21-23), the conscience that witnesses but cannot save (Rom 2:15), the universal condemnation (Rom 3:10-18). The answer to the desperately sick heart is not moral effort but the new heart promised in Ezekiel 36:26 — a divine transplant, which Paul identifies as the Spirit's work (2 Cor 3:3; Gal 5:22-23).
17.10: I YHWH search the heart and test the mind, to give every man according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds — divine omniscience applied to judgment. Christ exercises this divine prerogative: "I am he who searches mind and heart, and I will give to each of you according to your works" (Rev 2:23, Jesus speaking to the seven churches). At the final judgment, Christ is the one who opens the books and examines every heart (2 Cor 5:10). The heart-searcher of Jeremiah is identified as Jesus in Revelation.
17.11: The partridge that hatches eggs it has not laid is the image of wealth gained unjustly — it will leave at the end; the unjust will be a fool. James 5:1-6 applies the same judgment against those who have defrauded workers of their wages. Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) makes the same point: the man who accumulates wealth for himself is not rich toward God. The temporary possession of unjust wealth versus true eternal treasure is a consistent NT theme grounded in this prophetic tradition.
17.12: A glorious throne set on high from the beginning is the place of our sanctuary — the heavenly throne as the true cultic center, contrasted with earthly temples. Hebrews develops this extensively: the earthly temple was a copy and shadow of the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 8:5); Christ has entered the true tent not made with hands (Heb 9:11-12); the throne of grace is where believers now approach boldly (Heb 4:16). The "glorious throne from the beginning" is the heavenly court where Christ is now seated (Heb 1:3).
17.13: O YHWH, the hope of Israel, all who forsake you will be put to shame; those who depart from you will be written in the earth, for they have forsaken the fountain of living water — combining the living-water image of 2:13 with the Book of Life image. Christ identifies himself as the fountain of living water (John 4:14; 7:37-38), and the Book of Life appears throughout Revelation (3:5; 13:8; 20:12, 15) as the record of those who belong to Christ. The writing-in-the-earth image resonates with John 8:6-8, where Jesus writes in the dust.
17.14: Heal me, O YHWH, and I will be healed; save me and I will be saved, for you are my praise — the prayer of total dependence on divine action, not human effort. The centurion in Matthew 8:8 echoes this trust: "only say the word and my servant will be healed." The woman with the flow of blood (Mark 5:34) is healed when she touches Jesus and is told "your faith has made you well" — the sōzein (save/heal) that Jeremiah invokes. The prayer-of-dependence structure is the pattern of all NT healing faith.
17.15: The mocking question — where is the word of YHWH? Let it come now! — is the scoffers' challenge to the prophet: prove your prophecy by its fulfillment. Peter addresses the same challenge in 2 Peter 3:3-4: "scoffers will come... saying, where is the promise of his coming?" The NT answer is that the apparent delay of fulfillment is divine patience (2 Pet 3:9), not prophetic failure. Jesus endured the same taunt on the cross: "let God deliver him now, if he desires him" (Matt 27:43).
17.16: Jeremiah's protestation — I have not run away from being your shepherd; I have not desired the day of disaster — you know what came from my lips; it was before your face — is the prophet's declaration of transparent integrity before God. The motif of the true shepherd who does not run — distinct from the false shepherds who flee — directly anticipates John 10:11-13: the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep versus the hired hand who abandons them. Jesus fulfills the true-shepherd ideal Jeremiah claims for himself.
17.17: The prayer — be not a terror to me; you are my refuge in the day of disaster — grounded in the confidence that YHWH, not the attacking enemy, is the prophet's ultimate shelter. Hebrews 13:6 applies this trust-pattern directly: "I will not fear; what can man do to me?" (citing Ps 118:6). The pattern of taking refuge in YHWH when surrounded by terrifying opposition is the pattern of early Christian witness under persecution — grounded in the same prophetic trust Jeremiah models.
17.18: The prayer for the persecutors to be put to shame, not the prophet — let double destruction come on them — is the prophetic imprecation that the NT handles carefully. Romans 12:17-21 instructs: "repay no one evil for evil... leave it to the wrath of God" — the vindication is real but belongs to YHWH, not to private revenge. Jesus on the cross fulfills this: he does not call down destruction on his persecutors but prays "Father, forgive them" (Luke 23:34), trusting the Father with ultimate vindication (1 Pet 2:23).
17.19: The gate of the people — the Benjamin Gate of Jerusalem — where Jeremiah is commanded to stand and proclaim the Sabbath oracle is the public place of maximum visibility. Jesus similarly teaches in the temple courts (Matt 21:23; John 8:2), choosing public spaces for proclamation rather than private instruction alone. The prophet standing at the gate is the prototype of the apostolic public proclamation — the gospel is not whispered in corners but proclaimed in the public square.
17.20: The address to kings, all Judah, all Jerusalem, and all who enter these gates — the full spectrum of society from highest to lowest — reflects the gospel's comprehensive scope. Paul's statement that the gospel is the power of God for salvation "for the Jew first and also for the Greek" (Rom 1:16) expresses the same non-discriminatory scope: king and commoner, Jew and Gentile, all are addressed by the same word. The prophetic proclamation to all social strata is the pattern of the apostolic mission.
17.21: The Sabbath command — take care for the sake of your lives, and do not carry any load on the Sabbath day — grounds covenant faithfulness in its most practical visible expression: Sabbath-keeping. Jesus declares himself Lord of the Sabbath (Matt 12:8; Mark 2:28), claiming authority over the institution that was the sign of the Sinai covenant. The Sabbath controversy in the gospels is about who defines the Sabbath's meaning — and Jesus's answer is that he himself is its fulfillment (Matt 11:28-30: "come to me and I will give you rest").
17.22: The command to not carry a load out of your houses on the Sabbath day and to do no work — to keep the Sabbath holy as YHWH commanded — frames the Sabbath as active covenant sign. Hebrews 4:1-11 reinterprets the Sabbath rest as a theological category: the rest God rested on the seventh day is a rest still remaining for the people of God; Jesus is the one through whom they enter that rest. The physical Sabbath obligation points to the eschatological rest inaugurated by Christ.
17.23: The indictment that they did not listen and did not incline their ear; they stiffened their neck not to hear and not to accept correction applies the standard covenant-refusal formula to the Sabbath specifically. Hebrews 3:7-8 quotes Psalm 95 ("do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion") as a warning to the Christian community precisely about this pattern. The stiffened neck that rejected covenant instruction is the posture that Christ's invitation overcomes: "learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls" (Matt 11:29).
17.24: The conditional promise — if you listen to me and carry no load through the gates of this city on the Sabbath — frames the entire Sabbath oracle as a covenant conditional: obedience opens blessing. Jesus reframes the conditionality radically: not "if you obey, I will bless" but "I have already accomplished everything; now rest in it" (John 19:30: "it is finished"). The conditional structure of the Torah Sabbath is transformed into the completed-work structure of the gospel Sabbath.
17.25: The promise of kings and princes sitting on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses if the Sabbath is kept — the continuation of Davidic rule as the reward for covenant faithfulness — finds its NT fulfillment in Christ. The one who sits on David's throne forever (Luke 1:32-33; Rev 5:5) is the one who perfectly kept the covenant — and through his keeping, opens the inheritance for all who are in him. The Davidic throne promise is unconditionally secured in Christ.
17.26: The gathering from every corner of Judah — from the cities of Judah, the environs of Jerusalem, the land of Benjamin, the lowlands, the hill country, the Negeb — bringing burnt offerings, sacrifices, and thanksgiving to the house of YHWH is the vision of eschatological worship. The NT fulfillment is the gathering of all nations to the one house of God: Revelation 7:9-12 shows the great multitude from every nation worshipping before the throne. The geographical gathering from all regions becomes the eschatological gathering from all peoples.
17.27: The reversal — if you do not listen... I will kindle a fire in its gates and it will devour the palaces of Jerusalem and will not be quenched — is the warning that was historically fulfilled in the Babylonian destruction. But the NT reads the unquenchable fire in dual fashion: the historical judgment on Jerusalem (fulfilled in 586 BC and again in AD 70) and the eschatological judgment (Matt 3:12; Rev 20:14-15). Between the historical fulfillment and the eschatological one stands the cross — Christ bearing the unquenchable fire so that those who trust in him may be delivered from it.

22.1: YHWH sends Jeremiah to the royal palace to deliver a word directly to the king. Jesus likewise takes his message to the center of religious authority — the temple and the ruling establishment. The prophetic pattern: YHWH's word goes to the seat of power, and the messenger is sent not merely to comfort but to confront. Jesus's entry into Jerusalem and the temple (Matt 21:10-17) enacts this pattern as its culmination.
22.2: The address to the king sitting on David's throne. Jesus is the Davidic king who sits on the ultimate throne: 'The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever; his kingdom will never end' (Luke 1:32-33). Every oracle addressed to the Davidic throne points toward the one in whom that throne is finally and permanently occupied.
22.3: 'Do what is just and right. Rescue those who have been robbed. Do not mistreat the foreigner, the fatherless, or the widow.' The justice-command that defines Davidic kingship. Jesus embodies this: 'The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to set the oppressed free' (Luke 4:18-19 citing Isa 61:1-2). His first sermon is a justice manifesto fulfilling the Davidic covenant requirement.
22.4: Conditional promise: if the king obeys, Davidic kings will continue to enter through the gates. Jesus fulfills the condition perfectly — he is the obedient Davidic king who passes through the gates of Jerusalem in his triumphal entry (Matt 21:9: 'Hosanna to the Son of David!') and through the gates of the heavenly city in his ascension (Ps 24:7-10: 'Lift up your heads, O gates... that the King of glory may come in').
22.5: 'I swear by myself that this house will become a ruin.' The divine self-oath — the ultimate guarantee. Jesus speaks with the same absolute authority without requiring an oath: 'Truly, truly I say to you' (the 'Amen, amen' formula of John's Gospel) places his word on par with the divine self-oath. James 5:12 (echoing Matt 5:37) grounds this in Jesus's teaching: let your yes be yes, no oath required — because his word is as reliable as YHWH's own oath.
22.6: The palace compared to Gilead and Lebanon — prized, beautiful, but destined for desolation. Jesus uses cedar imagery for the contrast between the elaborate and the genuine: 'look at the birds of the air... your heavenly Father feeds them' (Matt 6:26); 'consider the lilies... not even Solomon in all his glory was dressed like one of these' (Matt 6:29). The palace's magnificent cedars will become a ruin; the kingdom of heaven outlasts all cedar palaces.
22.7: Destroyers sent against the finest cedar trees — judgment that attacks the specific symbol of royal pride. The destroying agents in the NT are identified: the Romans in 70 CE fulfill this, as Jesus predicted (Matt 24:2: 'not one stone will be left on another'). The temple and palace destruction of 70 CE is Jeremiah's oracle reaching its NT fulfillment through Jesus's announcement.
22.8: 'Why has the LORD done this to such a great city?' — the nations' question at Jerusalem's fall. Jesus anticipates that his own disciples will ask the same question about the coming judgment and answers it in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24). The 'why' question has a consistent answer: the rejection of YHWH's covenant, here climaxed in the rejection of YHWH's Son.
22.9: The answer: they abandoned the covenant and served other gods. Jesus in John 5:39-40 diagnoses his contemporaries: 'You study the Scriptures diligently because you think that in them you have eternal life... yet you refuse to come to me to have life.' The abandonment of covenant relationship for religious form is the same sin — now rejecting the covenant-maker who has come in person.
22.10: 'Do not weep for the dead king; weep for the one who goes into exile, for he will never see this land again.' The one who goes into exile loses more than the one who dies — death ends suffering; exile continues it. Jesus's blessing 'blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted' (Matt 5:4) reverses this: grief and exile under Christ's kingdom are temporary. The permanent exile of 22:10 is reversed by the permanent homecoming Christ promises (John 14:3).
22.11: Shallum/Jehoahaz — taken captive to Egypt after three months of reign (2 Kgs 23:31-34). His brief reign and permanent exile is a foil for Jesus's brief earthly ministry that leads to permanent enthronement. Where the Davidic king is exiled never to return, the Son of David goes away (via cross and resurrection) and returns — and his return is permanent (Heb 7:24: 'because Jesus lives forever, he has a permanent priesthood').
22.12: 'He will die in the place he has been taken captive, and will never see this land again.' The death in exile of an unfaithful king. Jesus, by contrast, dies in the land — the specific death in the specific place that fulfills covenant prophecy (Ps 22; Isa 53; Zech 12:10). His death is not exile but the definitive act within the covenant land that achieves what no exile could: atonement for all the unfaithful kings and people he represents.
22.13: 'Woe to him who builds his palace through injustice ... who makes his countrymen work for nothing, not paying them for their labor.' The woe against Jehoiakim's exploitative building projects. Jesus pronounces seven woes against the scribes and Pharisees (Matt 23:13-36) using the same prophetic form — woe-oracles against religious leaders who exploit those under their care. The form Jeremiah uses against the king Jesus uses against the religious establishment.
22.14: The grand palace with large rooms, cedar paneling, vermilion paint — conspicuous consumption as royal validation. Jesus's contrast: 'foxes have dens and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head' (Matt 8:20; Luke 9:58). The one who is more than any Davidic king rejects the cedar-palace model entirely. His kingdom is not built by exploited labor but by self-giving service.
22.15: 'Does building a rival in cedar prove you are a king? Your father ate and drank, and it went well — he administered justice and righteousness and it went well.' Josiah as the model king: the one who administered justice flourished; the one who built cedar proved nothing. Jesus's teaching: 'whoever wants to be great among you must be your servant... just as the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve' (Matt 20:26-28).
