Greatness through service rather than through construction or consumption.
22.16: 'He defended the cause of the poor and needy — is that not what it means to know me?' declares the LORD. The knowledge of God expressed in justice for the poor: da-at YHWH = care for the vulnerable. Jesus's Matt 25:31-46 enacts this with revolutionary force: 'whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me.' Meeting Jesus in the poor is meeting YHWH — the same identification that 22:16 makes between knowing YHWH and defending the poor.
22.17: 'But you have no eyes or heart for anything except your own dishonest gain, and for shedding innocent blood.' Jehoiakim's moral blindness — eyes and heart fixed on profit and violence. Jesus: 'blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God' (Matt 5:8) — purity of heart (the opposite of Jehoiakim's divided, greedy heart) is the condition for seeing YHWH. His own pure heart, set entirely on the Father's will, makes him the only one who sees God clearly (John 1:18).
22.18: Jehoiakim's shameless death — no mourning. The contrast with Jesus's death is complete: the women wept (Luke 23:27-28), Joseph of Arimathea took care for proper burial (John 19:38-42), the earth shook and rocks split (Matt 27:51-54). Even creation mourned at the cross in a way no one mourned for Jehoiakim. The death of the just receives the honor the unjust king is denied.
22.19: 'He will be buried like a donkey — dragged away and thrown outside the gates of Jerusalem.' The undignified death and disposal of Jehoiakim. Jesus was crucified outside the city gate (Heb 13:12: 'Jesus suffered outside the city gate to make the people holy through his own blood') — in the place of the cursed and disgraced. But his burial was honorable (Isa 53:9: 'he was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death'), and his resurrection is the ultimate reversal of the Jehoiakim-pattern.
22.20: The cry to Lebanon, Bashan, and Abarim — all allies crushed, all lovers taken. The collapse of the political alliances that replaced trust in YHWH. Jesus's disciples will similarly scatter when he is arrested (Matt 26:56: 'then all the disciples deserted him and fled') — the allies failing in the moment of crisis. But his resurrection reconstitutes the community on a different basis than political alliance: it is covenant loyalty, not geopolitical calculation, that holds.
22.21: 'I warned you when you were at ease, but you said: I will not listen. This has been your habit from your youth.' The lifelong pattern of not listening. Heb 3:7-11 cites Ps 95:7-11 for the same warning: 'today if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts'. The history of not-listening culminates in the rejection of the Son (John 1:11: 'he came to his own, and his own did not receive him'). The pattern of the wilderness generation, Jeremiah's generation, and Jesus's generation are the same.
22.22: The wind sweeping away rulers; allies taken captive — the comprehensive collapse of Jerusalem's power structure. Jesus's kingdom is established through the opposite of political power: 'my kingdom is not of this world; if it were, my servants would fight' (John 18:36). The wind of judgment that sweeps away earthly rulers cannot touch his kingdom, because it was never constituted by those structures.
22.23: 'You who live in Lebanon, nestled among the cedars — how you will groan when birth pangs seize you!' The image of birth pangs (chil ke-yoleda) for judgment. Jesus uses the birth-pang image for the eschaton: 'all these are the beginning of birth pangs' (Matt 24:8; Mark 13:8) — the suffering of the end-time is the labor that precedes new creation. Paul: 'we know that the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time' (Rom 8:22). The cedar-nestled pride of Jerusalem groans in judgment; creation groans in hope.
22.24: 'Even if Coniah ... were the signet ring on my right hand, I would still pull you off.' The signet ring (hotam) was the instrument of royal authority — the king's personal seal pressed into official documents. YHWH pulls the signet off Coniah: the authority is withdrawn. This is the background for Hag 2:23 where YHWH restores the signet to Zerubbabel (a descendant of Jehoiachin): 'I will make you like my signet ring, for I have chosen you.' The removed signet will be restored — but only through the Zerubbabel line that leads to Jesus.
22.25: Hand Coniah over to Nebuchadnezzar — the king being handed into the enemy's power. Jesus was 'handed over' (paredideto) to the authorities (Matt 26:2, 45; 27:2, 18) — the same covenantal surrender. But while Coniah's hand-over is judgment, Jesus's hand-over is redemptive: 'he was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification' (Rom 4:25).
22.26: 'I will hurl you and the mother who bore you into another country, a land where neither of you was born, and there you will die.' The exile of the king and queen mother (Nehushta). The queen mother (gevirah) held significant status in the Davidic court (1 Kgs 2:19; 15:13; 2 Kgs 10:13). Mary, Jesus's mother, is present at both the beginning and end of his ministry — at the wedding of Cana (John 2:1-5) and at the cross (John 19:25-27). Unlike Nehushta who is hurled into exile with her son, Mary is given a home by the beloved disciple (John 19:27).
22.27: 'They will never return' — the permanent exile of Coniah and his mother. Jehoiachin historically remained in Babylon until his release in his old age (2 Kgs 25:27-30; the Babylonian Chronicles mention him receiving rations from Nebuchadnezzar). He died in exile. The throne-promise is fulfilled not by Jehoiachin's return but by his descendant (through Zerubbabel) Jesus of Nazareth — who establishes the Davidic kingdom without geographic land-return, because his kingdom transcends territory.
22.28: 'Is this man Coniah nothing but a broken, despised clay pot — something no one wants?' The lament over the deposed king as broken pottery. Isa 53:3 describes the Suffering Servant: 'he was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering and familiar with pain ... he was despised, and we held him in low esteem.' Jesus the descendent of Coniah inherits both the throne-promise and the throne-curse — but in his case the despising and rejection become the means of redemption.
22.29: Eretz eretz eretz shim-i dvar YHWH: O land, land, land, hear the word of the LORD! — the unprecedented triple call, more urgent than the prophetic double-call. The triple invocation has eschatological weight: the land itself must hear what is happening to the Davidic covenant. The NT makes the land a universal concept: 'the meek will inherit the earth/gē' (Matt 5:5) — the land-promise of the covenant expands to the new earth (Rev 21:1-4). The triple call of earth-land-earth is answered by the eschatological new creation.
22.30: 'Record this man as though childless — no one from his descendants will succeed in sitting on David's throne.' The Coniah decree: throne-stripped, functionally childless for Davidic purposes. Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:12) includes Jeconiah (Coniah) in the legal line of Joseph — but Jesus is not biologically Joseph's son (Matt 1:18-25; Luke 1:34-35). He is Joseph's legal heir (inheriting the Davidic claim) but not Joseph's biological heir (not biologically descended from Coniah). The virgin birth is the theological key: it simultaneously maintains the Davidic lineage through Joseph and bypasses the biological throne-curse. Jesus is the Davidic heir through whom no descendant of Coniah biologically sits on the throne — exactly fulfilling the decree while also fulfilling the promise.

23.1: 'Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture!' The woe-oracle against the false shepherds opens the chapter that ends with the Righteous Branch. Jesus is the Good Shepherd (John 10:11-14) who stands in contrast to every false shepherd in Israel's history. His self-identification as the good shepherd takes up the entire language of Jeremiah 23: he knows his sheep, they know him, he lays down his life for them rather than scattering and destroying them.
23.2: The shepherds have scattered YHWH's flock and driven them away — YHWH will punish them for it. Jesus identifies himself as the one YHWH sends in fulfillment of this promise of punishment and replacement. In the Parable of the Tenants (Matt 21:33-46) the wicked tenants-shepherds are removed and the vineyard given to others. The punishment of the false shepherds is YHWH's vindication of the flock.
23.3: 'I myself will gather the remnant of my flock from all the lands where I have driven them.' YHWH as the gathering shepherd. Jesus: 'I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen; I must bring them also' (John 10:16). His gathering work extends beyond ethnic Israel to the nations — the global gathering that 'all the lands' implies. Matt 28:19-20: the Great Commission is the mechanism by which YHWH gathers the global remnant through the Son's sent messengers.
23.4: 'I will raise up shepherds who will truly care for them; they will no longer be afraid or terrified, and none will be missing.' The promise of faithful under-shepherds. Jesus fulfills the 'none missing' (literally: 'they shall not be lacking') guarantee: 'of all those he has given me I will lose none, but raise them up at the last day' (John 6:39); 'I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The 'none missing' of v. 4 is the 'lose none' of John 6:39.
23.5: The Righteous Branch Oracle: hineh yamim baim neum YHWH vahaqimoti leDavid tzemach tzaddiq: Behold, the days are coming when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch who will reign wisely and do what is just and right in the land. This is the central messianic promise of Jeremiah. Tzemach (Branch/Shoot) is a technical messianic title: it appears again at Jer 33:15, Zech 3:8 ('my servant the Branch'), and Zech 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple of the LORD'). The Branch reigns wisely (vesakhal), does justice and righteousness (mishpat utzedaqah) — the defining characteristics of the ideal Davidic king (Isa 9:7; 11:1-5). Jesus fulfills every element: Davidic descent (Luke 1:27, 32-33; Matt 1:1), wisdom (Luke 2:52; Matt 12:42), justice (Matt 12:18-21 citing Isa 42:1-4), and righteousness (Heb 7:26).
23.6: YHWH Tsidqenu: 'This is the name by which he will be called: The LORD our Righteousness.' The divine name given to the messianic king is itself a form of YHWH's name — the Branch is identified as YHWH present. This is one of the most explicit OT identifications of the messianic king with YHWH himself. Paul applies this directly to Christ: 'Christ Jesus... has become for us wisdom from God — that is, our righteousness, holiness and redemption' (1 Cor 1:30); 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21). Jesus is YHWH Tsidqenu — the LORD who becomes our righteousness.
23.7: The new Exodus that will supersede the old: 'no longer will they say, As the LORD lives who brought Israel up from Egypt.' The greater deliverance will make the first deliverance seem secondary by comparison. Jesus inaugurates this new Exodus: Matt 2:15 ('Out of Egypt I called my son') identifies Jesus as the true Israel making the definitive Exodus; the transfiguration has Moses and Elijah speaking about his 'departure' (exodon, Luke 9:31) — his death-and-resurrection is the new Exodus; the Last Supper reframes Passover around his own body and blood as the new Exodus meal (Luke 22:14-20).
23.8: 'As the LORD lives who brought up the descendants of Israel from the north country and from all the lands where he had driven them.' The new Exodus includes the diaspora from all directions — a global gathering. Acts 2:5-11 shows this beginning at Pentecost: Jews 'from every nation under heaven' hear the gospel. The gathering from north and south and all the lands is the Spirit-enabled gathering of the new covenant era.
23.9: Jeremiah's anguish at the prophets' corruption — his heart broken, his bones trembling, like a drunken man overcome by YHWH's holy words. This prophetic agony participates in the divine grief. Jesus's agony in Gethsemane (Luke 22:44: 'being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly') is the Son bearing the same weight of the holy word of judgment — but taking it into himself rather than pronouncing it outward.
23.10: Land full of adulterers; curse making the land mourn; the wilderness pastures dried up. The cosmic consequences of moral corruption: the land itself responds to human faithlessness (Rom 8:19-22). Jesus's death and resurrection begins the reversal: the creation that was subjected to futility will be liberated (Rom 8:21). His resurrection-presence transforms the barren (John 15:5: 'apart from me you can do nothing'; 'with me you bear much fruit').
23.11: 'Both prophet and priest are godless; even in my own house I have found their wickedness.' The corruption at the center — the sacred professions have been profaned. Jesus drives out the merchants from the temple (Matt 21:12-13) precisely because the house of prayer has been corrupted by those who should have been its guardians. His action enacts this oracle's judgment in real time.
23.12: Slippery paths in darkness for the false prophets — they will be driven into darkness and fall. Jesus's 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12; 9:5) is the answer to the darkness in which the false prophets stumble. Those who follow him 'will never walk in darkness but will have the light of life.' The slippery path in the dark is the condition of those who reject the true shepherd-prophet.
23.13: Prophets of Samaria prophesying by Baal, leading Israel astray. The northern kingdom's false prophecy as the precursor to Jerusalem's worse version (v. 14). Jesus's warning about false prophets (Matt 7:15-23; 24:11, 24) anticipates the NT pattern. The test of the true prophet: 'by their fruit you will recognize them' (Matt 7:20) — not the claims made but the direction they lead the flock (toward or away from the true shepherd).
23.14: 'In the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen something even more horrible: they commit adultery and live by lies.' Greater privilege means greater accountability. Jesus's woes against Chorazin and Bethsaida (Matt 11:21-22): 'it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgment than for you' — those who received the most (miracles in the presence of Jesus) and rejected it bear greater judgment.
23.15: Wormwood and poisoned water for the false prophets. The bitter cup they cause others to drink returns to them. Jesus takes the bitter cup that judgment requires (Mark 14:36: 'take this cup from me — yet not what I will, but what you will'). His willingness to drink the cup of judgment means that those who trust in him are not given the wormwood-and-gall that the false prophets prescribed as safe.
23.16: 'Do not listen to the prophets who are prophesying to you; they are filling you with false hopes.' The prophets who speak from their own hearts (machzon libbam). Jesus's teaching comes from the Father, not from his own heart in isolation: 'my teaching is not my own; it comes from the one who sent me' (John 7:16); 'the words I say to you I do not speak on my own authority; the Father, living in me, is doing his work' (John 14:10). The criterion for distinguishing true from false prophecy is whether it originates in the divine council or the prophet's own imagination.
23.17: 'They keep telling those who follow stubbornness of heart: No disaster will come.' The false peace — confirming the stubborn in their stubbornness. Jesus refuses this comfort: 'do not think I came to bring peace to the earth; I did not come to bring peace, but a sword' (Matt 10:34). The sword that divides is more merciful than the false peace that confirms people in paths that end in judgment.
23.18: 'For which of them has stood in the LORD's council to see and hear his word?' The sod YHWH (divine council) — the criterion for authentic prophecy. Jesus has unique, permanent standing in the Father's council: 'the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing, because whatever the Father does the Son also does' (John 5:19); 'I have made known to you everything I heard from my Father' (John 15:15). He is the prophet who has fully stood in YHWH's council and speaks from complete divine intimacy.
23.19: The storm of YHWH, his wrath going out like a whirling tempest, falling on the heads of the wicked. The divine-warrior storm that no false prophet can prevent. Jesus stills the storm (Matt 8:23-27; Mark 4:35-41; Luke 8:22-25) — 'who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!' — demonstrating that he is YHWH the storm-commander, not a victim of storms. The storm of divine wrath that falls on the wicked falls on him at the cross so it need not fall on those who are in him.
23.20: 'In the latter days you will understand it clearly.' The prophetic word intelligible only after the fact. Luke 24:45: 'then he opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures.' The risen Jesus is the interpreter of the prophetic word — in the latter days, standing with the disciples after his resurrection, he makes the purposes of YHWH's heart clear. The disciples are the 'latter days' generation to whom Jeremiah's prophecy finally becomes intelligible.
23.21: 'I did not send these prophets, yet they ran; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied.' The unsent, self-authorized prophets. Jesus: 'I was sent by him' (ekeinos me apesteilen) — the 'sent-ness' formula occurs dozens of times in John. His authority derives entirely from the one who sent him (John 8:42; 17:3, 8, 18, 21, 23, 25). The distinction between the authentic sent prophet and the self-running prophet is the distinction between Jesus and every false claimant.
23.22: 'If they had stood in my council and proclaimed my words, they would have turned them from their evil way.' Authentic prophecy that comes from the divine council actually changes people. Jesus's words have transformative power: 'the words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life' (John 6:63); 'if you hold to my teaching, you are really my disciples. Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free' (John 8:31-32). True prophetic words produce repentance and transformation; false ones confirm existing behavior.
23.23: 'Am I only a God nearby, declares the LORD, and not a God far away?' The rhetorical question of divine omnipresence. Jesus promises the same omnipresence in his own person: 'I am with you always, to the very end of the age' (Matt 28:20); 'where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them' (Matt 18:20). The God who fills heaven and earth is now present 'nearby' in the most intimate sense — in human flesh among his people.
23.24: 'Can anyone hide in secret places where I cannot see him? Do I not fill heaven and earth?' The omniscient, omnipresent God who fills all space. Jesus speaks with the same authority about his own omniscience: 'he knew all people and needed no one to testify about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:24-25); 'I am the good shepherd; I know my sheep' (John 10:14). The God who fills heaven and earth has in Jesus 'filled' earth in a new, incarnate way (Eph 4:10: 'he who descended is the very one who ascended higher than all the heavens, in order to fill the whole universe').
23.25: The dream-prophets claiming 'I have dreamed, I have dreamed!' — the subjective, self-generated prophetic content. Jesus's contrast: 'I tell you what I have seen in the Father's presence' (John 8:38); 'my Father has revealed this to you' (Matt 16:17). The dreamed prophecy vs. the seen-and-revealed prophecy: Jesus's prophetic authority is grounded in direct, unmediated vision of the Father, not in the vagaries of the prophet's own dream-life.
23.26: 'How long shall there be lies in the heart of these prophets — prophesying the deceit of their own heart?' The chronic condition of self-deception projected outward as prophecy. John 3:19-21: 'this is the verdict: light has come into the world, but people loved darkness instead of light because their deeds were evil.' Jesus's light exposes the self-deceiving heart — not to condemn (John 3:17) but to bring into the light where transformation can occur.
23.27: The prophets making people forget YHWH's name through their dreams. The name-of-YHWH as the thing at risk. Jesus inverts this: he makes the Father's name known: 'I have revealed you to those whom you gave me' (John 17:6); 'I have made you known to them, and will continue to make you known' (John 17:26). The dream-prophets who cause forgetting of the divine name are countered by the Son who makes the Father permanently known.
23.28: 'What has straw to do with grain? Let the one who has my word speak it faithfully.' The contrast between straw (the dream-prophet's chaff) and grain (YHWH's genuine word). John 6:35: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never go hungry.' Jesus is the grain — the nourishing, substantial word — versus the straw of false prophecy that fills but does not sustain. His word is the bread that gives life to the world (John 6:33).
23.29: 'Is not my word like fire, declares the LORD, and like a hammer that shatters rock?' The divine word as fire and hammer. Heb 4:12: 'the word of God is alive and active, sharper than any double-edged sword, it penetrates even to dividing soul and spirit, joints and marrow; it judges the thoughts and attitudes of the heart.' Jesus is the Word of God (John 1:1) — the fire, hammer, and sharp sword — who both shatters and reconstructs. At the transfiguration his face shines like the sun and his garments become brilliant (Matt 17:2) — the fire-of-YHWH incarnate.
23.30: 'I am against the prophets who steal my words from each other.' Plagiarized prophecy — borrowed words passed off as direct revelation. Jesus's word is wholly his own and wholly the Father's: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken' (John 12:49). There is no gap between his word and the Father's — no borrowing, no contamination, no secondhand transmission.
23.31: 'I am against the prophets who wag their own tongues and say: This is what the LORD declares.' Tongue-wagging prophecy — the physical gesture of the false prophet speaking his own words with YHWH's authority. James 3:5-6: 'the tongue is a small part of the body, but it makes great boasts... it corrupts the whole body.' Jesus's speech is purified of this gap: he speaks what he hears (John 8:26, 28, 40), sees (John 8:38), and receives (John 17:8).
23.32: 'I am against those who prophesy lying dreams ... who lead my people astray with reckless lies.' The lying dreamers leading the flock astray. Jesus identifies himself as the only reliable leader of the flock: 'I am the gate for the sheep ... all who ever came before me were thieves and robbers' (John 10:7-8). The lying prophets who lead astray are distinguished from the true shepherd by whether the flock is fed or fleeced, led or misled.
23.33: The massa (burden/oracle) wordplay: when the people ask 'What is the burden of the LORD?' they become themselves the burden — 'you are the burden, and I will throw you off.' Jesus's inversion: 'Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you... my yoke is easy and my burden is light' (Matt 11:28-30). The massa that crushes becomes the light yoke that rests: the burden-language of Jer 23:33 is explicitly reversed in Jesus's invitation.
23.34: Punishment of anyone who says 'the burden of the LORD' — prophet, priest, or person. The divine word has been so abused that the formula must be discontinued. Jesus announces a new covenant of speech: his words are his own because they are the Father's own — no formula required, because the speaker is himself the authentic mediator. His teaching 'as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law' (Matt 7:29) bypasses the formula-dependence entirely.
23.35: The replacement formula: 'What has the LORD answered you?' — asking about response rather than declaring a burden. Jesus models this responsive posture: 'the Son does nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing' (John 5:19). His whole prophetic ministry is one of listening for the Father's answer and declaring it. The disciple's prayer in John 17 shows this: Jesus reports what he has received and transmits it to his disciples.
23.36: 'You must no longer speak of the burden of the LORD, for the burden is every person's own word.' The word has been so corrupted by misuse that the vocabulary itself must change. Jesus announces a new vocabulary: 'the words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life' (John 6:63); in him, YHWH's word and the speaker are identical — there is no gap between the formula and the reality. He himself is the Word (John 1:14).
23.37: The proper question to the prophet: 'What has the LORD answered you?' The disciple's posture of asking rather than presuming. Jesus teaches his disciples to ask: 'ask and it will be given to you, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened to you' (Matt 7:7); 'until now you have not asked for anything in my name; ask and you will receive, and your joy will be complete' (John 16:24). The asking that replaces the false-burden formula.
23.38: Because they persist in using the forbidden formula despite the command — they will be punished. The persistence of self-willed religious speech after divine instruction is the same disobedience as the false prophecy itself. Jesus's warning: 'not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father' (Matt 7:21). Religious formula without covenant reality is the persistent sin Jeremiah identifies here.
23.39: 'I will surely lift you up and fling you away from my presence — you and the city I gave to your ancestors.' The exile as rejection from the divine presence — the ultimate covenant curse. Jesus's most terrible words use the same expulsion language: 'then I will tell them plainly: I never knew you. Away from me, you evildoers!' (Matt 7:23); 'depart from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire' (Matt 25:41). The flung-away of 23:39 is the eternal consequence Jesus describes — and which he takes upon himself at the cross ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') so that his people never hear the final version.
23.40: 'And I will bring on you everlasting disgrace and lasting shame that will not be forgotten.' Permanent disgrace — the opposite of covenant honor. Jesus's resurrection is the permanent reversal of this disgrace: 'looking to Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith, who for the joy set before him endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God' (Heb 12:2). He endured the maximum disgrace and received the maximum honor — and in him those who were disgraced are restored to honor (1 Pet 2:6: 'the one who trusts in him will never be put to shame').

24.1: The two baskets of figs before the temple: a vision given after Jehoiachin's deportation (597 BCE). The temple setting of the vision — figs placed before the LORD as offerings — makes the interpretation covenantal: YHWH is evaluating the quality of what is brought before him. Jesus's parable of the fig tree (Luke 13:6-9) and the cursed fig tree (Matt 21:18-22; Mark 11:12-14) both engage this fig-as-covenant-symbol tradition. The temple and the fig tree are interconnected in Jesus's final week.
24.2: Very good figs like first-ripe figs (bikkurot) — the highest quality, the first of the harvest, most prized — versus figs so rotten they cannot be eaten. The contrast is absolute: no middle ground, no 'acceptable but not great.' Jesus's parable of the wheat and tares (Matt 13:24-30, 36-43) has the same structure: two categories, same apparent field, final sorting. The good figs represent the exiles YHWH is about to do good to; the bad figs those who remain in Jerusalem under Zedekiah.
24.3: YHWH asks Jeremiah what he sees. The prophetic dialogue method — YHWH invites the prophet's observation before giving the interpretation. Jesus frequently asks his disciples what they see or perceive (Matt 16:13-15: 'Who do people say the Son of Man is?'; Matt 13:51: 'Have you understood all these things?'). The Socratic method of prophetic pedagogy: the observation precedes the revelation.
24.4: The word of the LORD coming — the transition from observation to interpretation. Jesus's teaching moves between observation (looking at the birds, the lilies, the fig tree) and interpretation (the kingdom of God is like this). The rhythm of see-then-interpret is the educational method of the prophetic tradition into which Jesus inserts himself as the definitive teacher (Matt 7:28-29).
24.5: 'Like these good figs, I will look favorably on the exiles of Judah whom I sent away from this place to the land of the Babylonians.' YHWH regards the exile as a divine sending for good, not merely a judgment. Jesus's understanding of his own 'sending' into the world has the same logic: he is sent by the Father (John 3:17) for the good of those he goes to, not merely to condemn. The exiled people in Babylon, apparently abandoned, are being specially cared for.
24.6: 'I will keep my eyes on them for good, and I will bring them back to this land. I will build them and not tear down, plant them and not uproot.' The four reversal pairs — build/tear down, plant/uproot — reverse the charter of Jer 1:10 ('to tear down and to plant, to destroy and to build'). Jesus's ministry is the new-covenant version: 'I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it' (Matt 16:18). The one who tears down the old structures builds the new one that cannot be pulled down.
24.7: 'I will give them a heart to know me, that I am the LORD. They will be my people and I will be their God, for they will return to me with all their heart.' The proto-new-covenant verse of ch. 24: the lev lada-at oti (heart to know me) is the forerunner of Jer 31:33-34 ('I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts... they will all know me'). Jesus gives this heart through the Spirit: 'the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you all things' (John 14:26); 'they will all be taught by God' (John 6:45 citing Isa 54:13). The heart-to-know-YHWH that exile could not produce, the Spirit provides through Christ.
24.8: 'But as for the bad figs ... Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, the survivors in Jerusalem, and those who remain in Egypt.' The category of those who did not go into exile, who resisted YHWH's purposes by staying. Jesus's warning: 'whoever finds their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for my sake will find it' (Matt 10:39). The counterintuitive reversal: those who held onto their position in Jerusalem (the 'good' outcome) are the bad figs; those who lost everything to exile are the good figs. Surrender to YHWH's purposes, even painful ones, is the path of covenant.
24.9: The bad figs become a horror to all kingdoms — reproach, byword, taunt, curse everywhere YHWH drives them. The covenant-curse catalog (Deut 28:37: 'you will become a thing of horror, a byword and an object of ridicule among all the peoples where the LORD will drive you'). Jesus bears all these titles at the cross (Matt 27:39-44: 'those who passed by hurled insults at him... the robbers who were crucified with him also heaped insults on him'). He takes the position of the bad-fig curse upon himself so that those who trust him become the good figs — transformed.
24.10: 'And I will send the sword, famine, and pestilence against them until they are completely destroyed from the land.' The triple judgment formula — sword, famine, pestilence — is the standard covenant-curse triad (Lev 26:25-26; Deut 28:22-25; Jer 14:12; 21:7). Jesus predicts the same triple judgment coming on Jerusalem in his generation (Matt 24:6-7: wars, famines, earthquakes as 'the beginning of birth pangs'). The complete removal from the land is the ultimate covenant consequence — which he enters and absorbs so that a new community can be grafted in.

28.1: Hananiah, a prophet from Gibeon, arises in the fourth year of Zedekiah to contradict Jeremiah's message of a 70-year exile. The collision of two prophets claiming YHWH's authority establishes the central NT question about true versus false proclamation. Jesus warns: 'Beware of false prophets who come to you in sheep's clothing' (Matt 7:15). The false prophet promises peace without repentance — the ancient version of the message that minimizes judgment.
28.2: Hananiah claims to speak in the name of YHWH: 'I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon.' The invocation of the divine name without divine authority is the pattern Jesus condemns: 'Not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom' (Matt 7:21-22). The false prophet's message is about removal of burden without transformation — an external fix rather than heart-renewal.
28.3: Hananiah promises the return of the temple vessels within two years. The specific timeframe is designed to seem verifiable and credible. Jesus's own predictions include specific timeframes (Matt 24:34) but are grounded in his authority as the Son who knows the Father's will (Mark 13:32). The false prophet's specificity is a rhetorical tool, not divine knowledge.
28.4: The promise of Jeconiah's return and the breaking of Babylon's yoke is a message of false comfort — removing the covenant consequence of sin without dealing with the sin itself. Christ's ministry does the opposite: 'I have come not to bring peace but a sword' (Matt 10:34) — first dealing with the root (sin, death) so that true peace may follow. False religion promises the fruit without addressing the root.
28.5: Jeremiah responds before the priests and all the people in the temple — the public setting of the prophetic confrontation. Christ's public confrontations with the scribes and Pharisees follow this pattern: truth and error must be distinguished before the watching community. The temple is the place where prophetic authority is contested because it is the place where YHWH's presence is invoked.
28.6: 'Amen! May YHWH do so; may YHWH fulfill your words.' Jeremiah's ironic amen — he agrees with the desired outcome (restoration, peace) while disagreeing with Hananiah's claim that it is imminent and without repentance. This models honest engagement with false hope: affirming the genuine desire while challenging the false timeline. Christ similarly affirms what is true in Jewish expectation while correcting the corruption (Matt 5:17-20).
28.7: The appeal to prior prophets establishes the rule of prophetic tradition: consistent witness over time validates a message. Jesus appeals to the same tradition — he stands in the line of the prophets whom Jerusalem killed (Matt 23:37). The authority of a prophet is demonstrated by continuity with the prophetic tradition, not by novelty.
28.8: The prophets before Jeremiah prophesied war and famine against many nations. The pattern of true prophecy is uncomfortable: it announces judgment before restoration. This is Christ's own pattern — the Sermon on the Mount begins with the poor in spirit, not the triumphant; the resurrection comes through the cross. True prophecy follows the death-to-life sequence.
28.9: 'As for the prophet who prophesies peace, when the word of that prophet comes to pass, then it will be known that YHWH has truly sent him.' The fulfillment criterion for prophecy (Deut 18:22) is applied to Hananiah — and his failure is later confirmed (v17). Christ is the ultimate test case: his resurrection on the third day, his prediction of the temple's destruction (70 AD), and his promise of his return authenticate his prophetic authority beyond all others.
28.10: Hananiah removes the yoke from Jeremiah's neck and breaks it — a dramatic counter-sign-act. The breaking of the wooden yoke prefigures a heavier iron yoke (v13). This is the pattern of false gospel: remove the visible burden (accountability, cross-bearing) and substitute a heavier invisible one (self-deception, bondage to sin). Christ's yoke is easy precisely because it is real: 'Take my yoke upon you and learn from me' (Matt 11:29).
28.11: Hananiah proclaims the nations' yoke broken before Jeremiah simply walks away. Jeremiah's withdrawal is not defeat — it is waiting for YHWH's word. Jesus similarly withdrew from confrontation at strategic moments (Luke 4:30; John 8:59) — not from fear but from sovereign timing. The true prophet speaks when YHWH commands and waits when he does not.
28.12: The word of YHWH comes to Jeremiah after Hananiah has broken the wooden yoke. YHWH's response to Hananiah's sign-act is to escalate: wooden yoke broken → iron yoke. This is the pattern of rejecting the light burden of covenant obedience and receiving the heavy burden of judgment. The NT counterpart: rejecting the easy yoke of Christ leads to the bondage Christ came to break (Gal 5:1).
28.13: Iron yokes cannot be broken as wooden ones can. The escalation from wood to iron communicates that false prophecy does not remove judgment but intensifies it. The message of grace that omits repentance produces harder hearts and heavier consequences — the theological principle Paul articulates: 'You are storing up wrath for yourself on the day of wrath' (Rom 2:5). Only Christ removes the iron yoke completely through the cross.
28.14: 'I have put a yoke of iron on the neck of all these nations to serve Nebuchadnezzar.' The nations' subjection to Babylon is YHWH's sovereign act — the world's empires are instruments in divine hands. This establishes the framework for understanding Rome in the NT period: human political authority is real but derivative (John 19:11). Christ's kingdom 'is not of this world' (John 18:36) but will ultimately outlast all iron yokes.
28.15: Jeremiah confronts Hananiah directly: 'YHWH has not sent you, and you have made this people trust in a lie.' The verdict against the false prophet is that he created false trust — faith in the wrong word. This is the precise danger Jesus warns against: building on sand instead of rock (Matt 7:26-27). The false prophet produces faith that does not hold when the storm comes.
28.16: Hananiah will die that very year because he spoke rebellion against YHWH. The swift judgment on the false prophet is sobering — false teaching is not a minor error but rebellion (sarah) against YHWH. James warns that teachers will be judged more strictly (Jas 3:1). The NT intensifies the stakes around prophetic authority, not relaxes them.
28.17: Hananiah died in the seventh month of that same year — the word of YHWH fulfilled against the word of Hananiah in real historical time. The swift vindication of Jeremiah points to the pattern of Christ's own vindication: prophecies made (Matt 26:64), the Passion, resurrection (Acts 2:23-24). YHWH vindicates his true prophet in history; the resurrection is the ultimate historical vindication.

29.1: Jeremiah's letter to the exiles in Babylon from Jerusalem — a letter sent across political and geographical boundaries to sustain a community of faith. The pastoral letter format anticipates the NT epistolary tradition: Paul writes to communities in exile from their true homeland (Phil 3:20; 1 Pet 1:1). The letter bridges the gap between the sender's presence and the recipients' need.
29.2: The letter goes after Jeconiah, the queen mother, officials, craftsmen, and smiths — the elite of Judah — have already gone into exile. The community that receives Jeremiah's letter is the best and brightest of Judah, stripped of their homeland. Paul similarly addresses letters to communities that include social elite (Acts 17:12) but re-centers them on a different set of values.
29.3: Elasah and Gemariah carry the letter — named messengers who bear the word. The incarnational principle: the word is not disembodied but carried by human agents. Christ himself is the Word made flesh (John 1:14), the ultimate divine messenger who does not merely carry the message but is the message.
29.4: 'Thus says YHWH of Hosts, the God of Israel, to all the exiles whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon.' YHWH acknowledges his own agency in the exile: 'whom I have sent.' The exiles were not accidents of history but YHWH's purposeful placement. Paul sees his imprisonment the same way: 'what has happened to me has really served to advance the gospel' (Phil 1:12). Providence shapes the mission field.
29.5: Build houses, plant gardens, eat their produce — the command to invest in exile rather than maintaining a posture of suspended animation. This is the NT pattern for the church as exile community: 'seek the good of the city where I have sent you into exile' (v7). The kingdom of God is not built by withdrawal but by engaged presence, bearing fruit in alien soil (John 15:8).
29.6: Take wives, have sons and daughters — establish family life in exile. The exiles are not to minimize their numbers but to multiply. The NT mission follows: 'Go and make disciples' (Matt 28:19). The people of God grow in every soil, including hostile environments. The church's demographic vitality in exile is itself a sign of YHWH's blessing.
29.7: 'Seek the welfare (shalom) of the city where I have sent you into exile, and pray to YHWH on its behalf.' This is the foundational text for the church's civic vocation: loving enemies, praying for those who persecute (Matt 5:44), being salt and light in society (Matt 5:13-16). The command to seek the city's shalom anticipates the NT's theology of the church as a community for the world's benefit.
29.8: 'Do not let your prophets and your diviners who are among you deceive you.' The warning against internal false prophecy mirrors Christ's warning: 'Many false prophets will arise and lead many astray' (Matt 24:11). The danger is not external but internal — voices within the covenant community that promise comfort without truth.
29.9: 'They are prophesying falsely to you in my name; I did not send them.' The claim of YHWH's name without YHWH's commission — the counterfeit gospel that Paul warns against: 'If anyone is preaching to you a gospel contrary to the one you received, let him be accursed' (Gal 1:9). Authentication of the messenger by YHWH is non-negotiable.
29.10: 'When seventy years are completed for Babylon, I will visit you and fulfill to you my good word.' The 70-year period is YHWH's appointed time — a specific, numbered divine plan. This temporal specificity about YHWH's plan is the foundation of the NT claim that Christ came 'when the time had fully come' (Gal 4:4). YHWH's plans have a kairos — an appointed time, not just chronos.
29.11: 'For I know the plans I have for you, declares YHWH, plans for welfare and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope.' This is one of the most-cited OT promises in the NT era: the certainty of YHWH's good intent toward his people. In Christ it finds its yes and amen (2 Cor 1:20) — every plan of welfare converges in the one who is himself the embodiment of shalom, and in whom 'all the promises of God find their Yes.' The future and hope is not vague optimism but has a personal name.
29.12: 'Then you will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will hear you.' Access to YHWH's hearing is promised to the exiles who seek him — anticipating the new covenant access Christ opens. 'Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do' (John 14:13). Prayer heard by God is the mark of covenant relationship, fully realized in Christ who intercedes (Heb 7:25).
29.13: 'You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart.' The seeking-finding promise is quoted by Jesus directly (Matt 7:7: 'Seek and you will find'). Christ is not merely a route to finding YHWH — he is the one through whom YHWH is found: 'I am the way... no one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). The wholehearted seeking of Jer 29:13 is directed to the person of Christ in the NT.
29.14: 'I will be found by you... and I will restore your fortunes.' The restoration promise — ashiv et-shevutchem — is YHWH's sovereign act of reversal. The ultimate restoration is the new creation: Rev 21:5, 'Behold, I am making all things new.' Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits of that total restoration (1 Cor 15:20-23), the guarantee that YHWH's plan to restore is not a metaphor but a physical, historical reality.
29.15: The exiles appeal to their own prophets in Babylon. The availability of prophetic voices in exile reflects the expansion of prophetic ministry beyond the land — anticipating the NT where the Spirit is poured out on all flesh (Acts 2:17-18). YHWH is not confined to a geography; his word reaches the exiles.
29.16: Concerning the king sitting on David's throne and the people remaining in Jerusalem — YHWH addresses the whole covenant community, not just the exiles. The scope of YHWH's word encompasses both those in exile and those who remain. Christ's commission is similarly universal: 'all nations' (Matt 28:19), 'to the ends of the earth' (Acts 1:8).
29.17: The bad figs who were not deported — those left in Jerusalem who think they escaped — will face sword, famine, and plague. The irony: those who avoided the covenant discipline of exile (the first deportation) face worse judgment. This reverses the apparent advantage. Jesus makes the same point: the tax collectors and prostitutes enter the kingdom before the self-righteous (Matt 21:31).
29.18: They will become a horror to all the nations — leharzinah, object of shock. The covenant curse of Deut 28:25 applied: those who thought they were safe become the example of divine judgment. The NT equivalent is the parable of the older brother (Luke 15:28-30) who thinks his proximity guarantees favor.
29.19: 'Because they did not pay attention to my words, declares YHWH, which I persistently sent to them by my servants the prophets.' The persistent sending of prophets is the pattern Jesus names in the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:34-36) — servant after servant sent, each rejected. The long patience of YHWH before judgment is itself an act of grace.
29.20: 'Hear the word of YHWH, all you exiles whom I sent away from Jerusalem to Babylon.' The repeated call to hear — YHWH's persistent address to the community that has been disciplined. This is the pattern of the father running to the returning son (Luke 15:20) — YHWH does not abandon the exiles to silence but pursues them with his word.
29.21: Ahab and Zedekiah, the false prophets in Babylon, spoke lies in YHWH's name and committed adultery. The moral failure of the false prophets is not incidental — Jesus connects false teaching with moral corruption: a tree is known by its fruit (Matt 7:17). The prophetic calling requires both verbal faithfulness and moral integrity.
29.22: They will become a byword: 'YHWH make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire.' The gruesome end of false prophets becomes a proverb. The severity of judgment on false prophets in the OT establishes the gravity with which the NT treats the same: 'their condemnation is just' (Rom 3:8).
29.23: 'Because they have done an outrageous thing in Israel: they have committed adultery with their neighbors' wives and have spoken in my name lying words that I did not command them.' The double sin — sexual and prophetic unfaithfulness — is treated as a unit. Paul's list of qualifications for church leaders (1 Tim 3; Tit 1) reflects the same principle: public ministry demands integrity in every domain.
29.24: Shemaiah of Nehelam wrote letters opposing Jeremiah — a coordinated effort to undermine the true prophet from within the exile community. The opposition to Jeremiah from multiple fronts (priests, prophets, officials) typifies Christ's multi-front opposition: Pharisees, Herodians, Sadducees, and finally the Roman state (Mark 3:6; 15:1-15). Truth-tellers face institutional resistance.
29.25: Shemaiah sent letters in his own name to Jerusalem's priests and all the people, claiming authority he did not have. The self-appointed authority — speaking without divine commission — is the false apostle Paul warns about: those who 'commend themselves' (2 Cor 10:12). True authority is given, not assumed.
29.26: Shemaiah's letter commands the priest to put any prophet who acts crazily in the stocks. The attempt to silence the true prophet by institutional authority — 'put him in the stocks' — anticipates Jeremiah 20 and prefigures the institutional silencing of Christ (Matt 26:65-66: 'He is guilty of death') and the apostles (Acts 5:18). The establishment often resists the prophet.
29.27: Shemaiah accuses Jeremiah of prophesying falsely, calling him a madman. The accusation of madness against the true prophet — Jesus was also accused of being out of his mind (Mark 3:21: 'He is out of his mind') and of having a demon (John 8:48). The pattern: dismiss the prophet's authority by questioning his sanity.
29.28: Jeremiah had written to the exiles to settle in Babylon for a long time — the command to build and plant. This long-haul patience is the NT posture of those awaiting the parousia: 'Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord' (Jas 5:7). Neither despairing nor falsely optimistic, but faithfully present in the world until YHWH's appointed time.
29.29: Zephaniah reads Shemaiah's letter to Jeremiah — the priest delivers the attack to the prophet himself. The transparency of opposition is notable: YHWH ensures Jeremiah knows who opposes him and why. Jesus similarly knows the intentions of his opponents (Mark 2:8: 'immediately perceiving in his spirit') and names the hypocrisy openly.
29.30: The word of YHWH comes to Jeremiah regarding Shemaiah's opposition. The divine response to false prophetic attack is more divine speech — YHWH does not leave his prophet without a counter-word. The NT equivalent: the Spirit gives speech in moments of persecution (Matt 10:19-20). The word is not silenced by opposition.
29.31: 'Shemaiah the Nehelamite has prophesied to you, though I did not send him.' The divine verdict is unambiguous: not sent. The criterion of divine sending versus self-sending runs throughout the NT: 'How can they preach unless they are sent?' (Rom 10:15). The mission depends on authorization, and authorization comes from YHWH alone — given to the Son (John 20:21) and through the Son to his people.
29.32: Shemaiah will not see the good that YHWH will do for his people — he and his descendants cut off from the restoration. The exclusion from eschatological blessing is the NT pattern for those who oppose the gospel (Matt 8:12; Rev 22:15). The mercy offered in YHWH's plans (v11) is not coercive — it can be forfeited by persistent opposition to the divine word.

30.1: The opening of the Book of Consolation (chs 30-33) — YHWH commands Jeremiah to write down these words. The writing command is significant: this is meant to be preserved for the coming generation who will witness the fulfillment. The preserved written word anticipates scripture's function in the NT: 'these things were written for our instruction' (1 Cor 10:11; Rom 15:4).
30.2: 'Write in a book all the words I have spoken to you.' The codification of prophetic speech into a text that will outlast the moment — the principle behind all scripture. Jesus's words are preserved and fulfilled as scripture (John 19:24,36; 20:31). The word written for the future is the basis of faith grounded in testimony rather than direct experience.
30.3: 'For behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will restore the fortunes of my people Israel and Judah.' The yemim baim (days are coming) formula — the prophetic horizon of eschatological fulfillment. Jesus opens his ministry with 'the time is fulfilled' (Mark 1:15) — he announces that the days Jeremiah was waiting for have arrived. The restoration of Israel and Judah points ultimately to the new creation in which all things are made new (Rev 21:5).
30.4: These are the words YHWH spoke concerning Israel and Judah — both kingdoms addressed, which means the restoration is not merely the end of the Babylonian exile but the full gathering of the divided people. The NT presents Christ as the one who breaks down the wall between peoples (Eph 2:14) and gathers the scattered children of God into one (John 11:52).
30.5: 'We have heard a cry of panic, of terror, and no peace.' The sound of terror preceding the deliverance. The tribulation pattern — anguish before rescue — is the structure of the gospel: 'the hour is coming, and is now here, when you will be scattered... and will leave me alone' (John 16:32), but also 'I have overcome the world' (John 16:33). Tribulation is the prelude to peace.
30.6: Men in labor-like anguish — the childbirth imagery for eschatological distress (cf. 4:31). Paul uses the same image for creation's travail awaiting the resurrection (Rom 8:22: 'the whole creation has been groaning in the pangs of childbirth until now'). The pain is not meaningless suffering but the labor of new creation.
30.7: 'Alas! That day is great; there is none like it; it is a time of distress for Jacob.' The yom gadol (great day) that is also a time of Jacob's trouble — unprecedented tribulation. Jesus quotes this tradition in Matthew 24:21 ('there will be great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world until now'). But the verse continues: 'yet he shall be saved out of it' — the tribulation is not the end but the passage to salvation.
30.8: 'I will break his yoke from off your neck.' The promise of freedom from the yoke of oppression — the reversal of the iron yoke threatened in ch28. Christ comes to proclaim liberty to captives (Luke 4:18; Isa 61:1), to free those enslaved under the law (Gal 4:3-5), to break every yoke (Isa 58:6). The broken yoke is the signature act of eschatological redemption.
30.9: 'They shall serve YHWH their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them.' A direct Davidic prophecy: YHWH will raise up (aqim) David as king — the resurrection vocabulary used for the Davidic covenant fulfillment. In the NT this is explicitly applied to Jesus: 'God raised up Jesus... David, having died, was buried... but he whom God raised up did not see corruption' (Acts 13:34-37). The raised Davidic king is Christ.
30.10: 'Fear not, O Jacob my servant... for I will save you from far away.' The oracle of salvation (al-tira) — the same formula spoken to Abraham (Gen 15:1) and repeated throughout Isaiah. Christ embodies this word — 'Do not be afraid' is his most common command (Matt 14:27; John 14:1). Salvation from a far country is the pattern of the incarnation: the Son comes from heaven to the far country where the lost sheep is.
30.11: 'For I am with you to save you, declares YHWH.' The Emmanuel promise — YHWH-with-us — which Matthew explicitly identifies as fulfilled in Jesus (Matt 1:23: 'God with us'). The presence of God as the means of salvation is the Christological core: not a distant deity sending instructions but a God who enters the situation personally. 'I will be with you' culminates in 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14).
30.12: 'Your hurt is incurable, your wound is grievous.' The honest diagnosis of the depth of sin's damage — not a minor adjustment needed but an incurable wound. This is the anthropological premise of the incarnation: only YHWH can heal what human remedies cannot. Jesus comes as the divine physician (Mark 2:17: 'Those who are well have no need of a physician') precisely for those whose condition is medically hopeless.
30.13: 'There is none to uphold your cause, no medicine for your wound.' The forensic and medical metaphors converge: no advocate, no cure. Christ answers both: as advocate (1 John 2:1: 'we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous') and as healer (Matt 8:17 quotes Isa 53:4 for Jesus's healing ministry). The double hopelessness of the incurable wound is met by Christ's double ministry.
30.14: 'All your lovers have forgotten you; they do not care for you.' The lovers (political allies) who abandoned Israel — a recurrent OT metaphor for covenant unfaithfulness whose reversal is the central theme of redemption. Christ comes for those abandoned by every human help: 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Heb 13:5) stands against every human abandonment. The faithless lovers are replaced by the faithful Bridegroom (Rev 19:7).
30.15: 'Why do you cry out over your wound? Your pain is incurable.' YHWH acknowledges the pain while also naming its cause: 'Because your guilt is great, your sins are flagrant.' The incurable wound is not arbitrary suffering but the consequence of sin. Christ does not minimize this: the cross is the place where the full weight of the wound is carried — not dismissed but borne (Isa 53:5: 'by his wounds we are healed').
30.16: 'Therefore all who devour you shall be devoured, and all your foes shall go into captivity.' The reversal of Israel's fate is simultaneously a judgment on Israel's oppressors. This retributive logic runs through Revelation: Babylon that devoured will be devoured (Rev 18:6). Christ's victory is not merely positive restoration but the definitive defeat of the powers that oppressed.
30.17: 'For I will restore health to you, and your wounds I will heal, declares YHWH.' The healing of the incurable wound — the theme that runs through the entire Book of Consolation. Jesus's healing ministry throughout the Gospels is the embodied enactment of this promise: 'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases' (Matt 8:17 quoting Isa 53:4). Every physical healing is a sign of the deeper spiritual healing Christ brings to the incurable wound of sin.
30.18: 'I will restore the fortunes of Jacob's tents and have compassion on his dwellings.' Restoration of the physical dwelling-place — the place of community, family life, and security. The New Jerusalem (Rev 21:3) is the ultimate fulfillment: 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them.' The tent/tabernacle/dwelling imagery runs from Eden to Sinai to the temple to Christ (John 1:14: eskēnōsen, tabernacled) to the New Jerusalem.
30.19: 'Out of them shall come songs of thanksgiving, and the voices of those who celebrate.' The community of restored worship — voices of praise replacing voices of lament. The Psalter and the Revelation are the two great collections of this worship. Christ is the leader of the congregation's praise (Heb 2:12 quoting Ps 22:22: 'In the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise'). The multiplied, rejoicing community is the telos of restoration.
30.20: 'Their children shall be as they were of old.' The generational continuity of the covenant community — the promise that the restoration is not just for this generation but for the children. The NT promises the same: 'the promise is for you and for your children' (Acts 2:39). The covenant community is not merely personal but familial and generational, passing the inheritance forward.
30.21: 'Their ruler shall be one of themselves, and their governor shall come from their midst; I will make him draw near, and he shall approach me.' The native ruler who approaches YHWH — the priestly-kingly figure who has access to the divine presence. Hebrews presents Christ precisely in these terms: the high priest who is one of us (Heb 2:17: 'he had to be made like his brothers in every respect'), who draws near to God on behalf of the people (Heb 10:19-22). The drawing-near of the ruler to God is fulfilled in Christ's priestly intercession.
30.22: 'And you shall be my people, and I will be your God.' The covenant formula — the most concise statement of the covenant relationship in the entire OT. It appears in Exodus, Leviticus, Ezekiel, Jeremiah repeatedly, and is fulfilled in the NT: 'I will make my dwelling among them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people' (2 Cor 6:16; Rev 21:3). Christ mediates this ultimate covenant relationship — he is the Immanuel, God-with-us, in whom the formula becomes personal presence.
30.23: The storm of YHWH's wrath — sar'ah YHWH, tempest of YHWH — will burst on the heads of the wicked. The divine judgment that precedes the restoration is YHWH's active wrath, not passive permission. In the NT, this wrath is poured out on Christ at Calvary for those who believe, and reserved for the final day for those who do not (Rom 5:9: 'saved by him from the wrath of God'; Rev 19:15: the winepress of the wrath of God).
30.24: 'In the latter days you will understand this.' The eschatological horizon placed at the end of the storm-oracle: full understanding comes at the end of the process. Paul echoes this: 'Now I know in part; then I shall know fully' (1 Cor 13:12). Christ's coming initiates the 'latter days' (Heb 1:2: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son'), and his return completes them. The clarity that comes at the end is the full revelation of what was always true.

25.1: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605/604 BC) — the year Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish and became master of the Near East. It is also the year of Daniel's first deportation (Dan 1:1). The convergence of dates links Jeremiah's great cup-of-wrath oracle with the beginning of Daniel's prophetic career in Babylon — the two prophets whose work together forms the chronological skeleton of the exile. Christ as the fulfiller of both prophecy streams enters history precisely at the end of their seventy-year and seventy-weeks countdowns (Dan 9:24-27; Luke 3:23).
25.2: Jeremiah as the prophet who speaks to all Judah — the comprehensive audience of the oracle. Jesus likewise addresses all Jerusalem in his final temple discourses (Matt 23-25), culminating in the same predictions of catastrophe. The prophet who must speak to the whole people even when they do not listen is the prototype of Christ, the final prophet (Deut 18:15-18; Heb 1:1-2), whose words go out to all people even when many reject them.
25.3: "Twenty-three years the word of the LORD has come to me and I have spoken." The long faithfulness of the prophet — speaking without visible fruit for two decades. Hebrews 11:36-38 describes those who suffered through faithfulness, "of whom the world was not worthy." Christ's own ministry of three and a half years, in which the majority rejected him, inherits and concentrates this pattern of faithful speaking to the unhearing. His ministry was not measured by acceptance but by obedience to the one who sent him (John 12:49).
25.4: The persistent-sending formula: "the LORD has persistently sent you all his servants the prophets." Hashkem v'shaleach — rising early and sending — is Jeremiah's signature phrase for YHWH's persistent patience. Hebrews 1:1-2 is the direct theological commentary on this verse: "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." Christ is the Son sent as the final, definitive word after all the prophetic sendings have been rejected.
25.5: "Turn back from your evil way and live in the land." The covenant call to repentance with the promise of remaining in the land. Jesus's first proclamation (Mark 1:15 — "repent and believe the good news") is structurally identical to this: the call to turn back, now with the kingdom of God as what the turning-back enables inhabiting. Where the land was the covenant blessing in Jeremiah, in Christ the "land" expands to encompass the new creation that repentance opens onto (Rom 4:13 — Abraham and his offspring inherit the world).
25.6: "Do not follow other gods... and do not provoke me to anger." The prohibition against idolatry as the center of the covenant. Paul's treatment of idolatry in Romans 1:18-25 and 1 Corinthians 10:14-22 is the NT explication of this warning. Christ enters this framework as the one who perfectly did not follow other gods — the temptation narratives (Matt 4:8-10 — "worship the Lord your God and serve him only") show him refusing the idolatrous shortcut and maintaining exclusive covenant loyalty throughout.
25.7: "You have not listened to me and have provoked me to anger with what your hands have made." The verdict of covenant failure — the idols of human manufacture versus the God who made human hands. Acts 17:24-25 (Paul at Athens): "the God who made the world and everything in it... is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything." Christ is YHWH in human hands making not idols but the healing and reconciliation that human hands could never achieve on their own (John 1:14 — the Word made flesh).
25.8: "Because you have not obeyed my words." The therefore-clause that introduces the judgment. Romans 5:19 — "through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners." But the second half of the verse is the gospel: "through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The judgment that falls because of disobedience (Jer 25:8) is met by the obedience of Christ that creates a new righteousness for all who are in him. The "because you have not obeyed" is answered by his "because he obeyed."
25.9: "I am going to summon all the peoples of the north and Nebuchadnezzar my servant." The designation of a pagan king as YHWH's servant is theologically striking — it asserts divine sovereignty over all nations, not just Israel. Romans 13:1 ("there is no authority except that which God has established") and Revelation 17:17 ("God has put it into their hearts to accomplish his purpose") extend this principle. Christ is the ultimate servant (ebed / doulos) in whom this pattern culminates — not a pagan instrument but the willing, fully obedient Son who gives himself (Phil 2:7-8).
25.10: "I will silence among them the sounds of joy and gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the bride, the grinding of millstones." The silencing of ordinary life — joy, marriage, daily work. Revelation 18:22-23 quotes this verse almost verbatim in the lament over Babylon's fall: "the sound of harpists and musicians... will never be heard in you again... the voice of the bridegroom and bride will never be heard in you again." What Jeremiah pronounces against Jerusalem, Revelation declares against Babylon — but both are reversed by Christ's own wedding feast (Rev 19:7-9) where the bridegroom's voice is heard forever.
25.11: "These nations will serve the king of Babylon for seventy years." The seventy-year exile-clock that Daniel reads as he seeks YHWH's face (Dan 9:2). Daniel's prayer (Dan 9:3-19) generates the angel's response: seventy weeks are decreed for your people — the prophecy of the 490 years that culminates in the anointed one being cut off (Dan 9:26). Christ arrives at the exact terminus of Daniel's recalculation of Jeremiah's seventy years. Jeremiah 25:11 sets the clock; Christ is its appointed hour.
25.12: "When seventy years are over, I will punish the king of Babylon." The accountability of the instrument of judgment — even YHWH's tools are judged for how they carry out their task. Revelation 18 is the extended fulfillment of the judgment on Babylon that Jeremiah 25:12 and chapters 50-51 announce. Christ presides over this final judgment: Revelation 19:11-16 — the rider on the white horse whose name is "the Word of God" executes the judgment that Jeremiah prophesied and Revelation completes.
25.13: "All that is recorded in this book, which Jeremiah prophesied against all the nations." The written prophecy as the permanent record against which history is measured. 2 Peter 3:7 ("the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire, kept for the day of judgment") and Revelation 20:12 ("the books were opened") place Christ at the center of the final accounting against which all written prophecy finds its vindication. The scroll of Jeremiah 25:13 becomes the opened books of the final assize that Christ presides over.
25.14: "Many nations and great kings will enslave them in turn, and I will repay them." The reciprocity of divine justice — what Babylon did to others will be done to Babylon. Galatians 6:7 — "God cannot be mocked. A man reaps what he sows." Christ himself teaches this in the parable of the unmerciful servant (Matt 18:21-35) — the one who was forgiven much and forgave nothing receives the same treatment they meted out. Divine repayment is not vindictiveness but the moral coherence of the universe Christ died to restore.
25.15: "Take this cup of the wine of wrath from my hand, and make all the nations to whom I send you drink it." The cup of divine wrath — the most direct OT antecedent to Gethsemane. Matthew 26:39 — "My Father, if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me." Mark 14:36 — "Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will." The cup that Jeremiah is commanded to carry to all nations is the cup Christ takes into himself. Isaiah 51:17-23 promises that the cup will pass from Zion; it passes to Christ, who drinks it to the dregs (John 18:11 — "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?").
25.16: "They will drink and reel and go out of their minds because of the sword I am sending among them." The effect of drinking the cup — disorientation and destruction. Christ drank the cup and in doing so absorbed its disorienting power: the cry of desolation (Matt 27:46 — "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") is the moment of maximum cup-drinking, the nadir of wrath absorbed. He experienced the reeling so that those who drink the new-covenant cup of his blood (1 Cor 11:25-26) receive not wrath but the Lord's death proclaimed until he comes.
25.17: "So I took the cup from the LORD's hand and made all the nations drink it." Jeremiah's obedience — the prophet who carries and presents the cup of judgment. Christ carries the cup one step further: not just to the nations but into himself. He is both the prophet who presents the cup (as Jeremiah) and the one who drinks it himself (as the Lamb). John 1:29 — "the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world" — the sin-bearing is the cup-drinking, now applied to all nations.
25.18: Jerusalem and the cities of Judah as the first to receive the cup. "It is time for judgment to begin with God's household" (1 Pet 4:17) — the same principle that YHWH begins with his own people. The cross occurred in Jerusalem — judgment began where the covenant was centered. Christ died in the city that first received the cup in Jeremiah's vision, bearing the judgment that the city had accumulated through centuries of covenant violation.
25.19: Pharaoh king of Egypt named among the cup-recipients. The exodus from Egypt that defined Israel's identity is now inverted: Egypt's king must drink alongside Judah's king. The great power that once held God's people captive now faces the same divine judgment as those it once enslaved. Christ's redemption creates a new exodus (Luke 9:31 — his "departure" / exodos is discussed at the transfiguration) that transcends the political boundaries of Jeremiah's cup-list.
25.20: The foreign peoples, the land of Uz (Job's homeland), Philistia — the cup extends to the nations at Israel's borders. Romans 3:9 — "both Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin." The cup-list of Jer 25:19-26 is the OT prototype of Paul's universal indictment: all are under judgment and therefore all are eligible for the universal salvation Christ provides. The comprehensiveness of the cup-list matches the comprehensiveness of the gospel invitation.
25.21: Edom, Moab, and the Ammonites — the eastern neighbors, descendants of Esau and Lot. Numbers typically associated with Israel's historical enemies and occasional allies. The cup crosses kinship lines: these nations share Abraham's lineage in various ways (Edom from Esau; Moab and Ammon from Lot) but must still drink. The universal judgment that disregards ethnic connection anticipates the universal gospel that equally disregards it: Galatians 3:28 — "neither Jew nor Gentile... for you are all one in Christ Jesus."
25.22: Tyre and Sidon — the Phoenician trading cities whose prosperity was legendary. Jesus uses Tyre and Sidon as comparators in his judgment-speech: Matthew 11:21-22 — "if the miracles performed in you had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago." The cities that were spared fuller judgment in Jeremiah's vision become in Jesus's discourse the benchmark against which Capernaum's greater refusal is measured. Christ uses the cup-list nations to calibrate the severity of rejecting him.
25.23: Dedan, Tema, Buz — the Arabian trade-route peoples. The cup extends into the heart of the Arabian desert. Isaiah 21:13-17 also addresses the Arabian peoples in oracles of judgment. The geographic completeness of the cup-list (from Egypt to Media to Arabia) reflects the universal scope of divine sovereignty. Revelation 14:6-7 — "an angel flying in midair, with the eternal gospel to proclaim to those who live on the earth — to every nation, tribe, language, and people" — the proclamation to all has the same geographic comprehensiveness as Jeremiah's cup-list.
25.24: The kings of Arabia and "the various peoples who live in the desert." No human sovereignty escapes the cup — even the nomadic desert peoples who seem beyond the reach of settled kingdoms are included. Revelation 7:9 — "a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people, and language" — the peoples drawn from the desert and the margins in Jeremiah's cup-judgment appear in Revelation's vision as the redeemed from every nation standing before the throne.
25.25: Elam and Media — the eastern empires that would later overthrow Babylon. They too drink the cup they will later administer to others. The irony is theological: even the destroyers of Babylon are themselves under divine judgment. No instrument of YHWH's justice escapes the scrutiny that it applies to others. Romans 2:1 — "you who pass judgment on someone else are condemning yourself, because you who pass judgment do the same things." Christ alone judges without being himself under judgment (John 8:46 — "Can any of you prove me guilty of sin?").
25.26: "All the kingdoms on the face of the earth — and last of all, the king of Sheshak will drink it." Sheshak is the atbash cipher for Babylon (š-š-k = b-b-l reversed) — the cryptographic naming of the empire that controls the world. Revelation 17-18 takes up this Babylon-mystery with full elaboration: "Babylon the Great, the mother of prostitutes... the great city that rules over the kings of the earth." Christ's judgment of Babylon (Rev 19:2 — "he has condemned the great prostitute") is the fulfillment of the Sheshak cup-judgment Jeremiah encodes.
25.27: "Drink, get drunk and vomit; fall down and never rise again." The comprehensive devastation of those who drink the cup of wrath to the end. At Gethsemane Christ prays "let this cup pass from me" (Matt 26:39) — this is the cup he is asking about, the cup that causes falling-and-not-rising. He does not refuse it. He drinks it fully on the cross. Isaiah 51:22-23 promises that "never again will you drink it" — the cup that made Zion fall has passed to another. The one who drank it rose: the resurrection is the reversal of the cup's falling-and-not-rising.
25.28: "If they refuse to take the cup from your hand to drink, say to them: You must certainly drink." The divine insistence on universal accountability — there is no opt-out from divine judgment. 2 Corinthians 5:10 — "we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each of us may receive what is due us for the things done while in the body." Those who refuse the cup-of-judgment from Jeremiah's hand have not escaped it — they face the same Christ at the judgment seat that Jeremiah's oracle announces for all nations.
25.29: "I am already beginning to bring disaster on the city that bears my own name — do you think you will escape judgment? You will not go unpunished." The logic of Jeremiah's universalism: if YHWH judges even the city that carries his name, no one escapes on the grounds of proximity to him. Romans 2:9-11 — "there will be trouble and distress for every human being who does evil — first for the Jew, then for the Gentile... for God does not show favoritism." Christ does not grant exemption to the covenant insiders; his judgment at the temple ("not one stone will be left on another") is the enacted form of Jer 25:29.
25.30: "The LORD will roar from on high; from his holy dwelling he will thunder." The divine roar from Zion — echoed in Joel 3:16 and Amos 1:2. Revelation 5:5 — "do not weep! See, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed." Christ is simultaneously the Lion who roars in judgment (Jer 25:30; Amos 1:2) and the Lamb who was slain (Rev 5:6). The two images exist in perfect tension in Revelation's throne-room vision: the roaring-lion power of Jer 25:30 is expressed through the slaughtered lamb's sacrifice. The shout of judgment is also the shout of the winepress-treader (Rev 19:15).
25.31: "He is entering into judgment with all flesh; the wicked he will put to the sword." The global judgment: kol-bāśār — all flesh. John 5:22-27 — "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son... for he is the Son of Man." The judgment of all flesh that YHWH announces in Jer 25:31 is given to Christ. Acts 17:31 — "he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed." The "all flesh" scope of Jeremiah's oracle becomes the "all people" scope of the final judgment with Christ as judge.
25.32: "Disaster is spreading from nation to nation, and a great storm is rising from the far ends of the earth." The global reach of divine judgment — no geography is exempt. Matthew 24:14 — "this gospel of the kingdom will be preached in the whole world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come." The storm-from-the-ends-of-the-earth that Jeremiah announces as judgment and the gospel-to-the-ends-of-the-earth that Jesus announces as salvation share the same geographical comprehensiveness. Both are unstoppable and universal; the difference is what they carry.
25.33: "Those killed by the LORD on that day will stretch from one end of the earth to the other. No one will mourn them, no one will gather them, no one will bury them." The eschatological slaughter — the scale defying normal grief-practices. Revelation 19:17-21 depicts the same scene as the battle of Armageddon: "an angel standing in the sun who cried in a loud voice to all the birds flying in midair, 'Come, gather together for the great supper of God.'" Christ is the presider over this final day, not its victim. He who was left unburied (no permanent tomb — he rose) presides over the final accounting of the unburied dead.
25.34: "Wail, you shepherds, and cry out! Roll in the dust, you leaders of the flock! For the time of your slaughter has arrived." The shepherds who destroyed rather than tended must now face the flock's fate themselves. Ezekiel 34 is the extended meditation on this — the bad shepherds who scatter the flock. Christ's answer is John 10:11 — "I am the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep." He does the opposite of the wailing shepherds: they flee from their own slaughter; he stays and accepts his.
